THE AUTHOR

This book was written by Maximiliaan Winkelhuis.
The presented material is based on his vast experience in
guiding competitive ballroom dancers on a mental level,
his extensive research, and his education and work in the
field of business communications, psychology and Voice
Dialogue.

After his studies in communication strategy, Maximilian

worked as a director for leading communication agencies
in the Netherlands. He now teaches dance strategies and tactics around the world. He helps dancers to find their
personal contribution to the dances, to increase the enjoyment of dance and to reduce stress and effective activ-
ities. Maximilian also created the dance competition simulation CD ’The Final Connection’ published by

DanceLife.

This book would never have seen the light without the great support of two of his closest friends: Ruud Vermeij
and Peter Townsend with whom he established Team Amsterdam.

Both Peter and Ruud have constantly challenged the methods of Maximiliaan during the workshops they have
given and in discussions on mutual clients. They both inspired the author to get the maximum out of himself

and are therefore acknowledged here.

Peter Townsend has dedicated practically his whole life to dancing. He danced in the competitive arena for many
years, from Juveniles through to Professionals. His training was enriched by intensive studies in ballet (classical
and jazz) Alexander technique and body mind centring.

He is specialized in ‘partnering skills’. In his work he helps the dancer to develop a natural sensing of their body,
the partner’s body, and their interrelationship. He stimulates dancers to have conversations within and with each
other. He holds the view that movement sparks the spirit and that dance can be a tool towards a more integrat-

ed path to wholeness.

Ruud Vermeij finished his master’s degree at the Laban Centre in London where he specialised in choreology.
His book ‘Latin. Thinking, sensing and doing Latin American dancing’ was successfully published in the early
nineties. [In 2000 Ruud was awarded a doctorate for his research on creating a dance profile for dancers. Major
issues in his thesis were: the style of the dance itself, the style of the teacher and/or choreographer and the per-
sonal style of the dancers.

He continues to apply the universal principles of movements to his teaching in both Standard and Latin

American dancing.

INTRODUCTION

When Ruud Vermeij invited me to lecture on dance strategies at the Dutch National Dancesport Academy in the
early nineties I never expected to work out my thoughts so thoroughly. My first lectures were mere thoughts of
what could be possible. Fortunately my models proved to be helpful for the Dutch couples. Quite soon the mod-
els became appreciated around the dancing world.

My first clients liked the way I approached dancing: to get the best out of them by making them more aware of
their abilities and strengths. I helped them discover that dancing is much more than a technicaily perfect per-
formance. I showed them how to keep in contact with themselves, their partner and the dances they performed.
They understood and felt the power of their own thinking. This experience improved the quality of their per-
formances and gave them greater enjoyment. In return they taught me that this was my professional path for the
future. Gradually my practice grew until it was impossible to combine mental training with my career in adver-
tising. I now feel very grateful that these dancers helped me to get the best out of myself. And they certainly
helped to increase my personal enjoyment as well.

The power of my thinking was greatly stimulated by all past and current clients, by the intensive contacts with
teachers and coaches and by my study in transformational psychology. The results of this process can be found
in this workbook for competitive dancers. | truly hope it will open up new resources for all readers. Dancers,

teachers and coaches alike.

DANCERS, TEACHERS, COACHES

Writing a book for the ballroom dance world is quite an adventure. At first I targeted my efforts towards the
dancers. They are the ones who need to do the real work in the end anyway. Soon | found out that effectively
working with dancers meant working with their teachers too. The more the people in the team worked together,
the stronger the effort became.

In later years [ encountered more and more people who also wanted to coach dancers professionally. Books on
sports psychology helped some but others found them too abstract. The transformation of general information
into our ballroom dance world was often a drawback. The same applied to books on psychology for ballet
dancers. Therefore many of such sources have been revised for your covenience.

To make the reading of this book active and easy | have chosen to address the dancers only. Needless to say that

the content of this book stili contains information to help teachers and coaches in their daily practice.

THE ROAD TO SUCCESS IS A UNIQUE ROAD

You ali have a great dancer inside you. That dancer rarely comes to the floor, This book presents tools to help
that dancer emerge.

Your success lies mostly in the pleasure you are able to find, In this case: pleasure through dancing. Each of you
has a different state of mind and different talents and needs. Therefore each of you has different options. There
is not one way. Find your unique road to fulfilment. Then your personal success will come naturally. With this

book I hope I have provided a road map for success.

HOW TO USE THIS BOOK

This book consists of three main parts: each of them giving you information, examples and exercises for a spe-

cific planning period in your career.

The first part introduces a model for reducing stress. You will find techniques for becoming more effective dur-

ing a competition day. The following part lists the tools needed for effectively planning a season and explains how
to use them. The third part is about career goal setting in relation to finding your personal contribution to your

dances.

THE NATURAL ORDER OF READING THE BOOK

It may seem natural to start with Part One: starting with improving the way you handle competition stress, followed
by the seasonal planning and closing - maybe a year later - with taking care of your long term career issues.

On the other hand, the natural order of planning can also be the reverse: first long term career planning (Part
Three), then work on the seasonal planning (Part Two) and finally work on the issues for the competition day
{Part One}. This would be a textbook order. The most logical order perhaps. But not the only one! Yes, you may
find that having completed the third part, it will be easier to make better decisions during the season. And this
will finally result in being more effective on the competition day itself. But there are other ways too.

You could also decide to first work on seasonal issues, then on competition day issues and close with long-term
career issues.

All planning orders are fine. Life isn't always very linear. Just start with that part or chapter which has the greatest

attraction for you. I have seen good results, no matter what order dancers have worked through this material.

AD HOC PROBLEM SOLVING
Over three hundred pages of information may scare you off starting with any part of this book. In that case you
can also opt to just read the chapter you currently need most. The chapter titles will help you to find the infor-

mation you are looking for.

Part One: The Competition Day
i)

WHAT COUNTS DURING COMPETITIONS

This part of the book offers you the tools you need to dance to your maximum on the days it really counts: the
crucial competition days. If you feel you want get rid of your competition stress, then do read on!

Many factors determine the success of the day. Of course all the weeks of hard practice, taking jessons, creating
your outfits have a great impact on the outcome of your performance. In Part Two you find information to

improve this preparation period. Here we take a closer look at factors that need to be handled just before, dur-

ing and right after a competition. These factors are depicted in the following chart.

Fico.)

FACTORS DETERMINING THE SUCCESS OF THE DAY
No research has been done to investigate the success factors during a competition in the dance world. Yet in

other fields of communication’ a lot of research has been done and could give some insights into crucial factors

for competitive ballroom dancing.
In the academic world, scientists - like psychologists and sociologists - wanted to know what determined the suc-

cess of communications. Also leaders in the business world needed to understand what made their executives

successful in their business presentations. Their public relations agencies and advertising agencies helped them

out by doing research. Even popular self-help or self-consciousness programmes like NLP*, Landmark and Essence

researched ways to help people to understand how they could communicate more effectively. All this research

came up with approximately the same results, Shocking results that have changed the behaviour of many lead-

ers in the business world over the last decade. Maybe there is something we could learn from these findings too.

Most of the research mentioned presented answers to the following questions:

Port One: The Competition Day

1. How long can people listen non-stop,

2.''How much cana person remember from a presentation

3. What percentage d
"le CONTENT

TOTAL

Before you read on, you might take a minute to answer these questions yourself. The reality might have a greater

impact on you when you first make yourself aware of your opinion. What do you think the answers are?

HAS THE WORLD GONE MAD?

Researchers found shocking results. People can only concentrate - non-stop - for a mere twenty-three seconds
on average. After that short period, people lose their concentration. Sometimes for a spiit second. Sometimes
their thoughts drift away for a longer period.

The second shocking finding was that people only remember seven per cent of the content of a presentation.
Mostly consisting of the beginning, some highlights and the ending. The rest of the words and materials shown

were totally forgotten!

CONSEQUENCES OF LIMITED CONCENTRATION AND MEMORY

Adjudicators see much less than you might expect. And they remember even less. So as a competitor you may
feel freer to make some mistakes. The chance that your mistakes are noted and remembered is quite small. In
fact, many adjudicators admit they only have three to five seconds per couple to make up their mind...

This information might be worth thinking about. Many of you suffer from a strongly perfectionist and critical
mind. Competitive dancing seems to attract such people. These personality-traits can be beneficial to you in your
preparation weeks. They make you work hard to improve your skills. However, during competition they can take
away your freedom to dance to the fullest. On these days this information is quite often incorrect and restricts

you dancing to your maximum.

CONTRIBUTIONS TO THE SUCCESS OF A PRESENTATION

Most people expect that what you say and what you present mostly determines the success of communication.
Research shows however that this content’ is only responsible for seven per cent of your success. In other
words: it is not what you say but how you say or present it that really counts. Consequently ’tone of voice’ and
"body language’ proved to be much more decisive in relation to success, Tone of voice accounts for thirty-eight

per cent of success and body language is responsible for the remaining fifty-five per cent of success.

What counts during competitions

ASSUMPTIONS FOR COMPETITIVE DANCING

There is no reason to believe that the results found for presentations in the academic and business world would

differ greatly for competitive dancing. Maybe some minor deviations would be found. As long as there is no such

research some assumptions could be made to help you understand what really counts in your performance on a

crucial competition day. Here follows a possible interpretation of the three communication components:

1. The content of your dancing (7%) could be interpreted as the universally accepted standardised technique
and the choreography.

2. The tone of dancing (38%) could be seen as your personality: who is performing the technique and chore-
ography and what is your personal contribution.

3. The body language of your dancing (55%} could be seen as your physical body, your floor craft, the space

you take on the floor, your body toning, your musicality, your facial expression and of course your wardrobe

and make-up.

The effect-distribution OF your performance

CONSEQUENCES FOR COMPETITIVE DANCING

The content of your dancing only accounts for seven per cent of your success. The consequence of this assump-
tion is that you can forget the technique and choreography on the competition day. First of all because you can’t
change it on that day anyway. Secondly because it accounts for such a small part of the success of that day.
Thirdly: realize that most of your direct competitors have about the same level of technique and choreography...
Most of your technical and choreographical mistakes/omissions can be compensated for by the other - more

influential - aspects: tone and body language.

CONTENT ACCOUNTS FOR 7% OF THE SUCCESS OF THE DAY

This assumption does not imply that you can forget to keep on working on the seven per cent for your content
In order to improve your level of dancing you do need to work hard on technique and choreography matters in
the preparation period. Also your sense of having improved technical skills and choreography helps your perfec-
tionist and critical mind to relax. This sense creates freedom for your body language and tone of dancing during
the crucial performance. So do keep on working on your dance skills and choreography in the preparation peri-

ods. Only forget about them when you perform. At that moment they are just less important!

Port One: The Competition Day

TONE ACCOUNTS FOR 38% OF THE SUCCESS

The tone of your dancing might be seen as your personal interpretation and which part of your personality is per-
forming. The first element - your personal interpretation - is helped by finding your personal contribution to the
dances you perform, Part Three of this book is dedicated to finding your personal dance concept.

The other element in tone is the part of your personality that is active during your performance. Do you pres-
ent your material from a proud part of your personality or from an anxious or even shy part? From a sensual
part, a cool part or an arrogant part? We all have such different parts (also called sub-personalities or selves).
Some rule our lives and are our primary selves while others are more or less hidden: the disowned selves, The
self who presents your dancing contributes enormously to the appreciation of your performance.

You can actually stimulate the preferred selves to come forward and so become more convincing. Convincing
expression begins with honest feelings and translating them into movements. A good balance between your
body, mind, emotions and spirit® increases your being in the now and feeling well. Over-focus on any of these
human levels tends to make you Jess complete and less convincing. Concentration on issues you can control
{your mind, your body, what you give to your partner) are some general basics for that and are explained in fur-
ther chapters.

Note in this context that genuine enjoyment is strong in convincing people. Some of you (and a lot of coaches)

create such a serious atmosphere that it is hard to tune into your enjoying selves...

SUGGESTIONS

Ask your coach to only tell you what works well during competitions. This makes you stronger than knowing what goes wrong
or is missing. (In Chapter 2-2 the different roles of the teacher and the coach are examined more closely.)

in Chapter 2-8 you find exercises to free you from a too serious approach (like breathing, relaxation techniques, creative visu-
alization and marking your choreography}.

In 2-8-9 you also find a method called Voice Dialogue’ to get more access to your different selves*

BODY LANGUAGE ACCOUNTS FOR 55% OF THE SUCCESS

Body language is the visual result of the use of your body, your musicality, the movement, your expression and
your look. To help most of these aspects on the competition day, it is necessary to have your body and mind in
optimal condition. It is crucial that you feel good about yourself. This begins with appropriate preparations in
the weeks before the competition, good food (food in most venues is not appropriate for sports people), warm-
ups and cool downs at the right time.

Your musicality can be stimulated in the preparation weeks. You could create a habit of finding your inner body
thythm before the music starts. This makes you less dependent on the music of the competition. The inner body
rhythm is something you can create yourself on demand and thus control it. The music during competitions is
a given thing that you cannot control and therefore can create stress.

Another preparation you could do is listen to tapes or CDs of the orchestra of the event. This might help you to
get in tune with the real music long before the competition starts. This could relax your mind too.

A very tangible part of body language - the look - could be prepared long before the competition day. You could

What counts during competitions

have the wardrobe ready weeks before the day you need it (instead of having it ready just before - or even on -
the competition day). This would relax your worrying mind tremendously. Your wardrobe would then only need

@ quick check’.

COMPETITIVE DANCING STRESS

Hopefully the information in this chapter has already taken away some stress: you now know you can worry less
about your content on a competition day. You can worry less about what judges actually see and remember.

Of course this mere mental information does not take away all of your stress. Stress is too complicated to solve
with understanding only. Practical exercises are needed to help you out. In the next chapter you can read more
about the stress that takes away your qualities in content, tone and body language. Chapters 1-3 to 1-7 offer you

useful information to solve most of your stress.

Dancing is non-verbal communication

Part One: The Competition Day

WHAT IS STRESS ACTUALLY?
People talk a lot about the influence of stress in sports. But what is stress actually? The Merriam-Webster’s dic-

tionary describes stress as follows':

tend: io alter of sistent eu 4
-. Joseet Shoben>:

From the above, my understanding of stress is seen as ‘a physical, chemical, or emotional factor that causes bod-
ily or mental tension and may be a factor in disease causation’ and ‘a state resulting from stress; especially:
one of bodily or mental tension resulting from factors that tend to alter an existent equilibrium’.

Stress may cause disease but it also may not. The fact remains that stress tends to alter the existent equilibrium.
In other words: you may not feel the stress but it does alter your state of being. The reason why you may not
feel the stress is because you could be used to the feeling that comes with it. Without that stress feeling you
might even feel something is missing.

Stress is often confused with the positive ‘arousal’. You can separate the two: ‘stress’ basically comes with situ-

ations you cannot control and ‘arousal’ comes with exciting situations you do control.

WHO IS YOUR BOSS?

€verybody has a guardian angel so to speak. In fact, this angel is your best boss. It leaves life up to you most of
the time. It will only interfere when it detects threat. This angel is called the Autonomous Nervous System
{A.N.S.), a part of your brain that overrules you when it feels you are facing something dangerous. As the word
‘autonomous’ indicates: it works independently. At a first glance the power of the A.N.S. is so strong that you
cannot prevent it from interfering.

Most of the time the A.N.S. works pretty well for us. We humans are faced with many threats around us and
without this guardian angel the human species would not have survived during the evolution of life on earth.
For instance: if you put your hand on a hot stove you automatically take away that hand - even before you feel

and mentally realize that the heat will burn your skin. You will not first feel the pain, then investigate how this

Part One: The Competition Doy

pain is caused and then take away your hand. That would take too much time. Instinctively you take away your
hand. Actually, it is not you doing that but your guardian angel: the Autonomous Nervous System.

Fortunately - or unfortunately - the A.N.S. also interferes in less clear threats. It not only acts upon physical
threat but also mental and emotional threats. The A.N.S. is constantly checking whether you can cope with the
situation you are currently facing. It compares the situation with past experiences. If it comes to the conclusion

that there is no recent history of you coping with it well, then it overrules your system.

DANCER'S THREATS

Physical threats

On the physical plane, dancers may face a lot of threats for the A.N.S. If you have not made a recent habit of
dancing five dances non-stop - to the fullest - this situation brings you stress when you are facing your final. Your
mind has decided that you keep on dancing during this final. Whatever happens. The A.N.S. checks your men-
tal instruction with the ability of your body. Without a recent history of simulating that final, the body does not
reassure the A.N.S. that it will work out well. The A.N.S. now warns you by sending stress signals. It fears you
cannot control the situation well.

Other physical threats may be lack of stamina, lack of food, lack of liquids’, etc. Physical threat may also come from

lack of warm-ups and cool downs and lack of experience with the choreography or new techniques to perform well.

Mental threats

Mental threats come from mental thoughts, demands and desires you cannot control. The thought ‘Now J must
dance well to make the final’ is decoded by the A.N.S. as a threat. It knows very well that you do not decide
the outcome in terms of results. That depends on many factors you cannot control: for instance your partner, the
other competitors, the judges, the audience, etc. Again the A.N.S. interferes by sending stress signals in the hope
that you will change your mind.

Other mental threats are, for instance, your focus on what your partner should or shouldn’t do. The Autonomous
Nervous System knows very well that you cannot control your partner and starts sending stress signals once
more in the hope that you will change your mind. Also thoughts and feelings about the judges induce stress. It
doesn’t matter whether you feel positive or negative about them. The A.N.S. knows you cannot control them
and such mental focus will be punished with stress.

A final example of mental threat is the other competitors. Any thoughts, feelings or desires about them activate
the A.N.S. because you cannot control them. You cannot control who enters the competition, you cannot con-
trol which couples are in your heat, you cannot control where they will be on the floor or how they will behave

during the dance, you cannot control how they are dressed, etc.

€motional threats

€motional threats often seem illogical to our minds (or the minds of others). Emotional threats are situations you

need to change to make you feel happy (when you are happy your A.N.S. is not activated!). Luckily there are

Stress and preparations

many things we can do to make ourselves happy. Some people like to isolate themselves in between rounds to
relax mind and body. Others might like to socialize in between rounds and can act on that too. These are things
we can control. Unfortunately there are also emotional demands we cannot control. Like the music, the venue
in which the competition is held, how your partner feels and acts that day, whether the audience loves you or
not, whether you get positive feedback from your coach in between rounds or not. Any thoughts, feelings and

desires on such issues activate your A.N.S. because - again - you do not have control over them!

STRESS DEGRADES THE QUALITY OF DANCING

Pressure - mental, physical and/or emotional - does not only cause tension in your mind (which is often experi-
enced as unclear thinking or no thinking at all: the sense of having a cloud in your head). It also prevents your
body from feeling free to dance as it normally could. Stress leads to short breathing high in the chest, a faster
heartbeat, too much consumption of energy and too tense muscles, because the A.N.S. has started the produc-
tion of hormones and chemicals.

Your Autonomous Nervous System is not your enemy! It only indicates that there are threats and that you should
change the way you handle the current situation. The production of hormones and other chemicals create a high
level of tension.

These hormones are very helpful if your body really needs to protect itself physically or mentally. In competitive
dancing this is not the case. Therefore you should not activate this defence mechanism. Other signals could be

given to stop the feeling of being threatened and - consequently - stop the A.N.S. producing stress signals.

THE AUTONOMOUS NERVOUS SYSTEM LIKES SECURITY

fis soon as you are required to do something that the A.N.S. knows you are not used to, it interferes. Next to
physical stress signals (short breath, tense muscles, clouded mind) the Autonomous Nervous System also tries to
help you. tt makes you do things it knows you can do well: the habits you have created in the past.

Under stress dancers will find that they produce old levels of skills, including old mistakes you would not recent-
ly have made during practice. All your new learning has gone since it is not yet recognized by the A.N.S. as a

habit you can control in any situation!

STRESS IS ALL AROUND YOU

To relieve yourself from stress, you first have to search for the source of the threatening feelings. There are many
possible answers to this question. In this paragraph you find your stress inducers via the Stress Indicator Test.
The list of stress factors for competitions is quite endless. All of them seem to hold some kind of threat. Some
threats are real and therefore you have to learn to cope with them. Most threats can fortunately be prevented.

YOU CAN LOWER YOUR STRESS LEVEL

Finding yourself in the medium or danger zone doesn’t mean that that has to stay that way. At first glance the power of
your Autonomous Nervous System looks so strong that you cannot prevent it from interfering, But you can hel Ip yourself
to prevent the A.N.S. from being activated to a great extent, Generally speaking you need to learn to focus on issues you

can control and so reduce your stress. That is what Part One and Part Two of this book are about!

PERSONALITY AS A STRESS CAUSING FACTOR

Personalities can have qualities that induce stress. Most partnerships consist of people with different personali-
ty traits (it is true: opposites do attract!). Becoming aware of these differences helps you to respect the different
needs and behaviour of others.

The characteristics of you as a dancer can be described in many ways. One is your general approach of life. This
is a combination of your goals (What do you want), motivations (Why do you want it) and values (What do you
get from it). Insight into your characteristics can be helpful to understand your lifestyle and the problems that
might occur. The way you approach life in general gives insights into possible stress causing factors appearing
during dance competitions.

A leading international marketing communications agency, Young and Rubicam, conducts large-scale worldwide
research every three years. This has culminated in the Cross-Cultural Consumer Characterizations (Four C’s).

They mention seven characterizations‘.

Resigned

People with a personality called Resigned are not often found in the competitive dancing world. The resigned
are people who see surviving in this world as their dominant goal. They have given up on getting more out of
life than they already have. Their values are based on subsistence only. Therefore dancing not often plays a lead-

ing role in their lives. They mostly stay at home and are quite often idle.

Strugglers

Strugglers are people who work hard in life for improvement but they have regularly suppressed feelings that no
matter how hard they might work, they will always fail to succeed. This fear has not (always) to do with the
skills of these people but mostly with their state of mind. And even when they get the success, they will still be
unhappy: they already fear that they will lose it again.

To improve their quality of life, the strugglers need other approaches to life itself. They need to find the power
of positive thinking to become more happy (and more successful) people. They need to transform their self-

destroying prophecies into self-fulfilling prophecies.

Stress and preparations

Amongst dancers Strugglers are found regularly. Even in successful partnerships sometimes one of the two dancers is a
Struggler. Up to a certain point the non-Struggling partner can compensate for the negative expectations of the Struggling
partner. The partner and coach need to learn to express only positive experiences in a clear way. When they would say,
’That wasn’t too bad’ they might mean that it was actually quite good. The Struggler would probably hear 'bad’ only
which would confirm the feeling that everything is useless...

At some point in the dance career compensation by coaches and partner can no longer help. The Struggler needs

to change his or her attitude towards life to get further improvements and more fulfilment.

Mainstreamers

The Mainstreamers form the largest group in society. Mainstreamers search for security. They like to conform to
the rules of society and feel a strong family responsibility. They like to be socially accepted. Dancing provides Main-
streamers with an opportunity to make friends and have a social life. They have no urgent need to win. Generally
they do not have a lot of competition stress since the results (an uncontrollable factor) are not so important.
Mainstreamers might not focus on their performing qualities and this only causes problems when they are
teamed up with a partner who needs more out of dancing. Knowing these differences between partners is the

first step toward better performances. The next step is to agree on how to fulfil both partners’ needs.

Aspirers

Aspirers have a need to be perceived as successful. They are envious and ambitious. Dancing provides them with
the means to success and status. They have an urgent need to win: success for them is essential for happiness.
This state of mind can lead to too much need for extrinsic stimuli. The thought have to win’ does not stimu-
late them to dance freely: it blocks the dancer. The Autonomous Nervous System interferes, since winning is an
uncontrollable factor. These dancers could be made aware that the best results only occur through good danc-

ing. So instead of focusing on the result, they could learn to focus on the dancing.

Succeeders

Succeeders have a lot in common with Aspirers. The difference is that they feel they already have success, They
want to keep that success and stay in control. They long for more material success and look for recognition.
Dancing provides the means for this recognition: success in dancing is felt as crucial. Like Aspirers they have a

tendency to ‘over’ focus on the results. They could learn to focus more on the dancing itself to relieve themselves

from unnecessary stress.

€xplorers and Reformers

These two groups are the innovators of society. They like to bend the rules of society to suit their own needs and
opinions of what is good and bad.

An Explorer's goal in life is to look for self-identity. They often look rebellious. They work hard on self-confidence
and self-satisfaction. Explorers like to experiment with their dancing style because it makes them feel good. Getting

or keeping success is not essential for happiness. Expression through dance is their ultimate goal. Reformers have a

Part One: The Competition Day

lot in common with the Explorers. They are mostly somewhat older. They like to balance out things more and are
no longer rebellious. They no longer need to change the world but concentrate on their own balance in life.

Both personality groups can get competition stress when they get lost in the normal competition routines and
expectations. They have - more than others - the power to perform for themselves and could learn to concen-

trate on their own goals and values.

UNPROFESSIONAL PREPARATIONS
Major stress occurs when you feel that the preparations were not good enough. You could prevent the follow-

ing problems by better preparations:

The week before the competition you had to work too hard and now you lack stamina.

The week before the competition you slept too little and now you lack stamina.

You arrived too late at the venue to really feel at ease in it.

You arrived too late to physically and mentally prepare yourself well.

Before and during the competition you consume the wrong food and beverages. Your body is now physicaly not in optimal condition.
You have not done good warm-ups and cool downs during the competition. This prevents your body technically from pro-
ducing the required performance. The A.N.S. produces signals of physical threat.

You are using choreographies and/or techniques that have not settled well enough.

You are wearing outfits that are not good enough or were ready to0 late.

You didn’t simulate dancing five dances to the fullest in your practice. Your A.N.S. sees your demand to dance a final as a

possible physicat threat.

SOLUTIONS FOR SOME UNPROFESSIONAL PREPARATIONS

Know the venue: use your senses

Both national and international competitions may be held in locations that are unknown to you. Yet it is impor-
tant to feel free in each location. Studies show that people perform better if they know the room well. You there-
fore need to know new places before you start dancing in them.

Sight is the first sense you really want to activate consciously upon arrival in a new location. Look around well;
take in what you see and then close your eyes. Try to visualize the ballroom. Ask yourself questions about the
place. What is the colour of the floor, the ceiling, the chairs, the stage and the doors? What kind of lighting is
there? Where is the exit? Where do you get on the floor? Where will the audience be? Where will the adjudi-
cators stand? And so on and so on. Close your eyes again if you can’t answer all the questions and ask yourself
the same questions. In just a few minutes you will feel much more at home.

Sound is the second sense you can activate. All locations have their own sounds. Often quite soft sounds so you
do not notice them consciously. Your A.N.S. however does hear them and might interpret them as conspicuous
or even dangerous. To take in the sounds consciously you just sit down and relax your body. Close your eyes
and listen to the sounds for a minute or two. Breathe consciously. You have now done two good things: relax-

ing and consciously taking in all the subdued sounds.

Stress and preparations

Most of you do use the sense touch. Maybe you could improve this sense by doing the following. You first walk around the
floor and feel it (with your competition shoes on). Try to cover the whole floor. Feel your feet on the floor and look around
well. Later on, you may want to dance a little too to feel familiar with the floor while dancing,

The fourth sense - smell - can be activated too! Just smell the building consciously. Each building has a different
smell. Take it in and fee! how this smell influences you. If you do not like the smell, then take some time to real-
ly take it in until you get used to it.

Taste is quite a different matter. Of course you cannot taste the building like Hansel and Gretchen did in the fairy
tale. However, you can start your day by consciously tasting the water, the drinks and the food offered in the

building when you intend to make use of them during the competition. Just to ease your A.N.S.

TPs

Do the above with the dressing room. You might spend longer there than on the competition floor! The same applies to your
hotel room...

When you have to perform in a new building and this competition is crucial to you, then try to visit the building at least one
day before you have to perform in it. Activate your senses. While you sleep you can internalize the experience. The next day
you will feel more at home in the place and will be able to perform more freely.

A final tip to help yourself on an emotional level: make friends with the staff of the building or the staff of the organization of

the competition {this helps if you enjoy socializing).

Know when to ‘be present’: not everybody needs the same

Some people like to take a long time to get in the mood for the competition. They arrive early and like to be in
the empty venue. Others need the excitement of people around them and like to come in later. Know yourself
and act accordingly. Do note that your needs might not be the same as your partner's. Do discuss this and plan
it to match both your needs.

Another aspect of you "being present’ could be influenced by a need for isolation or socialization. Your person-
ality may give you some indications as to what fits you best. Resigned, Strugglers and Mainstreamers rather like
to be in the ballroom itself. There they can socialize. Achievers and Succeeders like to get around, mostly look-
ing for compliments and approval. Explorers and Reformers may want to search for a more private spot where

they feel good. That might just be complete isolation. There is nothing wrong with that.

TIPS
Find a place in the venue where you can concentrate on the competition (or just withdraw and relax).

Discuss with your partner when you both need to be together to prepare yourselves for the next round.

Part One: The Competition Day

THE ‘PLAGUE’
In spring 1995 a great deal of criticism on the behaviour of couples during finals was published in Dance News'.
These complaints included couples running off the floor between dances, pouring water on the floor, swigging

from bottles and so on.

Hozel's Hang Ups

Mrs Hazel Fletcher even referred to it as The plague’. Mr Brian Allan spoke about new rules to forbid this awful
behaviour, At the Dutch National Dance Academy? we heid some discussions on this ’Plague’. Should the men-
tioned behaviour be forbidden? Basically we held the opinion that rules would not solve the problem. It would
be more helpful to indicate what you can do compared to telling people what you cannot do. Peter Townsend
and I took some time to analyse the problem and to come up with better solutions. We investigated the behav-

jour and found that it wasn’t effective nor necessary.

BETTER PREPARATIONS SOLVE THE 'PLAGUE' DURING FINALS
You can prevent a lot of the awful "habits’ during finals like running off the floor, drinking water or brushing your

shoes before you get onto the dance floor.

Drinking

If you are in good condition you do not need to drink between dances in a final. To get rid of this drinking habit
you could end every practice session by dancing a complete final with a normal length of music - and intervals -
without any drinking. This gives you the necessary stamina and adjustment of the body to perform five dances
without additional fluids’, Your Autonomous Nervous System starts to recognize your behaviour as a normal pat-

tern and stops being alarmed when you need to dance a final.

Slippery floor

The best remedy against slippery floors is, of course, increasing your movement control and balance. Yet even
skilled dancers find it hard to dance on very slippery floors. Unfortunately the remedy does not tie in brushing
shoes between each of the dances. Most of the time the floor will feel slippery again after the first few steps.
The remedy of wetting your shoe soles with water helps sometimes. Do not wet your shoes during the final. That

is preparation work which you could do in the dressing room.
TIP
Don't dab the soles of your shoes with water, but soak them in it if you really want to have control on a slippery floor. Be very

careful about where to throw the water and clean up afterwards, The organizers will appreciate that.

A FINAL IS ONE COMPLETE PERFORMANCE
Next to doing good preparations you need to realize that a final is seen as one connected performance of five
dances. Commencing with the entry onto the floor and only ending with the exit after the last dance. You could

start practising treating the final as one performance...

On the other hand it may seem difficult for you to dance to your maximum when you do five dances in a row
with a non-stop input of energy. You may feel that if you do not recharge and concentrate between each dance,
the quality of your outcome declines dance by dance. To help you The Nine Step Connection ModePhas been
developed. This model helps you to rehearse what you personally could do best between the dances. The model
helps to minimize stress and makes it much easier to perform the dances in a series.

The time between each of the semi-final or final dances might not be so long, especially when there is no open judg-
ing after each dance. After a thorough analysis of those few seconds between dances, we found that nine different

tasks are waiting for you. These controllable tasks formed the basis of The Nine Step Connection Model®.

THE NINE STEP CONNECTION MODEL?

Some of the causes of stress can be prevented by being better prepared. Having done all that, it can still be hard for
a dancer to really dance stress-free in competitions. More focus on controllable factors is needed. The Nine Step
Connection Model? makes you aware of tasks you really can control yourself. Once the Autonomous Nervous
System recognizes that your focus is on controllable tasks it is quite happy to relax and refrain from bringing stress
into your body and mind. The model hands you techniques to send less threatening signals to the A.N.S. The tech-
niques help you to get the necessary relaxation, concentration and connection with your partner. The model brings

you closer to yourself, your partner, your dance skills and that makes your free to dance to your maximum.

Greater performances

THREE IMPORTANT STAGES BEFORE THE NEXT DANCE STARTS

There are the three stages the body and mind need to pass through to dance to the best of your abilities.

HYSICAL AND MENTAL PREPARATION =

THE ENDING OF A DANCE

The ending of a dance consists of two steps: the actual finish of the dance and reception of the applause. These
two steps are taken almost automatically. Some deeper understanding could provide,a greater impact on the actu-
al ending of the dance and could bring you more relaxation and new energy diting the applause. In the next

chapter these steps are described.

THE PHYSICAL AND MENTAL PREPARATION

Mental and physical preparation would be greatly helped when preceded by some kind of relaxation. This relax-
ation could clean your body and mind from what has just happened (the previous dance). Unfortunately this third
step is quite often forgotten. This is the moment that the ‘plague’ starts. Instead of relaxing, dancers run off the
floor, drink from bottles and brush their shoes. They may think they really need that but in reality it is their only
known - unconscious - way to calm the body and mind...

Without taking this necessary relaxation dancers often go on with their *plague’ syndrome. They do not con-
centrate nor recharge their body with new energy for the next dance. They prefer to wait for the music to inspire
them. Is there a solution? Certainly. In the next chapter you will find information on relaxation, concentration

and energizing the body.

THE ENTRY TO THE NEXT DANCE

When you have recharged and concentrated correctly, you still need to go a few steps further. There is no use in
just walking with your partner onto the floor without real personal intent and connection. Many men waik onto
the floor and invite their partners on automatic pilot. This doesn’t look good to the judges nor to the audience.
But even worse is the fact that they also leave out some personal inspiration for their next dance. They miss the
essential human relationship!

You could work on the relationship with your partner, find the selected(!) position on the floor and start the next

dance with an authentic invitation. Steps 6, 7 and 8 show you the way in the next chapter.

STEP BY STEP

You just have a few seconds in between two dances of a semi-final or final. During these few seconds nine tasks
are awaiting you. If you follow each of the steps consciously you strongly reduce the chance of taking stress into
your next dance. You have no time to look back at the previous dance, to think about mistakes your partner

made, to feel the pressure of having to perform well, etc. Your body and mind only focus on the controllable tasks

for the next dance.

STEP 1: THE FINISH

Geginnings and endings are moments peopie can easily remember. It is, for instance, more easy to remember the
beginning and the end of a movie or a book than everything that happens in between. That is the reason why
advertisers always put their brand at the end of a television commercial and not somewhere in the middle.
Endings are memorable moments in life and also in dance performances. It is a moment where you can impress
by expressing a characteristic ending.

Most of you spend a lot of time practising the beginning of a dance for the same reason. This practising is easier
since it can be structured and performed in the same way. Practising endings is more difficult because you never
know when the music stops and at what point in the choreography. You need to practise your improvisation skills

in endings to feel free. The rewards for good endings are, however, almost the same as for good beginnings.

SUGGESTIONS
it is important to find a natural finish to the dance. When the music ends, the dance must also end quickly. Judges don’t want
to see an abrupt finish just because the music ends, but neither do they want to see you dance for another ten seconds.
Never stop before the music does. This may give the impression that you lack stamina, Consequently this situation could influ-
ence your marks negatively.

In some competitions you might have some time to prepare the finish, Maybe because the compére indicates to the judges
the amount of time left, or because there is an orchestra so you can actually hear the finish approaching. In these situations
you should finish in harmony with the music to create a professional look.

End in the character of the dance. Ending a Paso Doble needs a different expression from the ending of a Rumba.

Part One: The Competition Doy

Prevent overactivity. Women shouldn't spin a great distance away from their partners in the preliminary rounds, In the final
they might put some more effort into it.

Ballroom dancing stems from social dancing. It depicts the relationship between two people in various ways. It is disturbing
to the illusion when a man portrays a caring feeling in Waltz and then ignores his partner completely when the music stops.

This does not butld up the credibility of the performance.

STEP 2: THE APPLAUSE
Taking the applause could be a ‘receiving’ experience. You do not need to impress and demand attention from
the audience and judges. You have already had two minutes for that and you will get that opportunity again dur-

ing the next dance.
There is a trend amongst dancers to exaggerate their bows to the audience. These dancers are now '

ing
instead of ’receiving’. They do it automatically and do not enjoy the pleasure and relaxation that comes from
genuinely receiving the applause. It is a special gift that the audience applauds, It functions as an appraisal for
the couples on the floor and for their performance. You could be thankful and enjoy that moment. It could be
the first contribution to the regeneration you will need for your next dance!

The more you are able to genuinely receive, the more generous an applause will be your reward. Have a look at
ballet dancers taking bows. Notice their humility and receiving state.

The most important reason for concentrating on *receiving’ the applause is that it can take you away for a moment
from the competition at hand. When you are in syne with the moment and receive, you will forget the last dance,

the other competitors, the judges, the next dance, etc. You will have a short moment to regenerate.

SUGGESTIONS

Presenting the arms above the shoulder line is mostly a sign of overactivity or tension. It takes more energy and stops the  feel-
ing of receiving. (In big arenas like sports halls maybe a bigger gesture is needed. Still make sure that you stretch yourself to
open yourself up genuinely.)

Take the whole lengch of the applause. Do not start walking off the floor or to the starting position for the next dance as long
as the applause continues. This is quite offensive to the audience (they are giving and you refuse to receive). Also you will
miss some seconds to relax and enjoy the moment.

You might think that the applause is not for you and therefore want to walk away. Please realize that it is not up to you to
decide and check whether the applause is meant for you or not. You will never find the real answer. Just take what is given,
Dance Life has published a CD called The Final Connection. On this CD you find four complete finals with announcements
made by Bill Irvine MBE. Applause is added after each dance. Learn to make ita ‘receiving’ experience as long as the applause
continues. Make it a habit you can control.

Occasionally the applause can be too long. For instance when the audience wants to support a specific couple, These applaus-
25 can sometimes be too much - or even offensive. if you are facing such a situation it could make you look more profession-
al if you stop the applause by thanking your supporters and then walk away from it. It will show great control.

Do not put yourself in a fixed position during the applause. Feel free to relax and move your body gently. You could, for

instance, scan the audiences with your eyes (from left to right and reverse). You could give your arms some freedom to move

The Nine Step Connection Model?

as well: a gesture of opening your arms or a gesture of indicating the part of the audience you are acknowledging. Maybe you
also want to acknowledge your partner at some point during the applause and indicate that with a gesture. You could walk
towards the audience or walk to another part of the audience.

Do not seek acknowledgement from specific people in the audience. You have no control over them, Consequently such @

focus will activate your Autonomous Nervous System. Just glance over the audience without making personal contact.

EXAMPLE
A few years ago a world championship was held in Germany. Six thousand people were present. Probably 5000 Germans and
100 people for the couples from the other nations. The German couple could win the championship. Would it be true that

98% of the applause must have been for them? Should the ather competitors not have received the applause...?

Just for their few fans it was worthwhile for the other competitors to also receive the applause. And probably some or more
Germans in the audience might have applauded for other contestants too. You never really know who is appreciating whom!
To illustrate this not knowing whio appreciates whom: it was the German judge who marked the German couple second in the

decisive dance and so contributed to another couple winning...

THE RECEIVING TECHNIQUE FOR APPLAUSE

Start with the bow or curtsy with a toned, yet not overstretched body. Open yourself up to the audience. Your
arms can make gestures ike a modest ‘Here Iam opening myself to you’ or "Thank you’. It is crucial to receive
genuinely. This not only comes with the right gestures but also comes from a mental understanding that receiving
is now your focus. The modest ‘Here / am’ is helped by not charging towards the audience too much, Just take a
few steps in their direction. Prevent over-toning at this stage. Open your arms towards the audience. First by reach-
ing out to them. This is a forward gesture. Then spread the arms to be able to receive.

You could start the forward gesture at the centre of levity. This helps you not to open the arms in a too high pos-
ition. The centre of levity is the part of the body where emotions and feelings are more concentrated and felt. It
is located in the region of the sternum of the upper trunk {down at the middle of the torso}. In chakra termin-

ology it is called the heart chakra. It is the place where Western society people gesture ‘Me’.

Receiving the applause togheter
You are not just receiving the applause as a soloist. Sharing the applause with your partner fits the collective
effort you both have put in. It is like during the dance where you need to be aware of your partner and react to

each other’s movements.

SUGGESTIONS

To be able to receive it is easter for the partners to not hold hands during the applause.

Make sure that the woman is either diagonally forward to the man’s position or at a parallel position. If the man is positioned
in front of his partner this will suggest that he feels he is more important than she is, In the Western culture this is not often
appreciated. It is more likely that the audience will perceive the man as being arrogant.

Lead and follow are also needed in receiving the applause. First connect with your partner (with your eyes} when you want

Port One: The Competition Day

to change your position to be sure he or she can follow your change of position too. Maybe you can give a gesture to your
partner to indicate the direction you want to go to (forward, backward, change to another side of the floor}?

At the end of the applause the man needs to give his partner a clear signal of how he wants to walk Off the floor (or to the
starting position af the next dance}. Does he want her to take his hand or his arm to walk away together? And on which side

of the body?

STEP 3: THE RELAXATION

Although the idea of relaxing in the middle of a semi-final or final may sound strange to you, it is vitally impor-
tant to consider this step. After the end of a dance, you need to let go of the dance that has just ended in order
to prepare for the next dance. Without this relaxation you will run through the whole final without being able
to regenerate and make some changes.

Such changes are necessary because the character of the next dance differs so much from the previous one. And
maybe even more changes are necessary. Maybe you are focused too much on results and too little on the danc-
ing. This might influence the performance negatively. The relaxation will set your mind and body free to do the

next six steps of the model better.

SUGGESTIONS

Do not spend time analysing what went wrong, whether the judges looked at the right moment or not. Stop thinking about
the past and concentrate on your body for just a moment.

Avoid thinking about anything other than the body during this step. Concentrate on a Cleaning Breath: inhale through the
nose and exhale through the mouth very slowly and deeply. Try to fill up your lungs completely during inhalation (not only
the upper lungs). Also try to empty your lungs completely before inhaling again. Give some space for fresh oxygen. You'll
need it.

You can help yourself to breathe more slowly by counting the speed of the inhaling and exhaling. Count I-23-4 as you inhale
and also as you exhale. This conscious counting will keep your mind occupied. Therefore the Autonomous Nervous System
‘has little chance of bringing in stressful thoughts, The counting will actually clean your mind.

Concentrate on physically breathing in through the nose and out through the mouth.

First feel the air entering at the lower part of the abdomen. Then feel the chest fill itself with air. Consciously notice where
your torso expands on inhalation and decreases on exhalation, This conscious breathing and feeling of the body helps you to
relax physically. It cleans your body more than breathing unconsciously.

It helps to concentrate on the navel. Imagine that air comes into the body through a nose where the navel is located.

The Cleaning Breath costs just a few seconds and nobody - but you - will notice it. Practise it each time you simulate a final.

Then you will create a positive habit you can control and use on the competition floor.

Relaxation in between dances is quite normal

Step 3, relaxation, is not an unconventional step. In many other sports it is acknowledged that it is better to
relax before getting into the next stage of energy demand. The body just cannot function efficiently and effect-

ively when it is kept toned and totally energized continuously. Take aerobics for example: the technique of

The Nine Step Connection Model®

aerobics demands high impact exercises alternated by small breaks of a few seconds. Only that way can the
sportsman or sportswoman keep on functioning well. And look at boxing: in between the rounds the boxers try

to relax and release tension. They know they need to regenerate as well as they can in the few seconds they get.

And in tennis it is exactly the same...

Muscle toning method
Some dancers build up so much stress that the Cleaning Breath is not sufficient to relax the body. This may even
happen in the preliminary rounds. For these - hopefully rare - cases there is a stronger method to relax: the

muscle toning method.

Stand up straight with the legs about 30 centimetres apart. Tone your muscles as firmly as possible. Start with putting tension

on the toes, then build it up towards the head (toes,feet, calves, thighs, buttocks, abdomen, chest, back, fingers, hand, lower

and upper arm, shoulder line, neck and facial muscles). Increase the tension until it begins to hurt all over. Then let all the

tension drop on an exhalation. Repeat the sequence up to three times,

: Some people call this method "The Big Draw’. Of course a person using this method does not look nice. Use this
method only far away from the judges and the audience. The dressing room would probably be the best location.

| Practice this ’emergency’-method weil in advance. If too little tension has been used, relaxation will not follow.

qi If you put too much tension in it, muscle-ache might be the result. You will need some good practice to find out

li how much tension to put into it.

In chapter 2-8-5 many other exercises are given to help you to relax off the competition floor. Some take just a

|

find out what makes you feel comfortable.

| STEP 4; THE CONCENTRATION
i It is scientifically proven that physical activity always happens after mental activity. Your thinking leads your body

i so to speak. It helps you to prepare your body by first consciously thinking about the next dance. Before the
os music starts.
| You need to prepare your thinking - or concentration - well in advance. Only through repeated identical con-
| centration will you create a positive habit. Thanks to such a habit, you will have control over it during competi-
tions - even under stress
Concentration means making choices on what to think about (and consequently what not to think about). It is
easier to concentrate on only one item than on a series of items. The more concise the thought, the easier it will
be for you to connect with it. The one item you select is called the Key Word. This word gets you into the right

frame of mind to prepare your body for the next dance. Chapter 1-5 will inform you in depth on how to select

your key words.

Key word examples
SUGGESTIONS

Key Words can refer to many issues: ‘for instance the character of the dance, the posture you want to have, a part of the chore-
ography that inspires you, a general mistake you want to avoid or a certain strength you have in this dance.

There is no use in just thinking about the Key Word and repeating that word over and over again before you start.
Concentration by Key Words works best when you translate the thinking into a kind of sensing in the body (Step 5).

Always phrase the Key Words in a positive way. Do not chink for instance ‘Don't be weak’ in Tango, but replace it with a word
‘ike ‘Dominating’. This Key Word not only gives a positive physical feeling or reaction, bat will also lead easily to a very posi
tive creative visualization’.

it fs not necessary for you and your partner to use the same Key Words. No two dancers are alike. The rote of the man and
woman differs as well,

Select the Key Words together with your coach and also use them during practice.
STEP 5: THE ENERGY
After concentration it is time your body takes on the necessary toning. By coming into contact with this energy

you will be prepared to dance before the music starts and before the physical contact with your partner. If you

The Nine Step Connection Model®

have done the ‘Concentration Step’ well (by thinking and sensing), your body has already started to tone for the

next dance. Still a check at this point is helpful to be ready in time.

SUGGESTIONS

Make your body alert, prepared and ready, for movement. When the energy in your body is too. free, it will take too much en-
ergy and effort to physically get moving. So tone and align the body. Check the position of feet, hips, and shoulders. Check
the muscle tension from toe to top. Check the flexibility of ankles, knees, hips, arms and neck. Your body is now ready for the
next step.

Each dance has a different character. Most dances therefore need a different toning (this also applies to Standard dances}.
Get the right knowledge from your dance teacher if you are not aware of these differences.

Some dancers start toning their bodies after the music has started. They probably need the music for inspiration. This is a
waste of time. Also it makes you depend on an uncontrollable factor. It is better to start with your inner body rhythm that you
can control and will not cause stress.

Getting your energy ready does not necessarily mean that you have to start moving your body rhythmicaily before the music
starts (lots of Latin American dancers do this). It means that you have prepared your body so well that you can start at any
time. Advanced dancers might even want to start moving even later than the music starts because they know how to handle

their energy very well. This can also create a great effect.

STEP 6: THE RELATIONSHIP

While you dance you always need to be aware of the relationship with the partner. In most parts of The Nine
Step Connection Model® this is also the case. Exceptions to this rule are Steps 3, 4 and 5. During these steps you
are more concerned with yourself.

Therefore Step 6 is needed to re-establish a connection with your partner. This could be a visual connection or
taking hands consciously. This step may sound unimportant, but how you handie it is vital for the performance.
Just by looking or taking hands you will send messages to your partner.

Are these messages positive and sincere or are you putting on a computer program? Mechanical relationships will

not be rewarded with excellence.

STEP 7: THE POSITIONING

Your positioning as a couple on the floor can be an important stress-causing factor. You have Many options. You
can try to find the most available spot on the floor. Or you can try a strategic move to be more visible to the
judges or audience. Some of you may think it is more tactical to move towards your main competitor to dom-
inate this couple (others like to get out of the way)... Controlling where you start (instead of depending on where
the others are on the floor} does relax your Autonomous Nervous System.

The human body likes habits to some extent. Good habits make things easier. You help your body and mind by
choosing the starting point of each dance before a competition starts. After a few rounds your body gets used to
your choices (for instance that if you start in corner A, the Waltz always follows and in the opposite corner the

Tango, in the middle of long side B, the Viennese Waltz, etc.

There is one more reason to select starting positions before you get onto the floor with your partner. Your walk-
ing to your starting position has an impact on how the judges and audience perceive you. A straight line is more

convincing than lingering over the floor.

The Nine Step Connection Model®

It is not to your advantage to just wander around the floor, looking and feeling out of place. Make choices well
in advance. This gives you a clear focus of where to go next. Your eyes show that. It gives a good impression on
the floor. And remember: your partner needs to know these starting points as well, to relax the mind and to have

a clear focus when walking up to that position.

NOTES TO THE ABOVE EXAMPLE

Only two waiting positions are used to have good access to all four starting positions: position A and B.

The starting positions for the dances are scattered around the floor: each dance at different spots and making sure that alt
sides of the dance floor are covered.

The selection of the starting points is also influenced by the order of the dances. In The Open British Championships, the
Standard dancers will first dance the slow dances and then Tango and finish with Quickstep. Waltz and Foxtrot need to be

apposite each other since Foxtrot follows Waltz. Tango needs to be opposite Foxtrot and Quickstep needs to be opposite

Tango.

SUGGESTIONS

‘The tactic of starting a dance at the same spot in each round means that judges will always see the same part of your routine.
In competitions with many rounds this tactic might make you look boring. In these situations you might want to start each
round with a different part of your routine, Or you could start the dances in opposite corners per round (if you prefer to always
start with the same routine).

Do not walk away if you have selected a crowded starting point. You can always begin earlier or later than the other couples.
In stationary dances like Rumba you could dance yourself out of the crowd by doing pure basics and start a few seconds later
with your routine. You will get rewarded for that.

You may walk away to an empty spot. This only works if you are very concentrated and do not have any stress. Walk with a

natural feeling of control and dominance. Just a few dancers qualify for this strategy: the stress free ones...

Whatever strategy you choose, it is important to make a quick and clear decision about where you are going to commence
your next dance and to move into that position. Do this as a couple! Never drag your partner to the other end of the floor.
You can control your territory while you are at your selected starting position by having a clear eye connection with your part-
ner. This energetic link will keep most other couples out of the way.

You can expand your territory if you like by increasing the distance between yourself and your partner while still keeping a

strong eye connection.

STEP 8: THE INVITATION

You are standing in position on the floor. You are prepared at mental and physical levels and are ready to share
the next dance with your partner. Do you just start to dance when the music starts? Do you make it a real invi-
tation? Or do you act like a robot?

Try to make the Invitation step a joint venture with each of you contributing and each of you having responsi-
bilities. The following suggestions are no rules. They are meant to make you aware of your partner before you

start dancing. Recognition of your partner increases the quality of the following dance significantly.

Standard dances: five contact points
In the Standard dances the Invitation step has five points where partners can give each other some kind of recog
nition:

1, Make eye contact. Do not just look at your partner but really see and notice him/her. This may just take a split second.
It is not the length that counts but the quality of the energetic linkage.

2, Feel the connection of the man’s left hand with the woman's right hand. Notice the moment the hands touch. Notice
how the skin feels. Notice the temperature of the hand, the moisture, etc. Feel the sensation of the connection.

3. Notice the connection of the bodies. The woman moves towards the man (or reverse). She closes the gap between the
two bodies. She is not pulled by the man’s left hand nor is the closing arranged by him putting his right hand around
her. The closing of the gap is only an action for the woman.

4. Sense the man’s right hand closing the hold on the woman's back. The man only closes his right arm around his part-
ner when he feels that her body is in the right position towards him. As long as he doesn’t close his right arm, his part-
ner will know that a correction is necessary.

5. Notice how the woman's left hand completes the hold on the man’s shoulder. The man will not start the dance before
his partner has given the signal that she is ready. As long as she doesn't put her left hand in position the connection ts
not optimal. The man may have a too firm or too loose grip on her for he might have changed his posture towards her),

A correction is then necessary before the next dance begins.

An important part of this five contact points structure is that each of you has your own actions and Tesponsibil-

ities to make the Invitation step work out well:

1. Eye contact 2 Action by both.
2. Man's left hand + Action by both (the man initiates but both of you will have Co feel the contact with your hands}.
3. Body contact : The action comes from the woman {until the man accepts it by getting into action 4).

The Nine Step Connection Model®

4. Man's right arm + The action comes from the man (until the woman accepts it by getting into action 5).
5, Woman’ left hand + The woman indicates to the man not to start but correct the connection by not closing

the hold. The man has to wait to dance until she accepts his hold.

A negative start will not lead to a great performance. Corrections during the dance are harder to make than at

the beginning!

Latin American dancing: two or three contact points

In the Latin American discipline not all dances starts in some kind of a hold. Sometimes partners will not even
have the possibility to have eye contact at the beginning of their routine. Therefore these individual points are
even more crucial.

1. Eye contact + Do not only look at the partner but really see and notice him/her.

2, Energetic connection: When there is no physical contact at the beginning of a dance, the connection could then be made
through space. Move the bodies in the rhythm of the next dance. Feel through space whether each
of you interacts well with the body of the other. Create a feeling of an invisible elastic connection
between the two of you.

3. Body contact = In some dances [like the Paso Doble) physical contact can be part of the opening position. Do notice
all connection points, it might be a hand, a leg or any other part of the body. Do notice the touch,
how the skin or the weight feels. Notice the temperature of the other body, etc. Feel the touch of

the connection. In other words: be aware of the other's body completely.

SUGGESTIONS

Without any visual recognition of each other you are bound to miss the human relationship in the following dance. This most-

y results in muscle work.

Ifyou do have some kind of hold then the same applies as for the Standard dances: make sure that in each point you give your

partner some kind of recognition.

Ifyou start your routine with no physical contact and/or no eye contact (for instance in side by side positions}, make sure you
make some eye contact with recognition before(!) you get into that position.

ft is not a set rule that only the man invites his partner. The opposite might also work very effectively (both in Latin American

and Standard dancing}.

In the beginning it may take some time before the right partnering has been found during the Invitation step. It
takes concentration, awareness, patience and practice to work it out properly. Make it a conscious habit. Your

reward will be great.
Start the quality curve as high os possible
Most of the time the quality of your dance declines as time progresses. The higher the quality at the beginning,

the greater the chance that the level of quality at the end of the dance remains high too. Starting without an opti-

STEP 9: THE ACTION
After having worked on the previous eight steps you are now ready for your next dance. You can control ail

these steps yourself. Your stress level will be quite low. You are now free to dance to your maximum.

SUGGESTION
Let the entry of this dance follow quite quickly after the music starts, This does not mean that ‘you need to rush into the dance.

it is acceptable you need a few more bars of music. Now the dance may begin,

HOW MUCH TIME DO YOU NEED TO DO ALL NINE STEPS?

Nine steps may seem to be a lot to do in a few seconds. All steps are important and need practice to fully con-
trol them. The more time you spend practising the Nine Steps, the less time you will need to do them properly.
In the beginning it might take half a minute to do them well between two dances. Later on you will find it quite

easy to do them within the seconds you are given during a competition,

Not all steps are equally important

Years of practical applications of the model shows that two steps are most crucial to influence the quality of your
performance. We found that the next dance would not be optimally performed if seven steps were all right but
these two not.

Step Four and Step Eight have proven to dominantly influence the quality of the next dance. Concentration gives
you the power not to think about the previous dance, the results, the judges, etc. You will stay in the right frame
of mind: focusing on what you can control.

The other crucial step is the Invitation step. You create in this step the human relationship with your partner
before the real action starts. Ballroom dancing comes from social dancing. It is all about the connection of a man
and a woman. This connection should be established before the dance begins. If you miss this step consciously,
your performance could easily give a mechanical impression. You also miss the last Opportunity for real team-

building.

The Nine Step Connection Model®

Since Step 4 and Step 8 are so important, they play a leading role in the evaluation programme for final practices
{see Chapter 1-6}. The Invitation step has been described well enough in this chapter. In the next chapter you

will find more information on how to create your Concentration step well.

RECOMMENDATIONS BEFORE YOU START USING THE NINE STEP CONNECTION MODEL®

The model is only helpful if you practise it often. You are advised to end each practice session by dancing a Nine
Step Connection Final. Your body will get used to the demand to perform at the best possible level. This new
habit will prevent a lot of stress at competitions where the same demands are made.

Ending the practice with a Nine Step Connection Final will give you insight into how much you have learned

during that session. You can now check your practice in a competition simulation.

SUGGESTIONS

Make an agreement with your partner not to stop when things go wrong. Continue until the final is finished.

Make an agreement with your partner not to discuss anything during the final. Concentrate on your own tasks.

Make an agreement with yourself not to drink water and not to brush your shoes in between dances.

Do not try to adopt the model in just a few weeks. It takes months to create the desired habit. Maybe start with Step 3 and
8, Any level of competitor can master these steps quite quickly. Then add - dance by dance - Step 4 and 5. Finally work out
the remaining steps.

Dance at least one simulated final in your new competition outfit before you use it on the competition floor, You will feel much
more relaxed when you know you can dance in it well.

Wear the same kind of shoes as you wear for competitions during your simulated final, Male Standard dancers could also wear
a jacket to get used to how it restricts movement just like at competitions when they have to dance in tails.

Do notice if there is any difference in the feeling your practice oulfit delivers compared to your competition outfit.

EXAMPLE
Theo mostly performed brilliantly during competitions simulations in the studio, During the competitions he danced very well
but not to his maximum. It was hard to discover what the causes were because Theo was dedicated to the Nine Step
Connection Model. Improvements there were hard to find. One day Theo mentioned that he actually preferred his practice
trousers to his competition trousers. The latter were tight to show his well-proportioned legs. Theo felt more free in his loos-
er practice trousers. Therefore the cut of iis competilion trousers was altered to give him that feeling of freedom. It was just

one more centimetre of material to make him feel better and dance better too.
