CONCENTRATION BY USING KEY WORDS

Just before you prepare your body for the next dance you could first think about what comes next. Energy does
follow thought. Ruud Vermeij published in 1994 his choreological' approach to Latin American dancing in a book
titled ‘LATIN. Thinking, sensing and doing Latin American Dancing.’ His subtitle was more than correct: think-
ing helps the body to be activated on time and with the right energy and toning.

This chapter focuses on finding long-lasting concentration points, also called key words. The more you use the
same key word for the same dance, the easier you can connect to it, even under stress. They become accessible
through a conscious habit. Dancers who have created a positive habit with personal key words and the related
body toning find that they have less stress during performances. Their mind is occupied with what they can con-
trol instead of having their Autonomous Nervous System (A.N.S.) picking up stress signals. The extra benefit of
key words is that under stress the A.N.S. will try to calm you down by making you do something you can do
well. When the use of key words has become your habit then your A.N.S. will pick that habit up!

TACTICS FOR SELECTING KEY WORDS

€ach dance needs its own key word. Finding your five (or ten) key words might take some time. Most dancers
need six to twelve months to create the positive habit in all five dances. So don’t be disappointed if it doesn’t
work for you during your first important performance. The system really works but you have to put in some effort
and you need some endurance. First of all you need to tune in with what you require on a long term basis. Then
you need to find out - by trial and error - which thought inspires you and gets the best out of you. Once you
think you have found a key word for a dance you need to practise it consciously at every practice session. For a
few weeks. To see if it works well. Maybe you will find out that the first choice doesn’t work well and you have
to start all over again. The examples in this chapter will show you how other dancers have done it. Just to inspire

you. Finding your concentration point could be based on one of the following tactics:

Concentration points

1. VISUALIZE THE BEGINNING OF THE PROGRAM

The technique of visualizing a beginning is often taught to people when they start public speaking. They are
advised to memorise the first few sentences. Such a preparation helps them to speak publicly: when these first
few sentences work well the Autonomous Nervous System relaxes and the speaker may continue more freely.

Dancers could use this system by visualizing the beginning of their routines just before they perform.

Part One: The Competition Day

New competitors only

For commencing competitive ballroom dancers it might be stressful to dance competitions. They lack knowledge
and experience on almost all levels: the physical demands (for instance stamina, mastering new choreography
and technical skills), the mental demands (the written and unwritten rules and conventions, etc.) the‘emotion-
al demands (coping with the partner, the success or lack of it, etc.) and the spiritual demands (for instaneé, danc-
ing for the personal thrill versus dancing for audiences). ont

If you have just started to compete, it might be better to not use key words at the beginning. You havé enough
new challenges in front of you! Just concentrate on the programme itself. In your mind you could visualize the

first few figures of your routine for the next dance.

NOTE
More advanced dancers could use this technique but probably the other four tactics will help much more. Maybe Once in a while
this tactic helps if you have taken up a new routine in which you don't, feel secure yet. These situations should however be avoid:

ed for major competitions and championships. In these situations you had better not take unnecessary risks.

2. COMPENSATE A WEAKNESS

The first choice for finding a key word could be the focus on one of the weaknesses of the dance you are work-
ing on. Of course you do not compare your skills to those of a professional world champion (unless you area
professional world finalist yourself). Compare your skills to competitors at the same level,

Most likely it will be easy for you to mention these weaknesses. You take private lessons to become aware of them
and how to solve them. Selecting one of these weaknesses could(!} be a good choice to find your key word.

The exclamation mark after the word ’could’ is important. Key words based on weaknesses can only work if you

have proven - more than once - that you can produce the skill to overcome the selected problem in competition

simulations during practice. The key word would then make you more conscious of the newly mastered skill.
t = Just remember that a key word cannot make you do what you have not yet mastered. So, if you have hardly ever
‘ shown the ability to produce good swing in Waltz during practice, you better not use *Swing’ as a key word.

This key word would only make you more aware of your inability and would thus create stress.

EXAMPLES
Swing (Waltz), Grounded (Tango), Posture (Foxtrot), Active on Forward (Viennese Waltz), Light (Quickstep), Cha-Cha-One
(Cha Cha), Rhythm (Samba, Stretch the legs (Rumba), Body toning (Paso Doble), Connection (five).

3. ACCENTUATE A STRENGTH

Strengths can be used as input for key words too. They will give you an immediate positive incentive. You will
focus on an aspect which you can do really well. Normally, focusing on your strengths will not lead to stress. It
is something your A.N.S. knows you can control and do well.

So if you are, for instance, naturally gifted in portraying good rhythm in Samba, it might be a good choice to find

a key word which connects you with rhythm. For instance ’Sing the rhythm’. Each time you would concentrate

Concentration by using key words

on this quality you could energise yourself without feeling any pressure. For very advanced dancers there is
sometimes a drawback in using a strength as a key word. It may lead to an overexposed asset which can deliv-
er a boring result. Take for instance rhythm again. If you only focus on rhythm in Samba, your dancing might
appear too loose due to lack of contrast. To ’sell’ your rhythmicality it might be better to focus on contrasting
points like sudden changes in the choreography. Now your natural rhythm might come across more strongly. The
key word could be ’Suddenness (to show versatility in rhythm)’. The judges and audience would see you as a
more accomplished dancer who is very rhythmical throughout the dance (your natural asset} and also shows the

necessary different approaches.

EXAMPLES
Swing (Waltz), Grounded (Tango), Posture (Foxtrot), Pounding Swing (Viennese Waltz), Light {Quickstep}, Cha-Cha-One (Cha

Cha}, Rhythm (Samba), Stretch the legs {Rumba}, Body toning (Paso Doble}, Connection (five).

4, ACCENTUATE THE CHARACTER OF THE DANCE

€ach dance has it’s own special character that needs to be depicted. That is why you dance five dances and not
one! You can use a part of the character of the dance for your key word.

Opinions may differ on what the dance is about but general outlines can be found. Most Latin American dances
are often described in a narrative way (what is the story). Rumba is often about love, Paso Doble is about the
matador and his cappa and so on. Standard dances seem to have more technical descriptions. Waltz is about clos-
ing feet and pounding swings, Quickstep shows fast and syncopated foot conversation, etc.

There is more to be said about character than the narrative and technical aspects. A more choreological approach

allows for a description of the character in other aspects:

Recents of body parts Which body parts are accentuated mostly?
Waltz could be seen as having an accent on shaping the upper torso, Quickstep having an accent
on feet, Rumba focusing on hips, Paso Doble on upper torso, arms and feet.

Dynamics How do you perform the movements: heavy, light, direct, strong, sudden, sustained,
free, flexible or bound.

Space How you use the space {location, projections, design, progression).

Actions used What you do as a dancer: twisting, turning, jumping, stretching, travelling, stillness, weight
transferring, gesturing, inclining and overbalancing.

Attitude to time Past, present or future.

Rhythm Phrasing, accents, impulse, impact, etc.

Non-verbal communication Appearance, behaviour, proximity, touch and territorial zones.

Communication Kinetical, referential, responsive, poetic and attitudes towards competitive dancing like

sport, show, art or social.

Key words that accentuate the character of a dance can be powerful. They take away the focus from the ’tech-

nique’ and demand attention for the image the dance portrays. In this way technique’ is no longer a goal but a
“means to perform the character of the dance.

" EXAMPLES:
Sad Romantic (Waltz), Legs and Feet (Tango), Floor patterns (Foxtrot), Light (Viennese Waltz), Foot conversation (Quickstep),

Direct (Cha Cha), Impulse (Samba), Seduction (Rumba), Arms (Paso Doble) and Syncopate (Jive).
Personal interpretations can also be found by consciously building up a clear picture of the character of the dance,

EXAMPLE

Until the mid-nineties the accent on body parts in Tango was mostly seen as the shaping of the upper torso. The dynamics
were mostly strong, direct and bound. Some couples went back to the roots of competitive Tango: the Milonga and the
Argentine Tango. They gave the Tango extra soul and more accent on the legs and feet than on the upper torso. Also the
dynamics became more versatile: flexible, free and sustained became options. After a. few years the dance world followed this

renewed view on Tango characterization...

5. ACCENTUATE A FITTING PART OF YOUR PERSONALITY

Rumba is Rumba and Tango will always be Tango. But you still have the possibility to make a personal contri-
bution by accentuating a part of your personality for this dance. For some men tuning in with aggression fits
their Tango best. Others might prefer to play with being unpredictable or being selfish. Some women might see

themselves as more seductive while others would prefer an arrogant or a distant approach.

EXAMPLES
Caring (Waltz), Improvise (Tango), Vulnerable (Foxtrot), Ostrich (Viennese Waltz), Happy-go-lucky (Quickstep), Madonna
(Cha Cha), Beachboy (Samba), Provocative (Rumba), Egoistic (Paso Doble}, Elvis Presley (Jive).

It is important to understand that you are not arrogant yourself when you choose this personality for Tango. It
is not a private feeling but a consciously chosen characteristic with which you are able to tune in. Actor Anthony
Hopkins didn’t need to be a killer himself to play the role of Dr. Hannibal Lecter in the Academy Award win-
ning movie ‘The Silence of the Lambs’, Hopkins was capable of identifying himself with someone who could kill
scrupulously. If you could bring out different personalities easily then this could be a great choice for selecting

key words.

ALWAYS PHRASE YOUR KEY WORDS SHORTLY AND CLEARLY
When you have found the subject matter for your key word you need to phrase it. A one word proposition (or
a few words) makes the thought easily accessible. Make the phrasing very clear for what it is about. 'Proud’

might sound great but what are you proud of? Are you proud of yourself? Proud of her? Proud about the two of

Concentration by using key words

i

you? Or should it be self-conscious? Or even arrogance (too much pride about yourself...) Make sure the key

word describes exactly what you really want to portray.

ALWAYS PHRASE YOUR KEY WORDS POSITIVELY
Phrase the key words always in a positive way. Don’t think for instance ‘Don’t be heavy’, but replace it with
something like Feel like a feather’. This key word not only gives a positive feeling and energetic toning, but will

also give you a chance to sense or visualize it positively.

Ke¥Y WORDS ARE PERSONAL WORDS
No two dancers are alike. Also the role of the man and the woman do differ in each dance. So there is no need
to have the same words for both man and woman. For instance: in Paso Doble the man might want to think of

"Energy from the ankles’ while his partner would like to think of the key word ‘Resistance’ or 'Pride’.

FOR €ACH DANCE A DIFFERENT KEY WORD

Select a different key word for each of the dances you perform. Step | and 2 mark the ending of the previous
dance. Step 3 - The Cleaning Breath - cleans your body and mind so you can make a new start for the next dance,
Step 4 - Concentration - is the first step to prepare yourself for what will come soon. If you would use the same
key word for Quickstep as for Foxtrot then it will be difficult to really let go of the past. In this case the Foxtrot.
Therefore you need a different key word for each dance.

There is another reason why each dance needs a different key word. The body toning and energy distribution of

each dance is different. Step 4 prepares you for Step 5: Energy. Therefore Step 4 needs to differ too.

USE KEY WORDS FOR A LONG TIME

You probably have created a habit of spending your time ineffectively just before the next dance starts (e.g, drink-
ing water, brushing your shoes, talking to your partner, finding a starting position, etc...) It will take some time
before the Autonomous Nervous System can recognize any new behaviour as a desired pattern. It takes even
more time to overrule the old system with the new one. Consequently you would be better off if you use your
key words for a year or maybe a few years.

Concentration by using key words helps you to create a positive mental habit. Whenever you are under stress
the A.N.S. brings you back to this habit if it knows you can control it. Therefore you better use the same key

words over a long period.

KEY WORDS ARE NOT THE DANCE ITSELF

A key word is not the dance itself. When your subject matter for the next championship changes - or when for
instance your choreography changes - you do not need to change the key word. The key word only mentally
opens the door quickly and easily to the room of the next dance. Whatever you do in that room - metaphorical-

ly speaking - is totally up to you.

Part One. The Competition Day

i

KEY WORDS NEED CHECK-UPS

Maybe once in a while you want to check whether your key words are still working for you. Have a look at the
example on selecting a Cha-Cha key word further on in this chapter. The dancer in this example used her Cha-
Cha key word too automatically and had forgotten the essence of her choice... :

After a year or so you might feel you have progressed so much that you would like to have new key words to match your
new-found skills. Maybe you are right, but be careful: it takes quite some time to create a habit on new key words. Perhaps
changing one of them would be better than working on five (or ten) new key words at the same time.

ACTIVATE YOUR SENSES TO FREE YOU FROM STRESS

What should you do with your key words? Should you just repeat the selected word during the Concentration
step? Would there be any power in that? Certainly. It would take away the thoughts from the competition stress.
However it does not yet replace stress by something powerful and inspirational.

Use your key word as an incentive to produce a positive feeling both in your mind and in your body. To get the
desired feeling in your body, the key word should not just be repeated over and over again. Good key words
inspire you to make contact with your body through your senses. Some key words activate pictures in your mind
{sight), others increase your tactile sensitivity (touch) or your muscular feeling or kinaesthetic experience
(awareness of your body and its movements in space). Others might inspire you to create an inner rhythm (feel-
ing and hearing). Some people get energized by remembering the sound of the voice of an inspirational teacher
(hearing). There are people who are inspired by the memory of a scent (smell) and some are stimulated by the
taste of their lipstick or the memory of another taste. All these sources are welcome because you can control

them and create them on demand (Energy follows thought!’).

SOME DRAWBACKS TO THE USE OF KEY WORDS
Key words can help you to improve your performance because they take you away from all kind of stress build-

ing thoughts. There are, however, some drawbacks too.

Don't stop working on technique
In the beginning the focus on key words probably leads to more success. But do not consider stopping working
on the technical dance requirements because of such positive effects. Without developing the technique any fur-

ther, you will not improve your dancing skills, only your performing skills.

Don't use the key words all the time

You do not need to use key words each and every moment in a lesson or during a practice. Restrict the use of
key words to the moments you really want to excel. In a practice the use of key words could be very effective
when you simulate a final.

There are other moments when you want to use your technical and performing abilities to the maximum. These moments
might be championships, shows or even the moment when you want to show your teacher what you have learned. The

key words will help you to use existing skills. You can also check if new skills are fully mastered.

Concentration by using key words

Key words can create more stress

The complete Nine Step Connection Model® takes a lot of experience to make it work for you. If you fail to do the required
work and also fail to practise it regularly then you might have created a new source of stress: you will realize more than ever
before what is missing and you might activate your critical mind telling yourself that you are no good. The critical mind - also
called The Inner Critic’ - activated at the moment of dancing never helped anyone to dance freely... Do not activate it. Start

working on one dance only and when that feels OK, proceed to the following dance.

FINDING YOUR KEY WORDS
It takes some time to find the right key words. Fortunately it is not a very difficult job at all. Just follow the next nine exer-

cises. Take one dance at a time, Begin with your opening dance, or your best dance or even your worst dance.

Have a look at all your weaknesses
Which is the weakness you know you can correct if you just focus on it well? That is the one you will try out.
Now phrase the key word in a positive thought: change ’My head is tilted backwards’ into, for instance, ‘Stretch

the spine’ or 'Focus forwards’.

Have a look at your strenghts
Discuss the considered strengths with your partner and coach to check if they are real. While you are talking you
may find that you are more enthusiastic about one of these strengths. That is probably your best choice. Phrase

that strength into a clear thought.

Have a look at the character of the dance

Discuss the character with your partner and coach to check if your ideas are correct. Maybe you need some extra
information. You could read books, watch videos or even use lessons with your teachers to just talk about the char-
acter of the dance you are currently working on. Or imagine you have to describe the character of the dance to a
ballet dancer who doesn’t know anything about ballroom dancing. What would you say to him or her?

While you are talking about the character of the dance you may find that you are more enthusiastic about one

of the aspects you mention. That is probably your best choice. Now phrase that character into a clear thought.

Have a look at the personality you want to depict in this dance
Be precise, Do you want to be ’Proud’? That's fine. Let’s now find out what you want to be proud of? Proud of

yourself, of her or the connection? Write down what your choice is.
Now you have four possible key words: one on weaknesses, one on strengths, one on characterization and one

on personality. The mental work is done. Now comes the time to find out on the floor what really works.
Pick twenty seconds of choreography
Just dance it a few times on your own and then together. The next thirty minutes you will not talk but will dance

the selected choreography in a highly concentrated way.

Part One: The Competition Day

Both start with your key word for your weakness

Take a Cleaning Breath (Step 3). Think about your key word {Step 4) and translate that key word into a physic-
al feeling in your body (Step 5). Make an eye connection with your partner (Step 8) and start dancing together
the twenty seconds of choreography. Repeat this a few times. Do not discuss the result with your partner. Just

be aware of how it felt for yourself.

Work on the other three key words
Now do the same with the other three key words, One by one. Do Steps 3-4-5-8 followed by twenty seconds of
dancing. Do this a few times for one key word before you jump to the next one. Do not discuss the result with

your partner. Just be aware of how this key word felt for you.

Discuss the results

Take some time for yourself before you start sharing your experience with your partner. Key words are person-
al so you do not want to be influenced ty your partner's reaction. Start the sharing when you have a clear idea
which words worked well for you and which didn’t. The answers might seem conflicting. You might feel that
the third exercise (with the key word on characterization) worked best while your partner felt that focusing on
a strength worked best. You both might be right!

Check the right key words
Now only work with the best key word and dance your twenty seconds again a few times (Do steps 3-4-5-8 fol-
lowed by twenty seconds of dancing). Does this work out well? It probably does, but maybe the long span of

concentration was too much for you today. You might not be used to that. Try again tomorrow.

Creative Charles needed to work on a more peaceful approach to his Foxtrot. His interpretation of Foxtrot was a little too

dynamic. Charles saw himself as a hot, inspirational dancer. And he had a creative mind too: Charles had selected all kinds of

fire to inspire him for each of the five Standard dances. For Waltz he used ‘Smothering fire’. For Tango he tuned in with ‘Burn’.

' He activated his body for Viennese Waltz with 'Dance around the fire’. Quickstep was always welcomed by sensing ’Hot coal!"

For Foxtrot Charles created two options: ‘Lava Flow’ and ‘Smoke after fire’. Both of these key words clearly projected the

+i dynamics ‘sustained’ and ‘heavy’. Two qualities he could use well for his Foxtrot.

fs Part One: The Competition Day

The key word ‘Smoke after fire’ helped Charles to tune in with these dynamics in his body before the music would start: This
gave Charles the feeling that the key word was working well. However the key word brought in an old weakness of Charles
too: his posture changed too much. His head tilted upwards too much as if Charles was trying to ‘see where the smoke was
coming from. Therefore the key word was not appropriate. It didn’t bring out the best in Chatles. / . ;
Charles then tried to work with ‘Lava Flow’. The same positive dynamics were induced. Charles became more grounded than
before and could now maintain the right posture. Maybe just because the Lava Flow was on the ground instead ofthe smoke
in the air. While dancing Charles improved his improvisation skills. It was as if he became the lava looking for opportunities

where it is easlest to flow...

POINTS TO TAKE INTO CONSIDERATION

* The visualizations are not necessarily about people. Things can also project the right image for the next dance.

* Having a theme for all five dances can be quite inspirational.

* Do not accept a key word too quickly. It might work for the purpose you had in mind when you started but may also it

brings some side effects you do not want, Do not adopt any key word which causes such side effects. It would cost you a

lot of energy to compensate that new (or old) error.

Hasty Hannah was a very talented young Latin American dancer. She was always full of energy and wanted to work as hard as

After a year a guest teacher told Hannah to her big surprise that she was always too late in Cha-cha. This shocked her. What

tence was 'Be late in order to be fast’. Just changing the meaning of the key word would be a hard, fob for her. Therefore a new
concentration point was needed to give Hannah what she needed. oO

Since Hannah had such a dominant critical and perfectionist mind she was advised to use 2 more emotional key word.
Hannah was asked to think about something that would make her feel happy and have a focus on her legs and feet at the
same time. Finally she remembered that she loved warm socks. Wearing them makes her happy and connect with her limbs,

Hannah decided to have Warm socks’ as her concentration point. It opened up her radiant warm Personality and gave her

control over her feet.

* People with a dominant mental part in their personality are often helped by choosing a key word which is less technical...

EXAMPLE PASO DOBLE
Flexible Philip had a great talent for embodying the dynamics ‘flexible’, ‘free’ and ‘heavy’ in his dancing. Unfortunately, this

implied that he could never catch the character of Paso Doble, The dominant dynamics ‘strong’, ‘sudden’, ‘direct’ and "bound’
1 were his weaknesses and yet so necessary in this dance. His choreographer created a new style of Paso for Philip so he could
turn his weaknesses into assets. There was more ‘stillness’ in the dance than was generally used. ‘Sustainment’ was added and
his skill to perform difficult tricks was used to create a more attacking impression of the whole dance.

i Philip needed time to accept that his Paso would not be the same as those of others. In the beginning his lack of confidence
i

Port One: The Competition Day

made the performance look weaker. To stop his critical mind ("You don't want to be different. Do as others do...’) he needed
3 positive thought to prepare for Paso Doble. In daily life Philip had a tendency to protect himself from other people by with
drawing himself energetically, He just put up an imaginative wall around him. in this private Space, nobody could reach him.
We talked about this quality to see if we could use some of it for his preparation for Paso Doble. Being withdrawn was evidently
too much to use during dancing. Without any connection with his partner she could not survive the dance.

Alternatives qualities for ‘withdrawn’ ~ still holding a clear private space - could be ‘ignore’ or being ‘neutral’ or even being
‘cool’. To ‘ignore’ would mean that Philip would have no connection with his partner. This option was therefore rejected.
Being ‘neutral’ didn’t give Philip enough private space. The choice was made: ‘Cool’ This was the personality part Philip need-
ed to check in with before he started his energetic toning of the body for Paso Doble. His coolness helped him to turn his

weaknesses into assets. His innovative personal Paso style became quite convincing.

POINTS TO TAKE INTO CONSIDERATION

* Philip chose a weakness to work with. The close teamwork of the choreographer and the mental coach helped to create a
new style of Paso Doble which suited his skills and personality.

* Have a close look at the dominant parts in your personal characteristics. It makes it easier to enlarge an accessible part of

your personality than a disowned part.

* In-depth discussions help you to find out what may work for you and what would not.

out any problems. Her other career involved Latin American dancing as an amateur. She loved to dance and to practise for a
good result.

At competitions it was another Wanda who appeared on the floor, Her normal convincing attitude was nowhere to be seen.
it was clear that she suffered from competition stress. This was a new issue: as an aerobic dancer she had done - and won -

many competitions and never felt stressed about doing that. Her first thought was that she wasn’t used to doing competitions

Port One: The Competition Day

with a partner. The stress would just go away. But it didn't. She started to work with the Nine Step Connection Médel® which
“made Aer more free. Jive remained a problem. She felt she lacked the stamina to dance non-stop for ninety seconds. That
ought always bothered her when she had to dance the Jive. It was time to control the mind by giving it a positive thought,
Wanda chose to tune in with the energy she had in the aerobics studio, Each moment she walked into her studio she felt
-immediately energized. We talked about that and found out that the smell of sweat unconsciously triggered her energy sup-
ply. In the venues where the competitions were held, no such smeil was available. More dominant was the smell of perfumes,
body tan and cigarettes. Three things she actually disliked very much.

Wanda chose ‘Smell of sweat’ as her key word. This activated her memory of the smell in the studio and energized her body.

THE (DAILY) PRACTICE

‘Control’ is the crucial word to make The Nine Step Connection Model® free you from stress. The only way to
create such control and freedom is when you make the model a conscious part of your practice life. Repetition
is needed to allow your Autonomous Nervous System help you under stress. This repetition helps your A.N.S, to

select the model (the A.N.S. always brings you back to recognized habits...).

Practice music

Buy the simulation CD' or make a practice tape. Use real finat lengths: for instance 100 seconds waltz, 30 seconds
break, 100 seconds Tango, 30 seconds break, 100 seconds Viennese Waltz, 30 seconds break, 100 seconds
Foxtrot, 30 seconds break and 100 seconds Quickstep.

End each practice session with a full final. Use the Nine Steps between each dance. Try to dance with other cou-
ples on the floor so you get used to their disturbances {or ask somebody to walk in the way during the final sim-

ulation).

The Simulation Evaluation Form

FILUNG IN THE EVALUATION FORM

Take two minutes of rest directly after a simulated final before you fill in the form. Do not discuss the final
with your partner but take care of yourself. Breathe to relax and maybe drink some water.

Fill in the form separately without discussing.

in the column Overall Quality you give yourself a mark for the general quality of the performance from
1-10. Ten points meaning you really got the best out of yourself.

In the column Concentration you fill in two data:

First you write down your key word (the writing down of the key word over and over again will help to
memorize it quicker).

Then you write down how well you concentrated on that key word: 100% means excellent concentration,
0% means that you have totally forgotten it.

In the column Invitation you state whether you made a personal conscious connection with your partner
before you started to dance together. Just note what you did and not how your partner reacted. So if you
opened yourself up by making eye contact and by sensing all the physical contact points the starting hold

offered, you can say ‘yes’.

Evaluate the simulations

LEARNING FROM THE EVALUATION

Notice that the key words are different per dance. Also observe that both partners have different key words.
Notice that both partners have a mix of strategies in their key words: sometimes to compensate a weakness
of accentuate a strength, sometimes to depict the dance character and sometimes to get in touch with a part
of their personality.

Both partners can have different opinions on the perception of the quality of the performance. Do not discuss
who is right. You are both right.

Stop blaming your partner for low quality dancing: analyse where your own problems are. Maybe step 4,
maybe step 8 or both.

Notice that you wiil not have a good quality score when step 4 or step 8 were not evaluated as sufficient. {If
you do not have a good beginning you will not perform your best)...

You lack experience with the model if you have a quality average over the five dances of less than 8. Stress
has taken over and you have not yet created a positive habit of doing all nine steps of the model well. Be glad
that this is just a simulation. Keep on simulating regularly to prevent such a low quality level also is produced
during competitions.

Notice the decrease in quality when one of the two didn’t do the steps right.

_ After a few evaluations, you will be able to see what dances give you more problems in concentrating and

partnering than others.

When the concentration step has been marked low, you might need to ask yourself if you have done step 3
- The Cleaning Breathe - sufficiently enough. Without the Cleaning Breath it is often hard to get rid of stress
and make contact with your key word!

Notice that the problems during the final are hardly ever caused by lack of technique. Your technical skills
cannot function freely when step 4 and 8 are not done properly.

Have a look at your stamina: you might discover that your stamina is not yet sufficiently developed. (This will

clearly show if you see a regular decline over the five dances each time you evaluate yourself).

The Nine Step Connection Model? was originally developed for simulating and improving the performance qual-
ity in finals. Over time the model also proved to be very effective in preliminary rounds. In this chapter the model

is explained for three different situations:

FINALS WITH OPEN JUDGING

After the applause, the dancers do not waik to the starting position of the next dance - they have to go to the

side of the floor to receive the marks there. The order of the nine steps stays as it was:

HOW DO YOU APPROACH THE VISUAL MARKING OF THE JUDGES?

It is impossible to ignore the visual marking of the judges completely. Of course you are curious to see how your
performance will be rewarded. If the marks are good, it could give you a positive incentive (although it could
also make you too self-confident). If the results of a dance are not as good as expected, most of you will feel the
pressure to have to perform better. This incentive is extrinsic and doesn’t contribute to a better performance in

general. You do not control the judges and therefore stress appears. Your mind and body need controllable

stimuli.

Stage One .
You need to learn to stop your mind thinking about the consequences of the markings. You need to focus now

Part One: The Competition Doy

on what is coming instead of what has been. When the judges mark other couples you work consciously on steps
3, 4 and S. You only allow yourself to see and process the information when you step forward to receive your

marks. From the moment you step back into the line you discipline yourself to work on steps 3, 4 and 5 again.

Stage Two

When you have mastered stage One well you can go one step further. When you step forward you no longer
process the marks but only concentrate on relaxation and receiving the applause at the end of your marks. When
you have disciplined yourself in this way you do not have to compensate any stress derived from the marks. You
will be able to directly concentrate on the key word for the next dance. Stage Two is hard to accomplish. Most
of the time your natural curiosity will override the better tactic (not to consume the information before the fifth
dance has finished). Stage One gives you the space to quickly fulfil the need for information and then immedi-
ately leave it behind you with steps 3, 4 and 5.

CLOSED FINALS

In closed finals the time between the dances is much shorter than in finals with visual marking after each dance.
You will not leave the floor to receive the marks. You will stay on the floor and select your new starting position
directly after the applause. You now need to relax much quicker during - and directly after - receiving the
applause. The man will lead his partner directly to the new position. Therefore step 6 (Relationship) and step 7
(Positioning) will follow directly after the first three steps (Ending of the dance). Once at the starting position

steps 3 to 9 will follow in the normal order (without steps 6 and 7 since you are already at the right spot).

EXCUSES, EXCUSES...

The expression ’ Winners have plans. Losers have excuses.” is not only valid prior to - and during - the compe-
tition but also afterwards: when evaluating the competition.

It is quite safe for you to evaluate your competition by referring to your partner, the judges, the floor, the music
and the atmosphere. These factors take away the focus on yourself, Mostly such an evaluation offers excuses and
takes away your own responsibility and opportunity to learn and create a winner's plan for the next event.
Stop blaming uncontrollable factors. Many more factors influence your performance but if you do not really look
into them, you don't learn from them either. You might be making the same mistakes over and over again.

You will only learn from competitions when you really open yourself to what is going on. You could stop blam-
ing others and start identifying the obstacles you yourself can learn to overcome yourself.

Donnie Burns MBE phrases his ideas about ’winning’ very clearly. On many occasions he has said: “If you fail to

prepare, you prepare to fail’. In this chapter you will find out how well you usually prepare yourself...

CHECK THE VALUE OF FEEDBACK FROM OTHERS

You could learn to interpret the information which is freely given by others. ’Is the information valuable? Does
the commenting person have your personal interest at heart? Is the commenting person competent to tell you
what worked well and what didn’t? Is the commenting person really concerned about you? Is the comment just
social behaviour?”

Check these questions before taking commenis of others for truth. Do not open yourself to those people without

honest intentions and knowledge. Only use the valid comments for your evaluation of the competition day.

Having lessons in the week prior to a competition can have positive effects. The value of the lessons highly
depends on your teacher. The more your teacher sees the lessons as mental and emotional support (instead of
learning something new} the better the effect will be. Some teachers however tend to only stress faults and work
on those points. New information takes time to integrate into your performance. Consequently, learning some-
thing new is not advised in the week(s) prior to an important competition.

Effective coaches polish up the existing skills (which may have slipped away a little]. They accentuate what you
can do well instead of focusing on what is still missing.

You can also check for yourself whether having lessons from more than one teacher in the preparation week

helps your mindset. Different teachers do have different views. This could lead to unnecessary stress.

Part One: The Competition Day

Competition dancing is something you do together. How do you partner each other during the whole day? Your
needs for partnering between rounds may differ since the personalities of the two of you are not alike. There are
no general rules for the way you arrive and behave between rounds. One of you might like to socialize while
the other needs isolation. Knowing and accepting these different approaches can take away a lot of stress.
Couples who need different approaches should talk it over and make arrangements when to be together and

when not. For instance, to only be together half an hour before the next round to do the warm-up together.

Some dancers need a coach more than others. Try to find out how this works for you. If you feel that you need
a coach, you could first look into the reasons why. The need for a coach’s presence can have both good and bad
backgrounds. You could ask yourself the following questions: ’Did you practise the competition simulation on
your own and also miss the presence of the coach? Did you miss the coach to arrange things for you so you could
Isolate and stay out of the competition stress? Did you need a coach to feel politically more powerful?”

The availability of professional coaches is quite small. Realize that working with a coach also means that you
will have to give in order to receive the attention of a coach. A proactive attitude towards dancing and towards
the desired coach may be the best way to find one. Waiting for a coach to make himself/herself available will

not be the way to find one.

Some couples have a well-planned strategy which ensures they have as many supporters as possible for major
competitions. The larger the number of supporters, the more energy and self esteem they seem to get out of it.
It is fine to get a kick out of the behaviour of supporters but you should not depend on them. The quality of your
performances should come from. within. From controllable factors (like used in The Nine Step model). From the
moment you have mastered the nine steps it doesn’t really matter how many supporters you have (or the other
finalist) as long as you have reached an effective and controllable level of concentration...

In recent years several couples became world champions with only a handful of supporters in the ballroom. Other
challengers for the vacant titles had over half of the audience as supporters. The winning couples found the
power to perform well from within. The others might have felt the pressure to have to perform well in front of
their supporters. This stress might have been the reason that they did not dance to the best of their abilities. It

might have taken away from their dancing and made them lose by themselves...

The demand for extra effort from your body is freely accepted by the Autonomous Nervous System when your
body has energy. If not, your body will feel threatened and will send stress-causing signals via the A.N.S. Good
sleep is not a luxury but a necessity. Without enough sleep it is also more difficult to concentrate. Negative

thoughts (an important stress-causing factor) arise more easily in a tired body than in a relaxed body.

Biorythm

More than eight hours sleep will not lead to better performances. An average sleep should therefore last between
seven and eight hours per day. Have a look at your biorhythm. Most people have a fixed time-interval when they go
to sleep and when they wake up. Two hours before your natural sleeping moment your body starts to cut down the
energy and adrenaline levels. To calm you down and to prepare you for your sleep. But what happens if your final
round is planned at 23.00 hours and your natural sleeping moment is at midnight? You now have to perform to the
maximum during a period when your body is already reducing your energy levels. Dancing a final at that time cer-
tainly activates your Autonomous Nervous System. Your demand is feit as a threat. Prevent such stress-causing situ-

ations by changing your biorhythm weil in advance and simulate dancing finals during that period at real final time...

. “PRACTICE QUESTIONS

*# Did you simulate the semicfinal dr Final in ys

competition time schedule?

Some competitions - The Open British Championships for example - have many rounds before you get into the final.
If your body is not used to excel during, for example, six rounds with long intervals on one day, the same problem
might arise as when your biorhythm is out of order. Round by round, your A.N.S. will feel more and more threat-
ened and will increase the resistance to have your body work so hard. You are therefore advised to simulate most of
the rounds 3 or 4 days before the competition at competition times. Your A.N.S. is then able to find out that such a

demand doesn’t really affect your body much and will not react so strongly on the crucial competition day.

Competition evaluation

The warm-up is an essential preparation for your body to perform well when extra effort is demanded. Without

this warm-up the body could feel threatened. Your body could react by sending stress signals via the Autonomous.
Nervous System.

Warm-ups help to prevent injuries. Warmed up muscles are more extensible and therefore less vulnerable to
injuries. Warm-ups also reduce the chance of muscle-ache after the competition.

Muscles are warmed up by the blood that runs through the veins. The excess blood also provides the necessary
oxygen that is needed for muscle activity. A good warm-up slowly increases the speed of the blood circulation
through the veins. What is important is that all crucial muscles are warmed up gradually before the real per-
formance starts. Warm-ups do not only prepare the muscles but also the heart, the lungs and the tendons. Good

warm-ups ensure that all four body parts are prepared to function efficiently,

You need more than a physical warm-up
Next to the physical need for warm-up your mind also needs to be prepared for what is coming. And even your
emotions need to be taken care of before you can start sharing your dance with another person. In chapter 2-8-1

you can read more about warming up your whole human being.

How much time?
Generally top dancers take thirty to sixty minutes for the first warm-up of the day. Second and third warm-ups
{after breaks caused by the schedule of the event) could be done in ten to fifteen minutes. A minimal warm-up

for other couples takes about fifteen to twenty minutes. Second and third warm-ups could be done in eight to

ten minutes.

Best spots for warm-ups

Fresh air helps to condition your body. You could start your warm-up outside the venue with some running, jog-
ging and stretching exercises. In most cases it will not be easy to jog around the venue. Most of the time you
will have to do the warm-up entirely inside the venue. Fortunately warm-ups do not need a lot of space. Four

square metres is sufficient for you. Most dressing rooms will provide such space.

Insecure feelings?

Many dancers feel the need to warm up but they also feel insecure doing it because it is not (yet) a general trend.
You could help yourself by having a fixed routine that helps you to concentrate. You could even wear a Walkman
to feel more at ease. You can help each other by doing the same routine together at the same time. Together you

would feel stronger and it could also improve your partnering.

round up to the final. Some top couples have experienced the power of massage and have a masseur in their

team for important championships.

Port One: The Competition Day

Some coaches - especially in team sports - find sex a disturbing factor. It not only takes away energy but also the
desired single minded focus. In soccer, for example, lots of the national teams prepare themselves for world
championships in complete isolation. The wives are not permitted to join the players to prevent loss of energy

and/or concentration... Become aware of the impact sex has on you.

Most venues for competitive dancing do not provide the perfect climate for top performers. It is often too warm,
too humid and unfortunately people smoke there ail day long. The venues are also overcrowded with people so
it might be hard to find a private spot.

If you are sensitive to the condition of the air and the amount of private space, you could consider leaving the

venue between rounds to fill up your lungs with fresh air, and enjoy some space to for yourself.

INTERPRETATION OF THE PLANNING FACTORS

ACCUMATIZING ¢

‘Did you airive ja time ot

Ask yourself the following questions: ‘Did we arrive in time at the venue? Did we have enough time to adjust
to time differences, climate differences? Did we know the venue well enough to feel at ease?’
Studies have shown that people perform better if they know the room well. There are several ways to get to

know new places quickly. Reread chapter 1-2 for suggestions.

Competition evaluation

if you find in your pattern that you are always too late, you could be in trouble. Being late is often a stress-caus-
ing factor. It doesn’t feel good. It takes away a chance to do the necessary preparations. Check the different needs

in the partnership and work out an agreeable planning that suits both of you.

INTERPRETATION OF THE DIRECT DANCING FACTORS

Lack of stamina produces a general pattern. The more rounds to dance the worse it gets (note that this pattern
could also arise due to other causes). Another clear pattern that indicates lack of stamina: when you can dance
the first dance well in a semi-final of final but the quality of the performance drops dance after dance.

You should look into the reasons for your lack of stamina. The whole evaluation form is filled with questions that
concern your stamina (sleep, food, workload, genera! condition, etc.} Find your personal reason and work out
that problem (say, by ending each practice by dancing a Nine Step Connection final). Also read the example on

finding Samba key words in chapter 1-5.

Dancing without feeling the movement, the music and/or the connection with your partner will not lead to top

performances. The Nine Step Connection Modei® has been developed to help overcome the lack of such feelings.

Part One: The Competition Day

There are many other techniques to become more aware of and control such feelings, like kinetic movement
training, Alexander technique and the Five Rhythms of Gabrielle Roth.

New choreography in the week or weeks before a competition doesn’t provide most dancers with self-confi-
dence. These dancers need a well-rehearsed routine before they feel safe in performing it. Read chapter 2-3 for

more suggestions on this subject.

i

It is important to know your technical abilities well. Your teachers/coach could help you to find out if there is

E a difference in the technical qualities in the studio and on the competition floor.
' Bad competition results could be caused by lack of technique. This is the case if the general opinion is that your
level of technique in the studio is also not sufficient compared to the other direct competitors. In this case, you

should work harder on technique than you already have.

INTERPRETATION OF EXTERNAL FACTORS

You can learn not to be influenced by the members of the board of adjudicators. In some cases you might feel
that the board of adjudicators is biased. If you have good reasons for that you could decide not to enter such a
competition. If more couples would act in this way, these boards would no tonger exist.

It is unwise for you to let yourself be upset by the presence of members of the board of adjudicators. Just because

your abilities. This only happens when you learn to focus on yourself and your dancing (see The Nine Step

Familiarity.

} Competition evaluation
i

People perform better in venues they know because there is less uncertainty, The Autonomous Nervous System
likes certainties. Help yourself to feel at home in a venue as soon as possible. Using your imagination and acti-

vating all senses does shorten the time needed to feel free in a room. Reread chapter 1-2 for more suggestions

on this subject.

The floor will be the same for all competitors. Blaming the floor for bad results is therefore not realistic. The first
remedy against a slippery floor is balance. Yet, even well-balanced couples will find it hard to dance on very slip-
pery floors. The remedy does not lie in brushing your shoes between each of the dances. Most of the time the
floor will feel slippery again after a few steps.

The water remedy can help sometimes. However, do not wet your shoes during the final. It is preparation work
that you should do in the dressing room. You can find out well before the competition starts whether the floor

is too slippery and take the necessary steps in the dressing room.

Music can be a great inspiration for you to perform well. Unfortunately not all events use inspiring music. You

could try to find out what kind of music (or which orchestra) will be used and practise with that music. This
helps you to perform better. Even when you do not appreciate what is being played.
Sometimes it is impossible to find out the kind of music that will be used. Just take some time to listen to it before

the first round begins. See what you can use and accept the music before you start.

INTERPRETATION OF PEOPLE INTERACTION

It is nice to get a kick out of the audience's reactions but you should not rely on it. The quality of your per-

formance must come from within.

exXAMPLE

Some dancers have lost their desired title because they hoped the audience would react to a new, daring piece of choreog-
raphy. Their focus was not on performing the piece but on the reactions of the audience. Those reactions were not forth-
coming and that upset the dancers a lot. Their Autonomous Nervous System had to interfere with this uncontrollable desire.
Their stress level grew and consequently the quality of their performance dropped. They lost themselves.

In later championships they focused on the performing skills needed to dance that daring plece of choreography. They did:
nt focus on the impact it could have. On these occasions the audience reacted positively to the same piece of choreography

(now performed without stress...)

You do not help yourself by comparing your weak points with the strong points of others. This leads to negative

thinking and causes stress. In fact, you shouldn’t have any thoughts about the other couples on a competition
day. Learn to appreciate your own strengths and focus on your own dancing. That’s what the Nine Step

Connection Model® is about.

Your positioning on the floor is important. You could make a decision to find the most available spot on the floor.
You could strategically move to be more visible to the judges or audience, You could even try to dominate your
main competitor (or get out of the way). Realize that your body likes habits. Good habits make things easier.
Help your body and mind by choosing the starting point of each dance before the competition starts. After a few
rounds your body gets used to the fixed starting points.

nes
Select the starting points well in advance. This will reduce stress. It will give you a clear focus of where to go next. This will
give a good impression on the floor.

Starting a dance at the same spot in each round makes the judges always see the same part of your routine. In competitions with

many rounds this might make you look boring. In these situations you might want to start each round with a different part of your

Competition evaluation

routine. Or you could start the dances in opposite corners per round {if you prefer to always start with the same routine).
Whatever strategy you choose, it is important to make a quick and clear decision about where you are going to commence

your next dance and to move into that position.

Reread step 7 - The Positioning - of the Nine Step Connection Model® in chapter 1-4.

"Chapter 2-1

ACTIVITY PLANNING
FOR
THE SEASON

MANAGING A BUSINESS

This part of the workbook focuses on the activities you have to engage in during a season. Competing demands
more than only training your body. You have to plan ahead (lessons, practices, travel, competitions), you need to
be creative (choreography, outfits), you want to stay inspired and so on. A comparison with running a business
is quite easy to make. Without proper planning no company can take care of all that is needed to survive. And
those companies, who do survive without structure, mostly feel that they are struggling.

Asa dancer you have an even more difficult challenge than most business people: you have to Manage most activ-
ities yourself. You do not have a strategy planner, a financial controller, a publicity agency, a purchasing depart-
ment, a production unit or a marketing Manager. You just have to manage it all by yourself.

The more activities you have to do, the more you need to know where you are today and where you want to be

in future. This is the starting point for seasonal planning.

You may have already worked out your Long Term Career Planning (Part Three of this book) and used that infor-
mation to set your seasonal goals. You also may start with a seasonal plan now and work on your long term career
planning later. Whatever order you choose: do set feasible goals' that you can achieve in one season. These goals
will direct your use of time, money, energy, people in your team, look, choreography, competition selection, prac-
tising, etc. Each activity will be deait with chapter by chapter.

You cannot control your life but. you can make it easier by avoiding unnecessary hindrances. The more activities

you have planned well, the less unnecessary stress occurs.

SEASONAL GOAL SETTING
Itis not very hard to set your goals for each new season. Take some time to answer the questions below. Maybe

you first want to read all of them and relax and think - or meditate - about what you need for the next season. Goal setting

Part Two: The Season

cannot be hurried. When, you feel stressed at the moment you set your goals you will not get a clear picture of what you
Teally need on all four human levels: physically, mentally, emotionally and spiritually.

Since you are part of aicouple it might be wise to first think it over yourself. Take some time and answer the
questions below. Start sharing your vision with others when you have a rough idea of what suits you. You will

find the discussions with your partner and coach easier when you have made your plan first.

Activity planning for the season

Most of the answers you might find easily. For others you might need information that can be found in the following chapters,

BE REALISTIC

Gefore you start planning the season you first need to check your ideas with your partner and your coach. Discuss
the differences to find common ground between you. Check if the answers to questions | and 2 are realistic.
Take your talent, available time and money into account. Realize that other competitors may have such resources

too. An honest coach or teacher could make you see what is realistic and what isn’t.

EXAMPLE

You are a semi-finalist and want to reach the national final at the end of the next season. You and your partner have the tal-
ent to reach that goal but the time and energy is not sufficient. Due to your study or work you can only afford eight hours of
dancing a week. Your main competitors already work fifteen hours a week.

Your goals might be set too high. To feel satisfied at the end of the season it would be better to either lower ‘your goals or to

Jind a way to increase the available time. You probably need to increase the effectiveness of the time too.

THE STRATEGIC PLANNING FORM
On the following pages you will see how a world amateur finalist couple has planned their next season. In
Appendix 2-10 you will find clean strategic planning forms to be filled in by you and your partner together. This
is how you could fill in the forms:

Make a priority list of the 4-5 major competitions,

Put vertical red lines on the dates of priority competition dates.

Spread the priority competitions well over the whole season.

Put vertical black lines on the other competition dates.

Make A priority list of 4-5 subject matters for the whole season.

Focus between each priority competition on one subject matter only.

Fill in all activities and check the totals ‘time’ and ‘money’ against what you can afford.

MANAGING A SEASON IS LIKE MANAGING A BUSINESS
All the activities you have to take care of as a dancer equals the activities of a managing director of a company.

It is almost impossible to execute all your tasks on your own. You need a team around you to help you out with

many of the following activities:

Choreography a:

Technique

Personal interpretation of your dances

= Effective practice
Look: outfits, hairstyle and make-up ; :

Competition selection

Competition preparations a

Travelling

Finance

Coaching before, during and after championships '
Supporting dance activities: jazz ballet, mambo, salsa, melonga, Argentine tango, tap dance, dance expression, etc.
Supporting physical activities: aerobics, fitness, massage, chiropractor, dietician, tanning

Supporting mental activities: study, mental training, etc.

Supporting inspirational activities: reading books, going to the theatre, watching videos and movies, etc. “}
Media training (preparing you for interviews) .
Paid publicity (for professionals) . :

Creating and presenting lectures (for professionals)

Demonstrations and shows

Et cetera

You may have to manage it all by yourself or you may find a team of people around you who could help you to }

execute tasks. Help you to make the right decisions. And help you stay on track. Such a team is not easily found 4
nor built. It takes dedication to find the right team members. It needs a willingness to invest in all team mem. +

bers. It is not just using people. It is about giving and taking, so all members are rewarded.

CONFLICTING ROLES: TEACHER, COACH, FRIEND AND JUDGE
Teachers, choreographers and coaches play a dominant role in the primary team of a dance couple. Often the

roles overlap and sometimes these roles seem conflicting. Is the teacher only teacher or is he or she considered
coach as well? Or is the teacher a personal friend too? Roles may even become more difficult to control when
the teacher/coach/ friend also becomes judge in a major competition or championship. People often find it hard

to distinguish the different roles a person takes on. 4

Part Two: The Season

EXAMPLE 1

You feel your teacher {s your coach and also your friend. You trust him totaly. Professional and private expectations are now
mixed and thus unclear. Your 'coach’ has been asked to judge your national championship. You are in a position to win the
title for the first time, You feel lucky your coach is judging. A new talented couple also enters the champlonship. In the opin-
ton of your coach this couple is better in four of the ive dances than you are, And he marks accordingly. He proves he ts able
to separate the roles of being a teacher, a coach, a friend and a judge. You on the other hand experience his marks as a betray:
al, Due to his marks you did not win. A friend would never do that. Nor a coach. At least, not in your opinion. You get emo-

tional about his infidelity and decide to stop working with this unreliable person.

The previous happens all the time. It is one of the reasons dancers jump from teacher to teacher in the hope to
find someone who will answer all their needs and will be loyal without boundaries. In whatever role that per-
son takes on. Both dancers and teachers can put a stop to this ineffective behaviour. The easy way out in the
above example would be that the teacher/coach/friend would not judge his own students. This might look like
the ideal situation. The reality is still that the roles are mixed and overlap. There needs to be another way. Also

because other mix-ups can occur. Even when we take out the role of the judge.

EXAMPLE 2

Again you feel your teacher Is also your coach and your friend. You trust him totally. Professional and private expectations are
very mixed. During your national championship your teacher is not judging. He is present to support all his couples. Your
teacher is actually very successful and works with four of the six finalists. His task to personally coach each and every one of
them is physically impossible. He has to divide his time equally amongst all four couples. Emotionally you need a lot of sup-
port. You feel left out each time your teacher is talking to the other couples, You even imagine that he is more personally
involved with the others and that you only get the leftovers. That is not enough for you. You get stressed. Your dancing suf:

‘fers and the results are consequently not what you had hoped for.

EXAMPLE 3

You feel you do not need a coach during competitions at all. At your championship everything goes very well until one of your
teachers drops by. You haven't worked with him for months. He ts not aware of what you have been working on but tells you
that you are not good enough today. He tells you that you must create more stiliness and pay more attention to your timing.
He was not asked to give this information. He just dumped it since he regards himself not only as your teacher but as your
coach too. Stress now occurs: you have been working on dynamics for the last two months. He didn’t notice that at all. Are
you on the right track? Is his advice correct? Do you need to make changes in just a few minutes? Are you able to make such

changes? You haven't practised with his preferred subfect matters...

All three examples show the need for clarity in the roles teachers take on and what dancers expect of them.
Dancers and teachers need to analyse what they need from different people and what they are willing to give.
Both parties need clear communication on what is expected and what can be given (not every teacher wants to

be both a teacher and a coach. And some can’t}.

Teamwork during the season

THE ROLE OF THE TEACHER

Dance teachers have the primary task to help you on the physical plane: they analyse what is not working for
you and help you to improve your movement skills and routines. You do not pay your teachers just to hear what
you do well. You want them to help you to become better. Confirmations are only needed when new skills are
introduced and you have proved you can master them. Immediately followed by a new challenge: what is wrong
or missing right now and how can you improve that. The teacher’s major focus is on the glass being half empty

and how to make the glass more full.

The glass is half empty:

what you need to improve

Secondary tasks of dance teachers involve tailormade communications. Not every dancer receives the informa-
tion of the glass being half empty in the same way. About a quarter of all dancers are inspired by straightforwardly
being told what they do wrong and how they can improve that. They are the Aspirers and Succeeders' amongst
the dancers. Other dancers feel more isolated in life and have a greater need for confirmation on what they do weil.
They are called the Strugglers and the Resigned. They need connection and information to be put in a clear posi-

tive way.

EXAMPLE
In her previous lesson Struggling Frances was taught a new principle. Frances and her partner Quintus (Aspirer) have prac-
tised it and now come for their next lesson. They show what they have practised and their teacher tells them ‘that wasn’t bad’.
Frances perceives this comment as ‘that was bad again’. This confirms her that she will never master what is really needed.
She loses her self-confidence. At the same time Quintus, the Aspirer, hears the teacher say that it is quite OK...

Now the teacher might have really meant ‘that was quite OK after a first practice week’. Strugglers need to be told so in that
clear way! This way of communicating does not imply that the teacher has to lie or to make things better than they are.
Strugglers and Resigned have a strong sense of what is honestly meant and what not. The teacher has to take care how he or

she phrases and communicates the information.

Mainstreamers like the socializing moments most in life. A teacher with a constant focus on improvements will
miss a real connection with these dancers. They also need small talk in order to stay connected and stay willing
to improve.

Explorers and Reformers are innovative dancers. They like to improve and have a great need to be different. A

focus on the glass being half empty is not a problem for them. The teacher does need to be aware of their need

Part Two: The Season

for individuation. He/she should not only tune in with what are the general style demands but also how to bend
style issues to match the.needs of these dancers. Referring to successful examples in the past mostly stops these
innovators from connecting with their teacher. They do not want to be compared with what has already been.

They want to create themselves in a unique way.

THE ROLE OF THE CORCH

The role of the coach has two opposite functions: the focus on seeing the glass half full and on seeing the glass
being half empty. The choice of the role depends on the moment in the season. Most of the time the coach has
the same focus as the teacher. His task is to help you improve and therefore to see what is still missing or need-
ed and help you to take away these faults. He may help you himself (if the coach is also a dance teacher, which
may not be the case) or help you to find specialist to solve the issues at hand,

Exceptions to this focus are the preparation weeks before a major championship and during that championship
(and maybe even a few days afterwards). During the preparation weeks and during the championship it is bene-
ficial for you to feel good about what you already master. This is not the time to jearn more new skills. Any new
learning would probably increase the risk of stress: your mind may accept it but your body lacks the desired rou-
tine in doing it. It is more effective for you to feel free to perform the newly adopted skills of the previous
months. Confirmation creates more self-belief and self-esteem. Coaches now need to focus on the glass being
half full.

The glass is half empty:

what you need to improve

The glass is half full:

what you need to feel good about

The focus on ’the glass being half fulf does not imply that the teacher has to lie or to say that things are better
than they are. A coach can get away with that maybe once, but in the long run his credidibility is at stake. You
will stop trusting your coach if he says positive things during the competition and gives negative feedback after

the competition. Coaches need to look for what went really well to inspire you for further improvement.

EXAMPLE

After the first round the coach is quite disappointed with the performance of Gerald and Betty. So many things are missing.
He could focus on the glass being half empty and say "You lacked stamina, energy and rhythm in all your dances. Wake up.
Get yourself in shape. This really doesn’t work. You were also too late on the. floor. You looked lost and had too much trouble
finding a good spot to start dancing’. This information is not likely to help Gerald and Betty. Everything is put in a negative

way and too many subject matters are addressed.

Teamwork during the season

The coach might also have noticed that the start of the samba was the only thing that went well. The subject matter for the last
two months was rhythm and the coach could help Gerald and Betty focus on that again. His feedback could be ’OK, let’s see
what went well and build on that. Your rhythm in samba was great at the start of the dance. Did you feel that too...? Now let’s
get into your own rhythm before the music starts. Remember the exercises we did the last few weeks.? Singing the rhythm before
you start? That seemed to work well. Take the next few minutes to relax and then play with singing the different rhythms and

build up energy and body toning from there. That's all you need to do to get ready for the next round.’

Humour can help coaches to change a negative attitude in dancers. Around the world? there are some good psy-
chologists who have great results with what is called 'Confrontational humour’. The principle is to not disagree
with a person when he only sees the glass as half empty. The coach does the exact opposite: he presents the glass

as being totally empty. He exaggerates so much that it becomes funny.

EXAMPLE

After the first round the coach is quite happy about the performance of Nick and Blondie. It wasn’t perfect but it was enough.
Blondie comes off the floor and starts complaining that so many things are missing. She focuses on the glass being half empty
and says to Nick "You messed up our dancing. You gave me no chance. | hate that. You were pushing and pulling. I can’t dance
when you are like that.’

This feedback is not likely to help Nick, and Blondie is getting more and more uptight, The coach could disagree with Blondie but
it is unlikely that that changes her opinion at that moment. She is hurt and vulnerable. She needs to be seen and respected.

So give her the confirmation she so evidently needs. Only exaggerate. The coach could say for instance: "Yes Blondie, you are
right. It looked horrible. Like a real beginner. [ didn’t understand what you were doing there. Get out of your dress and go
home, Everybody saw that mistake of Nick's. Nobody will forgive him for that.’ Blondie is now likely to see for herself that she

was exaggerating. She might start laughing and relteve some of the stress.

The pre-condition for ’Confrontational humour’ is that the coach must have a trustworthy relationship with the
dancers. His (or her) intentions must be totally positive. The quality of the communication must be personal

(instead of impersonal or even withdrawn).

THE ROLE OF THE FRIEND

Real friends are great. They accept who you are: both your positive and negative sides. You can rely on them in
any situation. You can trust them totally. Both of you are personally interested in each other and are involved in
each other’s well-being. Such friends are rare. Maybe you have two, three, five or maybe even ten? You certain-
ly do not have tens or hundreds of real friends, do you? The same applies for teachers and coaches. If each and
every client was to be a friend they would lack the time to act as a friend for all of them.

Clients are clients. It is a professional relationship. There is no other choice. Most teachers and coaches work up
to 1200 hours a year. They have tens or hundreds of clients. They would not have time to be friends with all of
them. They can give professional attention. They may do that in a personal way to increase the quality of the les-

son but more is really not feasible.

Part Two: The Season

|

Friendship would not work professionally in most cases either. This would take away the objectivity a teacher
or coach needs in order to help you out. Finally it is too hard for most People to separate the roles. One moment
being the all-accepting friend and the next moment being the critical teacher: it is too hard to swallow for most
people...

MIXED-UP ROLES

The importance of clear roles may also be illustrated when we look at the quality of the telationship between
three parties: the male dancer (Sam), the female dancer (Tanja) and the teacher-coach-friend (Knut). Together
they form a threesome. The relationships between the three parties are not equal on all levels. As is the case

with most threesomes. Let's have a look at the three different roles they take on:

EXAMPLE

Knut, Sam and Tanja have quite a good teacher-student relationship. They all accept each other's roles in this model. Privately
Knut has very warm feelings for Tanja and these feelings are shared by her. So far no problems, The three can work profes-
sionally in the studio, and Knut and Tanja can have more if they so desire (in this case Sam and Tanja have no private rela.

tionship with each other).

Now we add a third role model: Knut is also the coach of Sam and Tanja: Knut Is so involved with Tanja that she takes all his
attention during his coaching. Sam feels left out. Tanja feels smothered with all of Knut’s attention. And Knut realizes that he
has lost his objectivity to coach the two of them well. This situation becomes quite suffocating. It feels like that they are all
stuck and that there is no way out. Sam likes Knut as a teacher but doesn’t want to offend him in his role as a coach since
there is a strong liaison between Knut and Tanja. Tanja doesn’t want to tell Knut that she doesn't like his attention as a coach

since she likes him privately so much. Knut doesn’t want to tell Tanja that he cannot coach her for the same reason.
Finally they start talking about it. They have to because all three feel stuck. They look at all three roles and agree

that the private role is in the way of creating safe and objective coaching by Knut. Sam and Tanja find another

coach and still keep Knut as their teacher. Tanja and Knut also keep their private relationship.

THE TEAM STRUCTURE

If you agree that competitive dancing has great similarities with running a business, you might as well create a
business team around you. You would need a management team that handles your primary tasks: the Primary
Team. You would need a secondary team for working out logistics, planning and finances. And you would need

some occasional suppliers. The members of this team are valuable but you do not need them regularly. In

dancer’s terms the organization could look as follows:

SUGGESTIONS

Keep The Primary Team as small as possible. This is a team where you need absolute trust and safety. It is the team that sets
goals, plans the season and helps you stay on track during the season. Big teams do not create safety, power and flexibility
since It is hard to get many people agreeing on one direction.

‘Members of The Primary Team should really respect and trust each other to create an effective team. Select teachers Who have

the same ideas about what ballroom dancing should look like and how to create it,

The Primary Team Members would need to be in close contact with each other to stay informed about each other's contr.
bution and to check ifall adds up. Maybe you could organize a regular meeting (for instance once a month) to create clarity
for all involved. :

The relationship of The Primary Team and The Seconday Staff is continuous but less intense than the interaction between the
‘members of The Primary Team. Some decisions made in The Primary Team need to be known by the staff members. They exe:
cute the decisions made in The Primary Team. For instance: your dressmaker needs to know how the team felt about the last
dress and which changes are needed for your next dress.

The Occasional Suppliers are mostly handled by you. These suppliers do not necessarily need contact with members of The
Primary Team. Maybe you would like to discuss with The Primary Team what to do in a lesson from ‘other teachers’ but you
would get the Information across yourself.

Some functions can be executed by one person. For instance: teacher A could also be the coach; the. financial controller could

also be responsible for planning and logistics.

SELECTING TERM MEMBERS: THE PRIMARY TEAM

Members of The Primary Team need to help you out on most of the four levels of human existence?:

When you feel good about all four levels of human existence you are more in balance and happy. This makes it
easier for you to produce the maximum of your dance abilities. Your Primary Team members should be able to
help you out on all four levels (or at least on the first three levels). You could ask yourself the following ques-

tions to check if a person has the qualities to be a member of your Primary Team:
