SELECTING TEAM MEMBERS: THE SECONDARY STAFF
Your Secondary Staff may consist of people who take care of your finances (bookings, bookkeeping), your plan-

ning (entering for competitions, booking hotels and travel), your dressmaker, your hairdresser (and make-up 4
artist), your fitness trainer and maybe even your advertising manager (if you are a professional}. Members of The

Secondary Staff need to help you out on three of the four levels of human existence: 2

Part Two: The Season

SELECTING TEAM MEMBERS: THE OCCASIONAL SUPPLIERS

Your group of occasional suppliers may consist of your other dance teachers (who are not part of The Primary
Team; you may need them once in a while for specific supporting tasks), your dietician, your doctor, people from
your national dance organization, the people who work in the studio you attend Tegularly, your masseur, etc.

These occasional suppliers are selected mostly for specific tasks in the physical or mental plane. Emotionally and

spiritually you do not usually need them.

Some dance teachers are only focused on ‘the glass being half empty’. Such teachers can be very beneficial to develop new
dance skills but are hardly successful in helping you out on an emotional or spiritual level, Try to plan these lessons not in
the preparation weeks for a major competition. Plan them at a time you do not need emotional support. For instance after

your major championships.

Nore
Teachers cannot deliver top quality hours to the maximum. With approximately 1200 hours a year that would be physically

and emotionally impossible. You only work with occasional teachers once in a while. Still you would like to get the best out

of them. What could you do to improve the quality of your lesson?
Although you really come for work on the physical plane, you may still give them some acknowledgement on the emotional

| plane. These teachers need to feel you are dedicated to their work. You may have taken notes on what you did the previous

{- time and refer to that. You may have questions on that material or you show how well you have improved on that matter. You

- may ask them to work with you on their field of special interest. And you may even give them emotional feedback if they need
k it. Some teachers do like to socialize before they start working. Respect that and give them that. Other teachers like to start
1 working directly and need a warmed-up body when the lesson starts, You could handle that. Others may need appraisal for
L what they did for you. You may even give them that.

It might be easier for you to acknowledge the emotional needs as part of the payment for what they professionally offer you.
Separate your personal emotional needs from their emotional needs. For support in your personal emotional needs you could

use your primary team.

Teamwork during the season

YOUR TEAM MEMBERS MAY LAST LONGER

It is effective to check the quality of the team members once in a while. Between seasons is the best moment so
you have time to invest in them and feel safe with them before you need them most: during the season.

When you feel that one or more members does not function well, you may want to talk about that with them
before you start changing the team. You might work out the problems and do not need to change the team every
season. The longer a team exists, the more effective the members can work with you, since they come to know

what you really need. And that takes time, experience and making mistakes as well.

CHOREOGRAPHY

THE STRONG NEED TO WORK CONSTANTLY ON CHOREOGRAPHY

Choreography is an essential part of producing good dancing qualities and results. Maybe that is the main reason
why many dancers and teachers like to put a lot of effort into new choreographies during the whole season. Good
choreography essentially shows the required style, your personal dance style and your abilities and skills.
However its effect seems overestimated. Information in chapter 1-1 shows that your technique and choreography
only count for 7% of your success on the competition day... This makes you wonder whether it is effective to

work on your choreography constantly.

The effect-distribution of your performance’

Teachers often like to work on choreography. Perhaps because they enjoy it or because they are good at it. Maybe
because it is for some the easier part to work on. Or maybe because dancers have a great demand for it. Giving
new choreography makes most of you happy, so why not give it to you?

Many of you have a desire to change your choreography throughout the season. Maybe you like to keep up with
current, fashionable choreography. Maybe you get bored with trying to improve what you have. Maybe you feel
you need new inspiration to keep on working. You may feel that the quantity of the choreography is not enough
("We only have three sides for Quickstep. We need at least six sides’)’. You may even feel that the quality of your
choreography is the cause for lack of results and/or pleasure.

Knowing that at the performing moment the choreography just counts as a minor factor, it may be wise not to

spend so much time on new choreography during the season.

PLANNING NEW CHOREOGRAPHY

Do not misunderstand the fact that choreography only influences the perception of your performance to a minor
extent. This does not imply that you can forget about choreography from now on. It only states that it does not
need an overfocus. Plan your choreography well, so that you have time to prepare yourself for more influential

elements during the performance. Do not work on choreography throughout the season.

Plan it according to your needs and skills

There are no general rules about when you should work on choreography and when you should’t. For some of
you the excitement of a new choreography helps you to perform better in a competition. Others may need the
security of a well-rehearsed familiar programme. The following questions may give your more insight into how

choreography works for you:

The Choreography Questionnaire

Part Two: The Season

SUGGESTIONS
Check the answers to question 1 with questions 2 and 3. Be realistic. Your experiences in the past will help you to plan your
future more realistically.

Your needs for new choreography may not equal your partner's needs. Discuss that together.

HOW LONG DOES IT TAKE YOU TO FEEL SURE?
Again: past experiences help you to predict the future. Also some feedback from your teacher(s) or coach might

be needed. >

EXAMPLE

Mike and Katie are skilled dancers and they are able to adopt new choreography in a day. They really enjoy creating and per-
forming new choreography too. Once in a while - at minor competitions - they get a new routine on the day and perform that
at night. Just for the fun of it. The audience likes that most of the time. Not because of the quality of the performance but
because Mike and Katie enjoy it themselves so much. For major competitions their level of execution of new(!) choreography
is not sufficient. They need about four weeks to have the required level of execution for one new group of figures. Their
choreography plan is as follows: create five new dances during the summer break. Add per dance a new figure or group of
figures one month before a major championship. This strategy provides them with the best of both worlds: a vast experience
in the majority of their material and new inspiration in the month before a major championship without losing the required

quality levels.

WHICH DANCES NEED NEW CHOREOGRAPHY?

Analyse with your partner and teacher(s} what your needs are to meet your goals. New choreography is not
always needed. Even if you think you do need it to keep inspired. Often other changes, like changes in direc-
tion, timing, dynamics and focus are enough to help you to stay inspired. Such changes may give you more time

to develop dance skills (instead of spending time adopting new choreography).

One dance only?

lt can be beneficial not to change all dances but, for instance, for one dance only. ’ You can never change the
Jirst impression you make’. Following this adage it might be useful to make sure that your first dance is excel-
lent (Waltz, Samba or Cha-cha). Another choice might be one of the five dances that inspires you more than
others and enables you to put more effort into it. Or the reverse: you may select the dance you have no con-
nection with and need to find inspiration for. For professionals there is another option: you want to make a state-

ment with one dance.

example
In The Open British Championships the judges mark the professionals through to the next round per dance. Mike and Katie
were very versatile in adopting choreography. Their favourite dance was Paso Doble. They spent time researching flamenco

in Spain and liked to show the skills learned there, They created four(!} totally different full choreographies for Paso Doble.

Planning your choreography

Round after round they performed different material. They were very inspired by that. The judges had no idea why (since
judges only have five seconds per dance to watch them). Their inspiration paid off: they made one more round with Paso Doble

than with the other four dances...

WITH WHOM WOULD YOU WANT TO WORK ON CHOREOGRAPHY?

It might be trendy to get around as many teachers as you can afford. The general idea behind this behaviour is
that it is good to be seen by many teachers (who also happen to be adjudicators...). This strategy is costly and
not always effective. Quality dancing helps more, since quality is always rewarded in the end. There are several
examples of world champions who have only worked with one teacher or a selected team. It may take a little
while longer but that is not even necessarily so.

The number of teachers you work with depends highly on yourself. Some of you might get confused when
trained by more teachers, while it inspires others. Less-experienced couples always need to be careful about
selecting more teachers.

When it comes to choreography, it is very hard to work with more than one choreographer per dance. Two
choreographers might have other, conflicting ideas about what works and what doesn’t. To create a balance in
the total choreography of a dance it is wise to have one choreographer responsible. Maybe some other choreog-
raphers might do some checks or add new pieces. In the end someone needs to be responsible for the overall
structure and quality of the material. That might be you (although it is hard to see the total outcome yourself),

or it might be one choreographer (like in most other forms of dance outside the ballroom world).

Jn short: your maximum numbers of choreographers depends on your needs, your skills, your mental flexibility

(to process conflicting ideas) and the number of dances you perform (five or ten plus maybe a show dance].

SELECT CHOREOGRAPHERS FOR PERSONAL NEEDS

Ballroom dancing has some very fine teachers. Not all of them are great choreographers though. Some of them
are just better in other areas of dance like characterization, technique, posture, performing skills, dynamics, part-
nering skills or rhythm.

It is natural that you like to be trained by top teachers only. But what is the use of it when he or she is not a spe-
cialist in choreography? What is the use of it when this top teacher is not involved with you? Due to lack of time
or lack of interest. Your choreography lesson is hardly effective if you have no follow-up within the required time.
Nor can your lesson be inspiring if your teacher is not involved. Find one or more choreographers based on their

skills, their interest and their availability.

CHECK THE CONTENT OF THE STEPS WITH YOUR TALENT, PHYSICALITY AND SHILLS
Choreography should suit your talent and physical possibilities. it seems unwise to select a teacher who is unable

to work within your possibilities and limitations.

Short heavy-legged people may not be able to perform a very snappy sequence of quick steps. Less-advanced dancers may

the couple but because it suits them. They may have turned on the automatic pilot while teaching. It is just another lesson.

But that is not the case for you, Is it?!

Discuss such feelings with your partner and your choreographer whenever this occurs. In the end it is better to

Your personality tells you what suits you best

for creating special choreography it is helpful to know your Long term strategy*. Another level of questions now
appears: what kind of choreography suits your personality and career best?

Most people belong to the Middel Majority*. They like to conform to the reigning style. About one fifth of soci-
ety can be depicted as Innovators’. The people in this group (sometimes) need to break away from the set pat-
terns in society. They like to experiment in life for personal growth and satisfaction and also approach dancing
in this way. Unfortunately we do not see twenty per cent of the couples working with their own beliefs and
interpretations in ballroom dancing. There are just some examples of people who try to find their own way.
Some of them successfully stick to their own beliefs. They are the ones who bring innovations to the style.
Others aim for success and titles and go with the flow. It isa sad situation for them and for the dancing world.
The effect is twofold.

If everybody sticks to the rules of the Middle Majority the dancing becomes more and more identical. It stops
development and does not keep ballroom dancing alive. This makes our dancing less attractive for spectators
{and for exposure in media like television, etc.). -

Another sad effect is that many of these couples never find the excitement of discovering their personal potential.
They might never realize that during their entire dancing career. They might realize it when it is too late: after they
have stopped competing. The quote from Martha Graham on page 12 in this book explains best why it is so impor-
tant to find your personal contribution. Please read that quote and think about it for a while...

To create choreography that is made especially for you, you need to become more aware of your personality and
goals. Part Three of this book offers you tools to find who you really are and what you really need. That may be
conforming to the reigning style or introducing innovations to our world of dance. In the end ’the best style is your

own style’.° Select teachers and choreographers who like to work together with you on the style you need.

THE SELECTION OF COMPETITIONS
The competitions on offer are much higher than anyone could handle physically, mentally and emotionally. You can’t
dance them all so you have to make choices. These choices could be planned in advance to prevent the feeling of

constantly being in a hurdle race. The choices could be based on balancing the answers to the following:

“The Compatitior

election Questionnaire

YOUR PERSONALITY’ COMES FIRST

People who fee! dominantly isolated in life - like Strugglers and Resigned people - mostly like to compete often,
since dancing means an escape from daily life. If they don’t compete they often feel they are missing out on some-
thing. For them a good competition is a competition where they feel appreciated and accepted. Success may help
them but personal acknowledgement is also important.

Mainstreamers also like to dance often since competitions (and practice nights and lessons) offer social experi-
ences and opportunities to meet friends. For them a good competition is a socially pleasant competition.
Achievers and Succeeders look at competitions quite differently. They dance to see progress, to become a final-
ist or even to win. Participating in a competition needs to be effective for them. Good competitions are compe-
titions which deliver them good or better material results and esteem from others.

The Innovators - Explorers and Reformers - have another outlook on dancing competitions. They mostly like to com-
pete whenever they feel that their dancing is up to it. Good competitions are competitions when they feel they do
what they personally want to do in terms of dancing. Success may not be needed to feel good. Appraisal from others

is only appreciated when it comes from people they admire themselves (most likely other innovative people).

MANAGING THE PERSONAUTES WITHIN THE COUPLE
The two of you do not always have the same dominant personality. Within your own personality structure you
probably have more than one dominant group. These groups may even be conflicting. What can you do with this

information when planning the competitions for a season? Let's look at some examples:

EXAMPLE 1: TWO CONFLICTING PRATS WITHIN YOUR PERSONAUTY

Within one person more personality groups are often dominantly active: two to three Groups are mostly found. So you may
be first a Explorer and second an Aspirer. You mostly want to be individual and different but also need success to feel ful-
filled. When you listen only to your innovator - the Explorer - you might never feel ready to compete while on a competitive
level you are ready for it. You need to find a balance between both needs and maybe compete a little earlier than your Explorer
might want to.

The reverse may also be the case: your Explorer feels quite ready to dance while your Aspirer Part knows you are not up to
the competition yet. It may be necessary to slow down the drive of your innovator part because you would be unhappy if you

were not successful.

EXAMPLE 2: THE TWO OF YOU HAVE THE SAME DOMINANT PERSONALITY

An upcoming professional couple, Hans and Candy, are ranked in the world top 24, The trend for top 24 couples is to enter
all major championships like The international, The United Kingdom Championships, The US Open, The Open German and
The Open British. Each time Hans and Candy participate in these championships they end in the quarter-finals.

Both of them are Aspiters: they need material success to feel happy. They face the following question: 'Is it good for their
£08 to go to these championships and be eliminated after the quarter finals?’ Or would it be better, “for them to dance smalt-
er championships like The Imperial, The Open London, The French Open, The Italian Open or The Dutch Open? In these
champtonships they would have higher results, And that is what the Aspirer needs to be happy and build up further confi-
dence. The smaller championships may also show Hans and Candy their progress more clearly. That would appeal to their
egos. They might even get better and longer write-ups in dance magazines and newspapers. That would bring them recognt-

tion and appraisal...

€XAMPLE 3: THE TWO OF YOU HAVE DIFFERENT DOMINANT PERSONALITIES

Imagine this couple: Nils is an Aspirer and Cindy is a Mainstreamer. Nils only wants to dance to show progress and to be
rewarded with good results. Cindy wants to dance to socialize, They are not yet in the upper league, In some competitions,
promotion points could be gained with a finalist result. Many other competitions are merely for the fun of it as no promotion
points can be gained. What could Nils and Cindy do best?

To attain his ambitions, Nils could decide when to enter a ‘promotion’ competition. He could select those competitions that
offer him the greatest chance of material success. For Cindy's pleasure, they could also enter a selection of the non-promo-

tion competitions. Both have to give in a tittle.

The selection of competitions

AVAILABLE TIME FOR DANCING

Time is a limitation for everybody. It doesn’t matter whether you are a full-time professional or an amateur with
just six hours a week for dancing. Planning the different elements of dancing will make your season more effect-
ive. Your growing edge (learning new skills) is most likely to be higher when you do not have a competition
coming up in the immediate future: your mind is freer to adopt new information.

You can spend your time only once. A day of competition dancing prevents you from having a good practice on
that day. Even more time is lost when you have to travel and stay overnight. It might be more effective to dance
fewer competitions to be able to invest more time (and money) in practices and lessons. Have a look at your sea-

son plan. Do you have enough moments to really grow: moments to take in new information without the stress

of an impending competition?

THE ADJUDICATORS: BEING SEEN AND NOTICED

Many dancers look at adjudicating panels and decide whether the judges are in favour of them or not. The nega-
tive side effect of that - stress - has been explained in chapter 1-2. Most of the times it is better not to focus on
who is judging (especially if you have no choice: when you have to dance the event}. It is only helpful to know

who is on the adjudicating panel if you have a choice to go or not. The following questions help you to make up

your mind:

Do you want to show yourself to one or more of the judges? Maybe you would like to know his or her opinion on your danc-

ing afterwards.

Do you think the adjudicators are professional enough in terms of quality and non-political behaviour?

HAVING A PREVIEW
You will perform better when you know the venue, the organizers of the event and the staff of the venue well.

Knowing the circumstances prevents a lot of stress.2 When a major event takes place in a venue you do not know

you could do one of the following:

Enter another competition in the venue before your major championship is held in the same place. Or: arrive a day for a few

days) earlier and try to practise in the ballroom or just take in the ballroom to feel at home there.

Part Two: The Season

BLOOD, SWEAT AND TEARS

The more you practise, the quicker you improve the quality of your dancing.’ Is this adage true? Does practising
with blood, sweat and tears contribute only positively? Are your dance skills only less than those of others
because you practise less than them? Can you explain the difference between couples by looking at the amounts
‘of hours spent on the dance floor?

Many explain the difference between their results and those of others in terms of money (more lessons} and time
available for practising. Some also include the difference in talent but that would take self-knowledge (and
acceptance} of your own limitations...

This chapter is neither about your talent nor about having more lessons from better teachers. There is another
‘way you can improve: by making your practice more effective. The most common mistakes will be discussed:
focusing on too many subjects at the same time, the lack of a plan, focusing on the improvement of your part-
ner, always practising with your partner, practising too much without other couples, practising at the wrong

times and the lack of competition simulation.

HOW MANY HOURS DO TOP COUPLES PRACTISE?

Internationa! top couples seem to practise more than many other dancers can - or want to - afford. These dancers
are active on the dance floor for about twenty to thirty hours per week. Of course there are periods of more or
jess activity. Periods of less dancing due to, for instance, holidays, work, study and giving workshops. And
periods of intense dancing like the weeks prior to important competitions.

Note that not only the top couples reach this level of practising. Many talented coupies practise equally hard but

do not reach the same progression in their dance qualities. What might they do wrong?

AN ENDLESS LIST OF THINGS TO DO

The fist of subject matters for improvement of your dancing skills is undoubtedly endless. Just to mention some:
posture, choreography, rhythm, balance, timing, footwork, partnering, actions, dynamics, stamina, breathing,
personality building, mental preparations and performing skills. And since you do not only do one dance, but five

{or ten) you can multiply the list of things to attend to by five {or ten)!

THE AUTONOMOUS NERVOUS SYSTEM LIKES HABITS’

Your body is an instrument that likes habits. You notice that in your own behaviour: you probably like to sit in
the same place in your studio when you change your shoes for practice? You probably have a favourite place to
start your dances in the studio?

Why does this happen? A part of your brains, the Autonomous Nervous System {A.N.S.}, is constantly trying to
make you fee! comfortable and safe. Good past experiences will be preferred above new ones. So if you felt good
about certain behaviour in the past, your A.N.S. unconsciously brings you back to such behaviour when neces-
sary. It is just risk prevention. Your A.N.S. reacts each time you want to do something you are not used to. An
unconscious feeling of uncertainty and threat will occur. When the desired activity is not a built in habit, your

ANS. responds by sending stress signals into your body. Your breath becomes shorter, faster and higher in your

Part Two: The Seasen

=

chest. Chemicals are produced and transported via the blood to curb you: you no longer feel free in your body.
Your A.N.S. warns you with these signals: please get out of this situation or change your mind and do something
else you can do well.

Your A.N.S. is not your enemy. It prevents you getting into trouble. It even tries to help you in unsafe situations
by making you do things that it considers you can do well: acquired habits. Each time stress occurs - a situation
that is regarded as uncontrollable by the A.N.S. - you will respond by doing something you did well in the past.
In dancing terms this means that whenever you are under stress you feel tess free to dance and you pick up old
skills.

PRACTISING IN OTHER DANCE STYLES

Take a quick glance at the practice schedules of other dance styles. Most of these show schedules with great dis-
ciplines to work out subject matters monotonously. Take classical ballet for instance: in this style dancers do not
practise just ten minutes to learn the right positions. Many hours of hard work over a long period are needed to
execute the five positions accurately. Nobody seems to even avoid that. And once the right positions are realized
the real work starts. These ballet dancers understand that having produced the right positions just a few times
doesn’t mean a new habit has been created. The years of ’mal’-practice still dominate the Tecognized habit for the
A.N.S. Their body and mind now have to accept the newly learned positions as a habit. And since the dancer pro-
duced the positions incorrectly for many years, many hours are needed to compensate that.

Baliroom dancers often make the mistake that once they sense that they have mastered a certain movement a
few times, they feel they must go on to a new subject. That is really a pity because the newly mastered skill is
not yet accepted as a habit by the A.N.S.

BITS AND PIECES

Let’s look at an average practice of two hours. Most of you will dance three to four dances in this practice. You
work, for example, on your choreography and other subject matters which spontaneously come up like timing,
footwork, the hold, directions and maybe some dynamics or actions. Each subject matter probably gets about ten
minutes of attention. This is far too short for your body to get into the desired movement and to recognize it as a

desired habit. It is far too short to have the movement sink in so well that you can do it without thinking.

EXAMPLE

This odd example may make things more clear: you can tie your shoelaces unconsciously. Can’t you? You don’t even have to
look at what you are doing to get the job done, This action has sunk in very well, Well, this hasn't always been the case. It
took you months as an infant to learn the trick of tying your shoe laces. You would have learned it more quickly if your mother

had made you tie your laces a few times a day,
EXERCISE
Untie your shoes right now. Take them off: Cross your hands and keep them crossed. Now put on your shoes again and tie

your shoelaces. With your hands crossed. You will manage to do so but now you consciously experience mental activity and

Practical practice points

stress in your body. What would you do if the phone rang while you were doing this exercise? Would you uncross your hands

and quickly get your shoes on in order to pick up the phone? You probably would. Your old habit has taken over.

HOW MUCH TIME DOES EACH SUBJECT MATTER NEED?

In order to adopt new learning as a recognized habit you need to rehearse it over and over again. Unfortunately,
no fixed schedules can be given to state the time needed per subject matter. This depends on the skilfulness of
your body and mind to learn new movements.

An hour's practice on one subject matter is more effective than six times ten minutes. Repetition is also import-
ant. However, the effect of the repetition diminishes when the time used is too short. Therefore you better select
one or two subject matters for a few weeks or even a few months. You can then have the required time per sub-

ject matter per practice as well as the profitable repetition.

WHICH SUBJECT MATTERS WOULD YOU SELECT?

There is so much to do. And everything seems equally important. However, you still need to make choices to
give new learning a chance to sink in as a habit.

The two of you could make a list of subject matters you would like to handle during the next season. First put
it all on paper and then put them in order of priority. Compare the two lists and create a mutual list. Discuss this
list with your principal teacher or coach. Select the subject matters you will focus on during the next season and
prioritize them. Less experienced dancers could choose, for instance, four subject matters for the season (they
can divide the year into four blocks of three months). Top competitors - who have more skills - could probably
work with a plan per one or two months and select six to twelve subject matters.

Select per term the most relevant subject matter. Coordinate the selected subject matter with the major competi-
tion at the end of the term. For some competitions it is better to work on rhythm (for instance when the floor is
quite small}, others might need a subject matter like floorcraft (when the floor is very large and/or packed with
couples per round). Work out the selected subject matter in ail five dances. Stick to the plan during your prac-

tises and your lessons, In this way each reinforces the other.

The advantages of one subject matter per term

At the end of the term you will find out that your body is able to control the subject matter much better. Also
the A.N.S. has started to recognize it now as a habit. Finally, it will bring you peace of mind: you don’t have to
worry any longer about what to do at each practice. The choice has already been made. This makes you feel
good... And each time your inner critic tells you that other subject matters need to be attended to, you can relax:

that subject matter is planned for later. [t is not the issue now,

WILL BOREDOM HIT YOU?
Nowadays people are used to variety. We no longer work for forty years for one boss but change jobs much more
often. We even change careers. We no longer eat our national food but enjoy international cuisine. This longing

for variety applies to almost everything in life. In our era we prefer not to do the same thing over and over again.

Part Two. The Season

Therefore it is understandable that you fear that you would get bored, sticking to one subject matter for weeks
in a row. It doesn’t seem appealing to have to work on one subject at each and every practice. This is under-
standable and - fortunately - there are solutions for this need for variety. You could start to practise your subject

matter in different ways.

EXAMPLES OF EXERCISES AROUND A SINGLE SUBJECT MATTER
The following examples show the versatility of exercises you may find for one subject matter. Some of the exer-

cises will appear in all three subject matters (like breathing techniques and analysing good examples).

EXAMPLE ONE: POSTURE

Suppose you are a man and you have decided with your coach that you will work the coming three months on
a better posture in your Standard dances. Your top is too rigid. Your shoulders often creep up and leading your
lady is quite difficult. Gaps regularly appear between you and your partner while you are dancing. Your
teacher(s) could help you by giving you a great variety of exercises. But you could do more than just the practi-

cai, technical instructions from your teacher(s). Think, for example about the following eleven exercises,

Concentration on relaxation
Practice the relaxation of shoulders and arm muscles. Your exhalation will help these muscles to relax while you

are dancing. You could practise this solo and/or together with your partner. Also read chapter 2-8-2.

Increasing leading skills with the lower torso
Dance together without the arms in dance hold. The only contact you would have would be lower torso-pelvis
to lower torso-pelvis. You could start to practise this in basics and gradually try it out in parts of your routine,
You could also consider activating your pelvis before You start practising by doing the following activities:
The Cleaning Breath (see chapter 1-4, step 3).
Move your pelvis by breathing: fallow your breath with the movement.
Think about your pelvis: where is it located? Look at your pelvis. Touch your pelvis, Feel the muscles you use to move
your pelvis.

Activate sexual arousal.

Improvement of breathing techniques

A short breathing high in your chest makes your pelvis turn backwards. By practising deeper breathing you
strengthen your diaphragm (the largest muscle in your body). You have more energy to dance. You cramp less.
Moreover, due to your deep exhalations you contract the diaphragm and your pelvis now tilts forward.
Through better breathing alone you could improve your dancing considerably. Specialists indicate that you can learn

to breathe more naturally in about six weeks. Read chapter 2-8-2 for Suggestions to improve your breathing skills.

Practical practice points

Practising breathing accents during dance
For this subject you could find many exercises you could do on your own or together with your partner. Read

chapter 2-8-2 for suggestions to create accents with your breath.

De-stressing your body and mind during warm-up

You can use the warming up for technical reasons: stimulation of the blood circulation (for extra oxygen supplies
for the muscles), warming up of the muscles and stretching the tendons and the muscles. You can also use the
warming up to get rid of the stress of the day: both mentally and physically. Dancing the 'Five Rhythms’ of

Gabrielle Roth could be a good solution. Read chapter 2-8-1 for a holistic warming up programme.

Analysing good examples
Use the competitions you attend (or see on television) not only for your entertainment. Have a good look at the
couples that are better than you and notice what they do with your subject matter {in this case the dance hold).

Also read chapter 2-8-4 for suggestions on how to build a vast mental library.

Visualizing the right dance hold
Through meditation you can improve your mind to help you attain your goal. Making an analysis of good ex-

amples beforehand helps you to get the right picture.

Marking your choreography
An effective exercise during practice can be ‘Marking your choreography’. See chapter 2-8-6.

Dancing In Semi-hold
Take on the dance hold partly: do not connect the left hand of the man with the right hand of the woman. Keep

the elbows of these free sides up.

Strengthening your abdominal muscles

You can do crunch exercises for your upper, lower and oblique abdominals. Fitness instructors could give you
professional! advice for a routine. You can also buy abdominal practising machinery for about sixty dollar (AB-
shaper, AB-roller, AB-trainer etc.). it just takes you five minutes a day for four to six weeks to see noticeable

improvements in your control over the abdominals.

Focus on past- present-future

Your focus of your eyes can influence your posture. Dreamy eyes may unconsciously create a weaker posture
(forwards). Eyes that do not want to really see (like a glaze) may tend to make you go backward. Eyes that clear-
ly look where you are going to (the future) help to create a more forward posture. Eyes that are very present in

the now may have again another effect on your posture.

Part Two: The Season

i

EXAMPLE TWO: PROGRESSION
You are a Latin American dancer and have the desire to work on getting more control over speed. You want to
have more variety, flexibility and freedom of choice around this Subject matter. You have planned to work on

this subject matter for the next 10 weeks. Consider the following eight exercises.

Interpretation of the choreography
Decide what are the highlights and what the precedes and the follows in the choreography. Decide for each part
which parts are to be danced stationary and which with some or more travelling. You need to decide on this

before doing most of the other exercises.

Feel the use of the centre
The right technical use of the centre gives you the power to accelerate, to stop or to slow down. You could

analyse and practisé moments in your choreography where awareness of the use of the centre helps.

The use of foot pressure
The right technical use of foot pressure gives you the foundation to accelerate, to stop or to slow down. Analyse

and practise moments in your choreography where awareness of foot pressure helps.

Sing the rhythm of the choreography

Sing the rhythm of your choreography while you dance. Do this first solo and aloud (partly loud, partly hissing,
partly humming). Then dance together without sound.

This exercise helps you to create an inner rhythm (that makes you less dependent on the music). It also helps
to investigate where the rhythms of the two of you do not match (cross rhythms). Clarity on rhythm issues and
singing inner rhythms greatly helps to improve your skills on speed and progression.

Working with breathing accents

Sreathing exercises are helpful for many of the skills you need. Speed can be greatly induced, helped or stopped
by controlling your breath. Read the previous example for more information on breathing, Also read chapter
2-8-2.

Focus and sense of time

Although there is oniy the ’now’ for the dancer while performing, the receiver - the audience and judges - can
interpret the dancer being in the past, present or future. Eyes have the power to express such virtual time lines.
Accessible and/or open eyes - and eyes which clearly see things - give the onlooker the sense of the present (the
now}. Eyes that are strongly directed forward (to where you intend to 0) project a sense of the future (= pro-
jecting a feeling that you want to get to somewhere else soon). Eyes looking without seeing, eyes that are avoid-
ing connection, eyes directed downwards project a sense of wanting to Stay in the past.

Eyes that always express the past - or present - give you the image of using less speed compared to dancers

Practical practice points

always expressing the future. If you are versatile in the expression of your focus, you radiate a greater control

over speed (while the speed of your body has not changed).

Dancing solo
It is beneficial for control over speed if both of you can dance your routine solo. Of course there are parts where
a strong lead or counterbalance is necessary to dance to the fullest. Dance such parts as much as your own body

allows you to. The rest of the choreography you can handle perfectly solo.

Who is tatking?
Where do you want the audience/judges to look mostly? You can choose to put a visual accent on both of you,

on one of you or having no accent at all. This depends on the choreography and the interpretation of that.

Two things might happen when you do not make a choice about who is important in each step: both of you try
to attract the attention or no one tries to attract the attention during the whole routine. When the accent is
always on both of you the image indicates restlessness. When the accent is never on anyone, then your dancing
becomes somewhat boring.

Analyse the choreography and make choices about who ’talks’ where. Prevent ’Over-talking’ or too much

'Silence’. A good balance in ’Who is talking’ projects a better sense of speed control too.

EXAMPLE THREE: FOCUSING

This is an example of a Standard wheelchair couple (Tanja, a handicapped woman dancer and Fred a regular
dancer) have two months before their next national championship. Tanja and Fred had never planned their prac-
tice before, Their long list of things to do consisted of technique, highlights in the choreography, rhythm, part-
nering skills, posture, use of space and focusing. This was the emergency list of things they felt they needed to
attend to to win the championship. After some discussion Tanja and Fred understood that working on all these
aspects would not be effective.

The level of general standardized technique of their dancing was comparable to the other contenders for the title.
Therefore they decided to leave that subject matter out of the short-term plan. Their time span to adopt new
choreography was at least three months so working on new choreography was not a wise choice at this moment.
The issues rhythm, partnering skills, posture and use of space were on the level of their competitors. Noticeable
improvement on one of these issues would demand at least three month’s work. These subject matters were not
selected.

Fred and Tanja could adopt ‘focus’ and breathing techniques’ in time. They had a natural talent for that. They
could also practise it during moments when they were on their own. Improvements were seen immediately at
the first practice. The other subject matters were planned in the year after the championship so they would feel

that these were not neglected. Their exercises were as follows:

Part Two; The Season

a. ae

Energetic linkage: determine the use of eye focus

While dancing - and also in private and professional life - people have a preference for one of the following qual-
ities of energetic linkage. Some like a primary connection with others on a personal basis (for instance a warm,
challenging or attacking linkage). Others like to connect as a neutral observer (not indicating whether they like
or dislike the connection}. Some primarily dislike personal eye contact and withdraw their focus (like in a glaze)
or even prefer to have no visual connection with others (ignore).

In chapter 2-8-8 you will find exercises to increase your awareness of these qualities and to find your personal
primary style of eye contact. This quality you probably use dominantly in your dancing as well.

Dancing is however easier with a neutral or a personal focus. You can practise the exercises to develop choice
in your focus connection. This has little to do with your personal feelings towards one another. Energy follows
thought. Your mind can decide which linkage you need in any situation.

Determine the choice energetic linkage per dance
Fred has a preference for a warm energetic linkage in Waltz, Foxtrot, Viennese Waltz and Quickstep. For Tango

he has selected a personal attacking energetic linkage. These will be his overall focuses per dance,

Determine highlights in your choreography
Start looking at the choreography as sentences of preparation-highlight-exit. Determine four to six highlights per
minute of choreography. The highlights of Tanja and Fred do not need to be the same...

Determine your choice of energetic linkage around the highlights
In the two months up to the championship Fred practises a change of focus in some highlights (instead of ’warm’

for Waltz he chooses ‘personal attack’, ‘challenge’, ‘neutral’, ’cold’ or ‘ignore’ for say the Hover Corté).

What does the highlight depict narratively
Find out what the movements suggest in terms of the human relationship between you and your partner.

Develop a sense of time in the focus

What does your focus suggest in terms of time? Do you want to indicate where you are going to (the future), do
you want a focys which shows you enjoy the moment (enjoy yourself, and/or your partner), or do you want to
portray a feeling you want to stay where you were (past).

Check the moments where focus in time is not working for you. Fred, for example, had a focus on the past in
his Three Step and Feather Step in Foxtrot. Due to this focus Fred sent out the message that he was hardly pro-
gressing over the floor (a weakness he needed to solve). Changing his focus to the future in the Three Step and
Feather Step not only improved the sense of progression but also immediately improved his posture.

Tanja had a desire to have her focus in the past when getting into a promenade position (in all dances she han-
dled her time focus that way). For Foxtrot and Waltz the result was very interesting. For Quickstep and Tango

this choice really didn’t work. It made her iook heavy and not in tune with the character of these dances. Tanja

Practical practice points

learned to change these moments with a more effective focus. For Tango she chose to have an aggressive focus

on the future. For Quickstep her choice was to have her focus more in the ‘now’.

INTERRELATED EXERCISES

As you will have noticed many exercises can be used for different subject matters since everything is interrelated.
However there is a difference in working on one subject matter or going along with your heeds of the moment.
Working on a clear 'Focus’ will help you to create a better Posture’. That would be the reason to practise Focus’.

This ’Focus’ could also become a subject matter supported by other exercises,

POSTURE

HOW MANY DANCES CAN YOU HANDLE WELL IN ONE PRACTICE?
There are many exercises you can work with for one subject matter. Select three exercises for one week. Evaluate
after this week if you have mastered these exercises for all routines of all dances. If not, you may want to keep

one or more of these exercises for the following week.

Part Two: The Season

How do you handle the three selected exercises? Practise each dance for one hour. Divide the hour into three
blocks of twenty minutes. Use the same part of your routine for all three exercises, You now 7 work on your Waltz
routine for an hour with one subject matter to take care of. And you will not even get bored.”

The next hour you will work on your second dance using the same three exercises, each for twenty minutes.
The whole week you will follow this system. This works better than using all exercises, each for just a few min-
utes. The consequence of such a schedule is that you do need one hour of practice for just one dance. You are
able to handle only two dances in a practice of two to three hours. The following practice you can select two

other dances and focus on the same subject matter and use the same exercises,

NOTE
When you are not able to control the music during your practice you can do the following: work in blocks of twenty minutes
for one exercise, then take the next one and so on. You still have the advantage of being well focused without getting bored.

The disadvantage that you cannot work on one dance for an hour has to be accepted.

The whole system may look overstructured, Well, remember your time at school. School schedules are also well
structured. Teachers know that you will learn faster when they repeat new lessons. And good teachers do not
bore you by giving you one exercise for a full hour. Good teachers stay with the same subject matter but change

every twenty minutes from teaching method to keep you interested and focused...

AN EXAMPLE OF A PRACTICE PLAN FOR A MONTH
The previous page shows a practice schedule for the four-week period before Peter and Brenda’s championship,
Their subject matter was posture improvement. They created - together with their coach and dance teachers -

eleven exercises to master their posture in a better way.

POINTS TO TAKE INTO CONSIDERATION

Peter and Brenda practise about 17 hours. They also take 2 hours of private lessons and I hour of mental coaching. You may
not be able to spend 20 hours a week. In that case you can do one dance less per practice and so save 5 hours a week. You
can also delete one or more practices and save hours. Or you can take two exercises of 20 minutes per dance (instead of 3)
and save 40 minutes per practice.

The first two weeks Peter and, Brenda simulate the final only once per two practices. Later on they do the simulation every
practice mounting up to two simulations per practice in the week before the championship.

The simulations in the last four practices are at the competition times so the body gets used to the demand to dance to the
maximum at those times, The body will adjust its biological clock accordingly.

The day before the championship Peter and Brenda enjoy leisure time to prepare themselves mentally and emotionally.
During the whole period each dance has been practised for two consecutive days in order to create a stronger learning curve
in that dance,

During the last 3 practices they do not repeat each dance for two consecutive days. Now they attend to all five dances for f
Aour in three days.

The exercises in the last week are not new. They are repetitions of earlier effective exercises to make Peter and Brenda aware
of their improvements.

In appendix 2-10-2 you will find a clean planning form for four weeks, which you may copy and fill in for yourself.

THE PRACTICE DIARY

You may want to keep a ’Practice Diary’. This makes you more aware of what you are really doing. You keep
track of your improvements more consciously. The Diary-method also gives your coach the opportunity to. check
the effectiveness of the time you spend on practising. With the diaries at hand your coach can give you well

founded advice on how to improve your practice time. On the following page you will find an example. For your

own use you can copy the clean form in appendix 2-10-3. The two of you fill in the forms separately after each

practice. Analyse these forms together with your coach regularly.

i woTe

You can still use the Practice Diary method if you don't have a coach or teacher to do the analysis with. The two of you fill in the
forms separately. Discuss them periodically (not directly after each practice). Discussing them directly after each practice would
become quite boring. Furthermore it will evoke an ‘It isn’t true!’-'It is true!’-discussion on the scores of ‘Focus’ and 'Vatue’.

| It is not important to know who is right and who is wrong. Both interpretations are right! It is more important to see a pat-

tern emerging from a series of forms. And to see the patterns you will need to have more input than the evaluation of one day

of practice.

DO YOU ALWAYS NEED TO PRACTISE WITH YOUR PARTNER?

In the world of ballroom dancing most of you are used to doing everything with your partner. You have lessons
together, you practise and travel together and - of course - you dance competitions together. In other dancing dis-
ciplines this is not necessarily the rule.

The best ballet couple of the previous century was probably Rudolf Nureyev and Dame Margot Fonteyn. These
dancers didn’t aiways work together. In fact the opposite was more often true, Sometimes they wouldn’t see each
other for months. They practised individually to bring body and mind to perfection for the forthcoming ballet per-
formances. They would only need a few weeks to make it work together. The same also applies to opera. The
divas study their scores at home and are able to Put the opera together in a few weeks with the conductor, the
orchestra, the other singers, the stage managers, the lighting technician, the dress maker and make-up artists. It
could help you to develop your skills individually too. Much of your work can be done more effectively on your
own. The two of you often have other individual problems/needs.

EXAMPLE
Peta - a Latin dancer - needs Flamenco classes (as 4 Supporting dance} to increase the toning skills of her body while Dimitri

focuses on Salsa to increase his flexibility and freedom.

{n many cases supporting dances and techniques from. outside the regular ballroom world help you to learn the
required skills more quickly. The reason for that is that your mind is freer to accept the new information (not
lumbered with all the usual demands). Once your body is used to this new technique, it is easier to incorporate

that skill into your dancing.

EXAMPLE
Fabio wants to improve his posture. He needs to breathe his movements more (as in contrast o using his muscles more). For
Fabio itis easier to first learn better general breathing and strengthen his diaphragm before incorporating breathing skils into

nis dancing.

A final reason why an individual approach can be effective at times is to stimulate your partnership. It is hard to
. Surprise your partner with new skills if you do everything together. This applies even more when the two of you

not only dance together but also share your private lives,

PRACTISING PRIVATELY DOES NOT ALWAYS HELP
In many countries the dance clubs do not have tens of top competitive amateur couples all practising hard to-
gether. Most clubs just have a few good amateur couples. In the professional field top couples can also face an
isolated existence: they need to practise between giving lessons and travelling. In these situations the dancers
often practise on their own. This has some advantages.
It is easier to build up your own practice scheme since you are free to select the music you need each time. You

are not hampered by other couples on the floor so it is easier to concentrate and/or experiment. You feel freer

Practicol practice points

since you can control most of the circumstances. Next to these advantages there are also some drawbacks you
have to take into account:
You could get used to having it all your own way. Unfortunately this is never the case at competitions. Consequently your body
and mind are not used to the uncontrollable elements of competitions: stress is now more likely to occur;
You may get the feeling that you are the only ones who practise so hard and this may make you feel down-hearted at times;
You may never learn enough floorcraft. When there are no other couples on the floor it is practically very difficult to learn to
avoid them. You may miss the ‘habit’ of avoiding others and - at the same ttme - staying in contact with your own dancing.
The Autonomous Nervous System will recognize the other couples on the floor as a threat for you. Consequently your A.N.S.

starts sending stress signals during competitions.

The best thing you can do is to have a combination of both practice situations: have solo practices and attend
public practice nights.
If there are no practice nights in your vicinity, then you could organise them yourself for at least once a week. Contact your
close competitors to have a joint practice. You will all profit from it.
If you never have a studio for yourself you then start investigating the possibilities. Talk about it with your teachers. Check
local dance studios and sport clubs. If you really want tt, you will find a place. Maybe just once a week but that would already

be an improvement.

DIFFERENCES IN PERSONALITIES

No two dancers are alike. Not physically and certainly not mentally, emotionally and spiritually. As humans we
have a tendency to think people experience life like we do. But the reality is that they don’t. Our personality is
based on our genes, our upbringing, our culture and our experiences in life. Therefore we all react differently to
external stimuli.

During practices these different personalities often come out clearly. There are numerous reasons for that. You
mostly dance in close contact with each other, which means that your partner is in your private space most of
the time. Anywhere else in life you would probably not have people in your private space so continuously. This
makes you quite vulnerable. Secondly the presence of other couples on the floor means that you have less con-
trol over the space. They might be in the way or even invade your private space when they bump into you.
Thirdly, you are trying to improve yourself. You need to be in touch with your faults, which makes you quite vul-

nerable too.

The differences between yourself and your partner can feel quite awkward at times. When a problem occurs you
may feel that you need to work harder while you partner feels victimized and wants to go home... The situation
is the same but the experience is different. Who is right? Both of you are right since there is not one objective
reality. You can keep on arguing about the issue or have more respect for each other’s different needs, and opin-

ions. This respect prevents many arguments and when they do occur it helps you to solve problems between you

more easily.

Part Two: The Season

One way of looking at personality differences is the Four C’s research as explained in chapters 3-3 and 3-4. This

research mentions seven personality groups, each with different attitudes and needs:

Practical practice points

Try to recognize yourself in one or two groups. And where do you expect your partner fits in? Discuss your views
to get more clarity. Once you have a sense of who you and your partner are, you will find it easier to give your-

self and your partner what. you both need.

COMMENTS ON YOUR PARTNER

Making comments on your partner during practices, lessons and competitions is a widely accepted phenomenon.
Some of you even comment on your partner during dancing and in between dancing the five dances in the final.
Generally this focus on your partner does not lead to improvements in the dancing. At least three reasons explain
why:

The self-improvement reason

Hf you would practise with a better partner for two months, would you yourself then become a better dancer?
Visualize yourself dancing with your idol for two months. Now visualize yourself practising with your normal
partner for two more months. In which situation would you have improved most? Of course you would be bet-
ter after having danced with your idol.

In other words: if your partner is better, you become better as well. Consequently this means that if you become

a better dancer your partner also becomes better. As a couple you will improve when you improve.

The stress reason

The Autonomous Nervous System (A.N.S.} interferes whenever you concentrate on the tasks of your partner. The
grey matter in your brain knows perfectly well that you cannot control other people. In other words you now
want something your A.N.S. cannot provide you with. The Autonomous Nervous System tries to warn you by
sending stress signals. This stress makes you less free in your body and the quality of your movements is reduced.
At the same time your partner feels your desire and stress. She/he will react to that. In many cases the reaction

will be stress too and the two of you get into a negative spiral.

The autonomous compensation reason
Something else happens when you concentrate on the mistakes your partner makes. The A.N.S. tries to help you.
Your body reacts to your expectations. The A.N.S. - automatically - compensates the expected error of your

partner.

EXAMPLE
When you expect your partner to rotate too little, you will - unconsciously - give your partner more lead. Let us also suppose
that your partner now produces the right amount of rotation. What will happen? The movement still doesn’t work because

now you have led her too much. And you are not even aware of it!

Part Two: The Season

CHECK THE €FFECT OF COMMENTING ON YOUR PARTNER

It is easy to check the negative effects caused by the Autonomous Nervous System. Do the following exercise

the next time you want to correct your partner instead of yourself:

DISCUSS THE PROBLEM YOU ARE FACING
The two of you will probably have a difference of opinion.
FIRST FIVE MINUTES’ PRACTICE: ACCORDING TO THE MAN'S NEEDS

Practice for five minutes exactly the way the man has suggested. The partner tries to do what the man requires her to do.

Practical practice points

Without any discussion. The. man gives one suggestion at a time and phrases tt positively (instead of saying "You are too early
on the two’ he says: "Try to Keep the four-and a little longer’. Practise each suggestion at least three times before the man gives

a new suggestion.
SECOND FIVE MINUTES' PRACTICE: ACCORDING TO THE WOMAN'S NEEDS

After these first five minutes you will reverse the roles; the man now has to do exactly what the woman wants him to do.

H Without any discussion.

THIAD FIVE MINUTES’ PRACTICE: A FOCUS ON YOUR OWN TASKS

Concentrate on your own:tasks and forget about the tasks of your partner. Practise this for five minutes.

If the first practice exercise proves to be better, then the suggestions from the man were appropriate. If the sec-
ond practice-exercise proves to ‘be better then hers were better. No one loses: you both win. You have both

noticed from experience what works best. That is much more effective than having a mental ’war’ about it. You

have given yourselves the chance to find this out by focusing on clear tasks.

If none of the three practice exercises proves to be functional, the two of you lack the information to make it

| work, Stop trying to solve this difficulty and take it up with your teacher to obtain the information you evident-

ly need. The same applies when two of you cannot agree on which exercise felt better. Practising without clear

information is destructive.

In most cases however, the third practice exercise proves to be best. This indicates the need to change your men-
tal focus. Stop bothering about the mistakes of your partner and start making a habit of thinking: "How can [
improve myself?’ Adopt this thinking as a new practice rule for both of you. Check your personal focus whenever

you feel you are getting stressed. Refer to the sentence "How can I improve myself?’ whenever your partner starts to

comment on you.

NOTES

There are times when comments on your partner can be beneficial. In that case you better first ask your partner if suggestions

eee

it are OK before dumping your thoughts and desires. Still many of you have the habit of readtly commenting on your partner. To
| get out of this habit it is easier to pick up the new habit by not allowing any comments for a period of time. Maybe at a later
te stage you could start commenting again when you are sure that you focus primarily on your own tasks.

! "How can | improve myself?’ is not just a rule to prevent you giving unasked verbal comments to your partner. It encompass:

es an attitude. Shutting yourself up but non-verbally indicating that your partner is wrong doesn’t help. You really need to

embrace the concept totally.

WARNING

i Focusing on yourself does not mean that you become a selfish dancer. Focusing on your own tasks means that you have to
i partner each other well. You still need to be aware of your partner, you still need to lead and follow each other. The focus has

just changed from what your partner should do to what you can do yourself.

Part Two. The Season

PRACTICE TIMES

One of the unpleasant features of competition dancing is that events are often held at peculiar times. A competi-
tion might start at noon and have the final run at midnight. [f you always practise in the morning, your body
does not have the required habit of exerting itself late at night. In preparation weeks for major competitions you
need to practise at the same times as the crucial rounds occur in competitions.

Fortunately you do not have to do this the whole year round. First of all, you do not have important competi-
tions every week. Secondly, your body is quite capable of adjusting its biorhythm in a short time span {note the
few days it takes to recover from jet lag or to restore a daily rhythm after a week of night shifts).
