Part Two: The Season

TURN YOUR APPEARANCE INTO A REAL ASSET

Competitive ballroom dancing has its own rules for fashion. Most dancers like to follow the general fashion trends.
This may make you wonder?! Is this collective fashion trend beneficial to the sport and the performers? Would the
results of the competitions change dramatically if everybody wore the same outtit? If appearances had no bearing
on results why would you then spend so much money on your dresses, suits, hair and make-up?

If you follow the trend you may miss an opportunity to stand out the crowd. Wardrobe, hairstyle and make-up
are important elements to impress the judges and audiences. They may also give you the inspiration to perform

well. They may hide some weakness and accentuate some strengths as well. And finally they could confirm your

personal dance style. i
Appearances do make clear statements. You do need to know how to turn your appearance into a real asset. The
information in this chapter is based on marketing communication techniques and:a vast experience of working
with both Standard and Latin American dancers around the world. The techniques demand some time and effort
but the investment will prove worthwhile. Your appearance will improve and your worries and stress around it

will decrease notably. You’ll learn more when you stop and think before you copy others.

i WARDROBES ARE VERY FUNCTIONAL
i
\ Dance fashion has three basic functions in communication towards judges, audiences, other competitors, officials

and even towards yourself and your partner:

I Reflecting the discipline

Most of you are very much in tune with the first function of dressing: the style (Standard or Latin American danc-

ing) is mostly recognized. Few people try to break the rules. And sometimes these revolutionary ideas become

tules again.

EXAMPLE
For a very long time the Standard dancer's dresses were made of net and were very bulky. Dancers did not like these so-called
a ‘Teapots’ in the early eighties. Perhaps because these dresses were hard to travel with since they took up so much space.

i Perhaps because leading professionals needed something new to identify with. In one championship almost all professional

female Standard finalists chose to wear Ginger Rogers’ style dresses. Nowadays nobody wears 'Teapot-dresses’ any more. The

revolutionary idea became the new rule.

Some leading professionals have introduced new ideas: female Standard dancers competing in trousers, male Standard dancers

dancing in a lounge suit (instead of the traditional Tails’). Maybe these are still revolutionary ideas for you.

Maybe one day, when these ideas turn into new rules, you will accept them. The more innovative your mind, the earlier you

Hi Part Two: The Season

adopt these new ideas. The more traditional you tend to be, the more time it takes for ‘you to accept the ‘follies’.

Whoever you are, whatever you like, your outfit needs to portray the essence of the style in which you are competing.

Bringing you personal inspiration

Many of you get a kick out of new outfits. It often makes people feel good because it is a guarantee for atten-
tion. New dresses give you a feeling of being special. They mark the extraordinary occasion too. New dresses
- like expensive make-up - may even give you a feeling of self-reward or indulgence. Your outfits give you a
chance to connect with who you are in daily life. Your private taste of fashion has some influence on what you

wear on the competition floor too, This can bring quite some inspiration,

UNPLANNED DRESSES:

Getting a personal kick out of a new dress can be positive but you better not exaggerate. Top couples generally
have @ new outfit for each major championship. And that is fine as long as each outfit has been carefully se-
lected. In a lot of cases dancers run from dress to dress during the season. Some work out well, some don’t.
Some are ready in time; others are too late. Would it not be more effective to plan your outfits well in advance?
To work it all out before the season starts? To find the right style for the next season and make good variations
within the selected theme? To have them planned and order them for the next six months and replan during

the Christmas break? It would take away a lot of trouble during the season.

TOO NEU DRESSES

For most dancers inspiration means new dresses. Brand new! It is hard to convince many female dancers to try
out the dress during a dance before they compete in it. They prefer to check whether the dress fits and leave it
like that. The symbolic value of the newness almost equals that of a wedding dress. This May create positive
arousal but can also create stress'. Posing in a dress brings other physical experiences: than dancing in a dress.
While you pose you may feel good in your dress. While you dance you may feel restrictions you could not feel
while posing. Your body is not used to that feeling and so stress may occur. Therefore you need to dance in a new
dress before you compete in it. And there is another reason for that: it is the only way to check if the dress real-
ly looks good. It is the only way to check the movement of the skirt, the effect of the decorations, to see if the
dress hides your weaknesses and accentuate your strengths. Of course these try-outs take away a little of your

inspiration for the "brand new’, On the other hand it also frees you from unnecessary stress.

EXAMPLE
Kathryn had found a style which suited her well. She had many dresses made in the same design while changing the materi-
als, colours and decorations dress by dress. Kathryn felt it was time to move on and find a new design for the next six months,
Her dressmaker came up with a beautiful idea: a two-piece dress with a top covered in feathers. Kathryn, her partner Tom
and the primary team around them were all delighted. The new dress looked so good on her that they decided to adopt the
new design. Kathryn really didn't want to try out the dress. It would be her secret weapon for her next major championship.

The dress was neither to be seen by others nor to be practised in. Not even once.

Effective appearances

A primary team member convinced her to dance just one dance in it. After a mere ten.seconds the floor was covered with
black feathers. The studio suddenly looked tike a chicken ranch. Somehow the dressmaker had not fixed the feathers on well
and they came loose. Fortunately it happened in the studio and there was enough time to have the feathers fixed on in the

right way, Kathryn's inspiration from wearing a brand new dress could have turned into a nightmare.

Bringing you recognition

Appearances can make it easier for the audience and adjudicators to note and remember you during a champion-
ship and even during your career. Changing dresses competition after competition (or even round after round),
each in different designs and colours, doesn’t add up easily. Effective recognition needs differentiation (being dif-

ferent from others) and repetition.

BUILD RECOGNITION THROUGH OUTFITS BY DIFFERENTIATION

Less-known couples search for recognition to get noticed. Well-known top couples use recognition for building
up their image. This recognition is helped by a carefully selected mainstream style, which suits you, but it
demands differentiation too. Why do you spend so much money on outfits that all look the same? You could all
wear training outfits and the results of the competitions would probably be the same. It is possible to differenti-
ate further.

Fortunately things are changing as the spirit of the times is changing. In the seventies, society used to have a col-
lective frame of mind in which people liked to do what other people did. At the beginning of the twenty-first
century the collective frame of mind is changing. More and more people are looking for a more personal
approach. They want to grow in a way that is not necessarily the same as the majority. This changing mood can

also be found in the dance world. We see more and more different styles at the same time.

BUILD RECOGNITION BY REPETITION

Not only differentiation helps your recognition, You need to repeat your visual statement to create your image.
Just like advertising repeats commercials to get the message across.

In recent times dancers have changed their outfits during major championships. Most of the time the different
outfits have nothing to do with each other. The visual communication effect does not add up. The next dress
tells another story and that might prove to be more disturbing than effective. If you do want to change, make
sure that all outfits tell the same story and that they are complementary. Perhaps more basic during the prelim-
inary rounds and more exuberant for the final. Or the other way round. Or just change the colour of the materi-

al or the decorations.

€XAMPLES

Manufacturers of premium brands know very well that repetition is needed. They repeat their commercials over and over
again, which helps the brand to appear more reliable. It is one way of making sure that the target groups understand what the
rand is all about. And yes, they do change their commercials. But the core message always stays the same. Nobody mistakes

a Coca Cola commercial for a Pepsi commercial. Would you?

Port Two: The Season

i

reco

The need for constant change is quite specific for baliroom dancing. In other dance Styles, like classical and contemporary
ballet, most choreographers decide upfront how the dancers are dressed. Most of the time their choice is respected for
decades. The costumes for Swan Lake - created by Ivanov and Petipa to Tchaikovsky's music - are still identical to their orig-
inal design. Sometimes new choreographers may change the theme and change the ballet. Like Matthew Bourne did, In his
ballet Swan Lake, the major role is a male swan instead of Odette/ Odile the female swans in the original ballet. Male dancers
also make up the corps de ballet. For this new concept he - of course - had created new costumes for the male swans. They

will probably last a few decades as weil,

The examples above do not suggest that changing is wrong. It is a part of the ballroom institution and is most
likely to stay that way. One of the reasons may be that we see you perform the same dances more often than we
see one ballet piece. We even see the same dance and choreography up to seven times on one day. So some
changes may help you to keep the judges and audiences interested in you. Whenever you feel such a need to

change then be consistent: send the same signals by repeating the design in different ways.

EXAMPLE

Beatrice wore in the preliminary rounds of an important championship a white dress without. feathers or voiles. The design came
from an advertisement for a perfume called ‘Diva’. This dress fitted her personality and made her look beautiful and sensual, The
whole body was covered with diagonal rhinestone lines put on the plissé material, In the final she changed the dress and made
an unexpected twist: she wore an exact copy of the rhinestoned dress but this time without any stones on it. Her dress was just
plain white plissé material. Beatrice made a strong statement and stood out in the crowd. She wanted to show herself and her
dancing and not the dress. This choice fitted her because Beatrice has a personality that likes to innovate.

If Beatrice had been a Succeeder or Aspirer she would probably have made another choice. She would have gone for the main-
stream idea of wearing the most expensive looking dress in the final. She would then have worn the plain dress in the pre-

liminary rounds and changed into the rhinestoned dress for the final...

SIX CRITERIA FOR GOOD APPEARANCES

Criterion one: have your appearance suit your personality

People in each of the seven personality groups used in this book are happy with different starting points to cre-
ate and execute their costumes, make-up and hairstyle.

Differentiation needs to suit your personality’. Being totally different may work well for innovators (Explorers
and Reformers). It might be a way for them to express their being different from the rest. These are the people
who sometimes start new trends. Their ideas may not always be effective in competitions since the majority of
the adjudicators go for the general trend. It is interesting however to help them to find the borders of what is
acceptable and what is not; acceptable for the adjudicators and acceptable to fulfil their personal needs.

Being different certainly does not work so weil for others. Succeeders and Aspirers probably like to be more
inventive in the current fashion style and do it better than others. They want to show their position by having
the most expensive type of outfit you could find. Mainstreamers probably do not care so much about a personal

style. They are happy when others like what they wear and they enjoy social feedback on it. For some, just any

Effective appearances

second-hand outfit might do. Isolated personalities like Strugglers and Resigned feel happiest in following the
general trend. They try to fit in as best they can.

Finally there are dancers who find outfits less important. Their dancing takes all their interest. In such cases their
coach could advise them how to keep up to the required level of quality in their appearance.

EXAMPLE
It is unwise to advise a Succeeder to wear an outrageous and striking newly styled outfit. This may suit the personality of the
designer or the coach but certainly does not help the dancer. The dancer would. feel uneasy in it since she would need some-

thing less different to feel comfortable. The style of the dress would cause her unnecessary stress.

Really interesting cases appear when the male and female do not have the same personality - as is often the case.

Knowing these differences makes it easier to find solutions that help both dancers involved.

EXAMPLE

Rob is clearly an Achiever and his partner Judy is a Mainstreamer. They are not very talented and dance in the pre-champion-

ship class. They are not partners in life. One day Judy tells Rob that she has bought a brilliant dress. He is overexcited until
he finds out that she bought the dress from their dance teacher. Judy liked the dress and bought it because it fitted and because
it was a real bargain. Rob, however, wants to radiate that he is an achiever and any second-hand dress - how beautiful it may
seem - does not fit into his picture, Rob feels let down and Judy doesn’t understand why.

How could this situation have been prevented? They could have talked about what each of them needed before Judy bought
the new dress. Then they could have found out their differences. Maybe Judy would not have bought the dress. It could have
been fine for her to have a dress made as long as it would be financially affordable. The solution could have been that Rob

would have paid the difference between the second hand dress and the tailor made dress.

Criterion two: create harmony between appearance and style of dancing
When your appearance and your interpretation of your dances do not add up, then you send out mixed mes-

sages. This makes you look less reliable.

EXAMPLES
If somebody tells you that the engine of a car is a BMW but the frame of the car looks like a Lada, would you buy that car if
you want a BMW? Most of you would say no. The packaging and the content need to be the same to feel safe.

Ifyou are a more showy, contemporary style Latin American dancer: what would sell your dancing best? A classical style Latin

outfit or a showier styled outfit?

It is important that you find out what your personal interpretation of the dances you perform is. You need to
know this to develop your dancing and you also need to know it to be able to find the right ‘packaging’ for your
product (the right appearance for your personal dance style). You will find information on how to develop a per-

sonal style in Part Three of this book.

Part Two: The Season

i

MISTAKES YOU COULD EASILY PREVENT ARE FOR INSTANCE

Showy Latin dresses in combination with a clearly rhythmical style of dancing.

A dance concept which contains contemporary choreography while the style of dress and make-up is classical Latin.
Standard couples with a high quality of free flow and good use of space wearing heavy dresses that clearly are in conflict with
their dancing style.

Authentic South American style Latin dresses for female dancers with a classical, technical approach.

Criterion three: show body assets and conceal weaknesses

‘Know your physical limits and assets well’ may sound logical. However, we often find proof that people don’t
know what suits them best. We are not all fashion specialists. And mistakes do not only happen to the lower
ranked dancers. You may see a top professional Latin dancer and think that she is overweight. A dress can eas-
ily make a woman look like that while in fact she is a slim as can be. What might have gone wrong for her?
The shape of the dress could be wrong for her. She might just have designed the dress to be as full of the most
expensive rhinestones as possible... She might have selected the wrong colour. Each colour tells its own story
and not each colour suits everybody's build and every complexion. Even her choice of material could have influ-

enced the effect of the dress in a negative way. It is unwise to just follow the trend without thinking.

EXAMPLES:

In the late seventies the Standard dancer's fashion trend was having velvet tops on ballroom gowns. All female dancers wore
it. Whether they were slim or not. And this material only suited the slim ones...

In the eighties Latin dress-skirts made of threads became fashion. It looked good on the world champion but not on many
other dancers...

In the early nineties feathers seemed to be a must for ballroom dresses: it was the only way to portray flow and lightness. In

fact, on many dresses the feathers created such overkill that they made the dancers look heavier...

MISTAKES YOU COULD EASILY PREVENT ARE FOR INSTANCE

Overweight female dancers in dresses that show that excess weight.

Male Standard dancers with a curved back or a little overweight wearing a white cumberbund or waistcoat and thus showing
such weaknesses.

Short male Standard dancers having a high collar on their black jacket, giving the appearance of a shorter neck and a short
er total impression:

Female dancers with quite big feet dancing in shining silver shoes and creating an even bigger foot impression.

Short female dancers visually breaking their neckline by wearing heavy necklaces or scarves. These accessories visually break
their vertical body axis and create a shorter impression.

Male Latin dancers wearing very tight trousers to show their muscular legs while suffering under it: the tight trousers may

give them less freedom to move around...

€ffective appearances

ell

Griterion four: show dancing assets and conceal dancing weaknesses

Next to your physical assets and weaknesses it is important to also know your dancing qualities very well. What
would you like to hide and what would you like to accentuate.

MISTAKES YOU COULD EASILY PREVENT ARE FOR INSTANCE

Female Standard dancers who have a problem with the shoulder line (for example the right shoulder pulling up a little bit}. They do not hide
that problem by wearing a dress with an open top line or a top line made out of body stocking. They may consider wearing a top in the
colour of the jacket of their partner: black. Her black shoulder line would now fade out against his black shoulders. Her ‘problem’ would be
noticed less easily. Another suggestion could be to cover the ‘problemshoulder’ with a voile hanging over it.

Female Latin dancers who are too slow and not Slexible wearing dresses that hardly move at all. Some coaches say: 'If the

woman can’t move then have the skirt move.
Female Latin dancers who are not well grounded confirm their lightness by wearing shoes ina light skin colour {while a dark-
er colour might give the impression of more grounding);

Female Latin dancers who are not well grounded confirm their lightness by wearing dresses with designs in the vertical axis
(while horizontal designs might give the impression of more grounding for them);

Men with a weak body posture wearing loose Latin blouses and oversized trousers. Maybe in order to try to conceal that weak-
ness while in fact they accentuate it;

Women with too little leg control in Latin wearing mini skirts that clearly show their inabilities in @ split second.

Criterion five: create a balanced impression
You are not dancing on your own. Together you are a team that needs to be in balance. Some appearances may

work for you solo but in combination with your partner some physical features need to be compensated.

MISTAKES YOU COULD EASILY PREVENT ARE FOR INSTANCE

Too tall femate dancers (in relation to their partners} wearing dresses which make them look even taller,

Too tall female dancers (in relation to their partners} having blond hair which makes them look even taller.

Dresses with asymmetric voiles hanging down from the shoulders or the arms creating an unbalanced impression.
(Standard) dresses with too many voiles that take away the Shape of the performers and almost become a 'whiripool” around
the dancers. This conceals too much of the movements and choreography.

Female Latin dancers wearing dresses that have no connection whatsoever with what the man is wearing (in style, colour,
material, etc.)

All kinds of aspects of the appearance that do not suit the chosen dance concept. The dress, the hatr, the make-up, the shoes,
the jewellery, the colours, the materials and the decorations should add up and not be in contrast with each other. For ex
ample: do not combine a traditionally styled ballroom gown with very modem shoes and a contemporary haircut in a striking

colour, Or do not wear a ‘rough’ Tiger dress in combination with a very classical, fixed hairdo.

Criterion six: stay in good taste

It would be impossible to describe what is good taste and what’s not. Some might find a carrot coloured hairdo

Part Two: The Season

offensive while others find it amusing. It depends on your background and personality. On one point some uni-
versal comments can be made: your make-up.

The ballroom dance world has its own rules. This cannot mean that it should place itself completely outside the
rest of the world. We need approval from the outside world to stay in existence. We need a constant influx of
new dancers and we need funds to sponsor our events,

Outsiders have - more often than not - a negative image of competitive dancing. A lot has to do with the outfits
but also with the make-up and hairstyling. Some dancers paint their faces in a way that makes them look more
like transvestites. Hardly any parent would be proud to see their daughter walk in the streets with all that make-
up and that hairdo. More make-up is not necessarily better.

Make-up is an art in itself. It is a skill you need to master as a competitive dancer. During their many years of
training, ballet dancers have regular make-up lessons to learn to do good make-up themselves. The outside world
hardly complains about the make-up of ballet dancers... So maybe it is a good idea for you to take make-up les-

sons. Make-up is an art that needs training too. And not only for the female dancers.

STEPS TOWARDS AN EFFECTIVE APPEARANCE

The following steps help you to create an effective appearance and prevent many of the depicted mistakes:

Analysis of your present condition

* Analyse and describe the assets and weaknesses of your body and of your dancing.

* Determine the dance style you want to portray.

* Analyse the physical compensations you might need between the two of you.

* Analyse your personal wardrobe (check dominant colours and materials you use and check the decoration

styles and designs you normally like).

The first creative phase

* Buy fashion magazines and cut out all the pictures that appeal to you. Both you and your partner could do
this. Cut out anything like hairstyles, jewellery, make-up, materials, colours, sportswear, eveningwear and
beachwear. Cut out anything that appeals to you. Get as many clippings as possible.

¢ Group these clippings into consistent fashion styles.

© Compare the fashion styles of the two of you: do you have common tastes? Are there tastes your partner real-
ly cannot live with? Discard the groups which do not appeal to both of you.

* Compare the fashion styles with the desired dance style; discard the groups that do not suit the dance style.

* Compare the fashion styles with your assets and weaknesses (both in body and in dance}; discard the groups
that do not suit your body.

* You now probably have one or two style groups left.

The second creative phase

+ Buy new fashion magazines and only look for pictures that suit the selected fashion style(s).

Effective appeorances

* Make a mood board of all correct pictures within one style. A mood board is a collage of all the pictures on
one piece of cardboard.

¢ Analyse the mood board(s) and write down the criteria for your long-term dance style.

EXAMPLES

The long-term style requirements are: plain, contemporary, dark colours {to minimize the endomorph body), short skirt and accen-
tuating the vertical body axis.

The long-term style requirements are: sensual, showing the body within good taste, low back, no stones but real jewellery, not
accentuating the vertical body axis (partner is not too tall compared to her).

The long-term style requirements are: classical style, skirt covering the knees, accentuating the hips and busts, the design should

create a curved silhouette (square torso of the female dancer).

¢ You might have found a picture by now that can be easily translated into a Standard or Latin dress. If not,

you have the right input to design your own or to brief your dressmaker for a design.

The check up phase
¢ Check your findings and designs with your primary team or coach before you invest in having the dress made.
This might prevent some mistakes. Others are often better able to see your assets and weaknesses.

© Order the dress when all invoived agree that the design matches all six criteria for good appearances.

The follow up phase

© Once you have done all previous steps, creating new dresses becomes easier.

* Evaluate each dress before ordering a new one. Make notes on how to improve the dress for the next time.
The changes could be minimal (an inch higher or lower on the waistline).

« Make photos of each dress - front and back - so you can build up a file for your dressmaker and yourself. It
is easier for you to evaluate your own dress on a photo rather than in front of a mirror...

« You can make hundreds of variations on the basic design. You can change the colours, the decorations, the
materials and the accessories.

You can order more dresses in advance. It saves you the worry of getting your dresses in time.

ONE STYLE IS NOT BORING
Don't be afraid that your appearance will be boring. Your outfits are getting better each time (since you learn from
them). This will make you feel more secure. Furthermore, the repetition helps to create your desired image. And

finally, you can vary as much as you like in the details and that will give you a new appearance each time.

Part Two: The Season

YOUR BODY NEEDS THE RIGHT FUEL

Healthy, sensible nutrition is a key factor in your dance sport career. Your body is your instrument and plays a
leading role during the dance. Realize that undernourishment and a shortage of nutrients and liquids do not
stimulate your body to. perform well. Also the link between mind and body suffers under such shortages!

A top competitor needs super fuel. As a top competitor you must be willing to adjust your diet and lifestyle. It is
the process of becoming conscious of this that demands a lot of discipline. But it will prevent weakness during
performances and - even moré importantly - it prevents serious diseases like anorexia and bulimia.

It is important that your body produces the right amount of energy for a proper practice plan and good perform-
ance during competitions.:An energy imbalance will lead to an increase in body fat or a loss of body fat and
muscle, Both a positive and a negative energy balance may affect your level of performance.

Little research has been done on the actual use of energy by competitive dancers. This chapter merely gives some

basic insights into important parameters for dancers based on literature! and on advice from Zenner’,

PERSONAL ADVICE

Uf you feel you need personal guidance on this subject you should visit your doctor or dietician. Make sure this health profes-
sional is familiar with sports since there is a significant difference in advice on diets for general health and diets Sor provid-
ing a healthy body with more energy. Even the kind of sport you do makes a difference to the dlet you need. Suitable diet-
icians are mostly found at Olympic or national sport centres and at professional dance institutes. Some Sports doctors are also

spectaltzed in diets.

BECOME AWARE OF YOUR HABITS

You may practise hard to prepare yourself for major championships. You may take many lessons and spend a for-
tune on outfits. But something will go wrong if you eat hamburgers and French fries for lunch on your competi-
tion day. Or if you drink soft drinks - or even worse - alcoholic beverages. Your preparations may have been fine
but the outcome will be affected. It is like having your mechanic prepare your beautiful Ferrari but when you
start to drive your car you put diesel in the fuel tank (instead of super petrol). Your car won’t perform well for
long... Of course not everybody eats hamburgers or drinks alcoholic beverages on competition days, but some
awareness of your intake is helpful. Have an honest look at your eating pattern and see if you can make some

improvements.

EXAMPLE

Nasser and Zara were preparing for a world championship. Part of the strategy was entering a competition in the country of
this championship. They danced there to check out some new skills with other couples on the floor. Nasser and Zara wanted
to simulate stress by having others in the way. They also wanted to become familiar with the audience before the champion-
ship took place. Since there were no real contestants they won the competition easily. However their coach and they them-
selves were disappointed in the quality of the performance. During the training camp they performed extremely well but none
of that was seen during the competition. Nasser and Zara took some time to evaluate what went wrong. They used the com-

petition evaluation questionnaire in Chapter 1-9. This questionnaire didn’t really help them to find out what went wrong. They

NUTRIENTS IN OUR DAILY FOOD

Daily food and beverages contain the following important elements: carbohydrates (sugars), fats, proteins, vita-
mins, minerals, water and waste products.

Carbohydrates are to be found in food such as bread, potatoes, pasta, rice and, of course, in sugary substances
like sugar, honey, jam and alcohol. The combustion of carbohydrates helps your body during intense muscle use

because it helps provide a higher intake of oxygen.

€ssential are polyunsaturated fatty acids like linoleic acid that cannot be produced by the body itself. You find
these unsaturated fatty acids in butter, margarine and oils. Fats build up important energy storage for long-last-
ing energy (and of course periods of hunger), Fats also have the ability to dissolve vitamins A, D, E and K, which

makes it possible to absorb these vitamins.

Amino acids are necessary and you get them from the intake of protein. They are an important Part of the struc-
ture of ceils and tissues. You also need proteins to restore hormones, enzymes, the immune system and blood.

Proteins that are not used for restoring and building up your body are used as an energy supply.

Vitamins are important for the growth, preservation and reproduction of mankind. They are also necessary for

your metabolism. Natural sources for vitamins are found in, for instance, fruit, vegetables, fish, meat and milk,

Minerals are inorganic substances which we need to sustain our metabolism. Iron, fluorine and iodine are impor-
tant minerals that are not always available in your regular nutrition. You may need to check that. Many miner-

als can be found in juices and mineral water.

Your body consists of up to 70% water. Water is the transport and solvent system for many important substances.
It is needed for the regulation of your body temperature and it is necessary for many chemical reactions in your
body. Nutrients can only enter (and leave) the cells via water solvents. Your body loses moisture continuously
(as a result of breathing and sweating). It is therefore vital that you compensate for the loss of moisture by drink-

ing water, tea or juice to maintain the right balance.

Food and beverages cannot be consumed for one hundred per cent. The waste products are the substances your

body cannot digest. Normally they will leave your body in a natural way.
The groups of nutritional elements are put into the following chart to give you an overview of their tasks:

Nutrition notes

ENERGY SUPPLIERS FOR COMPETITIVE BALLROOM DANCERS

The consumption of calories for combined arts and sports - like ballroom dancing - has not been researched thor-
oughly as yet. The research available also gives varying results. By and large, dance sport is not regarded as a
sport that needs an extreme or explosive use of calories like, for example, weightlifting. The general opinion is
that competitive bailroom dancers only need a normal healthy balanced diet based on three main nutrients in

the following proportions’:

CHECK YOUR BODY WEIGHT
Your body weight influences your physical condition. There is an easy method to calculate your optimum since
there is a distinct relationship between your body length and your body weight. Having your weight around the

optimum not only increases your ability to perform well but also prevents you from ‘unnecessarily disturbing your

metabolism. At present the BodyMassIndex (BMI) is commonly used to determine the desired optimum.

You can calculate your Body-Mass-Index as follows:

EXAMPLE

A dancer with a body weight of 78 kilos and a body length of 1.78 metres has a BMI of: 78 divided by (1.78 x 1,78}.
This equals 78 divided by 3.1684 = a BMI of 24.62.

INTERPRETATION OF THE BMI

Body Moss Index

Nutrition notes

It is fashionable for competitive dancers (like most dancers in other styles) to pursue an ideal weight that is quite
i I low. Slender seems to be more aesthetically attractive than a little or more fat. Some dancers - and not only the
women - therefore suffer under a strict dietary regime to prevent comments from partners and teachers or to
compensate for the self-image that does not match their ideal image.

i With the BMI you can calculate if your ideas and/or the feedback from others is correct. The ideal BMI for com-
petitive dancers should be low for aesthetic reasons but your BMI should never drop under 20. If your BMI is

lower this is a definite indication that your body is in need of nutrition. Some of the functions of your body will

be affected, and this has a negative influence on your performance. A BMI of 25 or higher is not only unhealthy

but also does not heip you to create an aesthetic presentation of yourself and your dancing.

CHECK YOUR FLUID INTAKE

Your body is composed of up to. 70% water. An average of 60% water is normal. During sports this water level

automatically decreases. Your’ body sweats more than in an inactive state. In this sweat you can detect substances
like sodium, magnesium and calcium. The body needs all these substances that are decreased by profuse
sweating.

Water shortage causes blood thickening. The effect is that oxygen and nutrients can no longer be supplied to the
cells efficiently. Also the transport of waste products from the cells starts to malfunction. Furthermore the body's

heat regulation begins to suffer. Overheating can be the result. This can even lead to collapse from heat exhaustion.

| During sports, dehydration cannot be prevented. In some extreme cases the loss of fluid can be as much as 8%
fl of body weight. For a person weighing 70 kilos this means a loss of 5.6 litres. Up to 2% loss of water does not
damage performance. More than 2% could affect the ability to perform. Above 3% it is certain that the ability to
ye perform decreases. In the case of a person weighing 70 kilos the loss of more than 2.1 litres therefore affects
j | performance.

The maximal loss of sweat is about 1.5 to 2 litres per hour. Realize however that your stomach and intestines can

only absorb | litre per hour. Consequently, drinking more than 1 litre per hour is not effective at all. Regulate your

intake during the whole competition day and make sure that you start the day with optimal fluidity.

Some people lose more water than others. On average it is estimated that a dancer loses 3 litres of water during

a competition. It could be wise to check your average loss of water so you know how much you need to com-

pensate.

TEST YOURSELF

Simulate a dance competition at your own studio. Put the heating up quite high since most ballrooms tend to have an
unhealthy high temperature. Weigh yourself before you start the simulation (in your underwear). Simulate 3 rounds, each con-
sisting of 5 dances. Take normal breaks in between, as you would have in your competition. Do not drink during the test.
Notice how (unpleasantly) your body starts to react. Weigh yourself 15 minutes after your simulation. Again in your underwear
only, Calculate the weight difference from start to finish. 85 % of your lass of weight is water loss. The remaining 15% is weight

loss caused by your use of energy.

+

You now know the amount of water you need to compensate for in three rounds. You are now able to calculate
the expected total loss during a championship that lasts more rounds (for instance The Open British, which

sometimes has seven rounds up to the final for new entries).

WHAT SHOULD YOU DRINK?

Carbohydrate drinks help to increase the sugar level in your blood, which helps to increase performance. Many
sports brands sell these isotonic drinks. Although they may taste good when you drink them ice cold, it is bet-
ter to drink them at room temperature. The warmer the drink the less energy your body needs to adapt it to its
own temperature.

Some of you find it hard to drink the amount you really need. So start to train yourself to drink the necessary
amounts during practice sessions. Varying the tastes of isotonic drinks (so you will not get bored) also helps. If
you decide not to consume isotonic drinks you are advised to use supplements that are normally found in iso-
tonic drinks. You could drink a variety of the following: room temperature or medium-hot mineral water (to
change the taste you can add fruit juice); fresh fruit juices at room temperature; hot broth, hot soups and hot

tea in different flavours.

MAGNESIUM

As you sweat your body loses substances like sodium, magnesium and calcium. But your body still needs all
these substances. Sodium and calcium are easy to add to your daily diet (with cooking salt) so problems need
not arise. Magnesium is another matter. Magnesium is found in vegetables, potatoes and corn products. Since
you do not always consume food like this during dance events, it may be necessary to take supplements.
Magnesium is needed for muscle contractions as well as for an optimal combined action of your nerves and
muscles. Muscular aches and pains or cramp could occur if your body lacks magnesium. But taking too much

magnesium could also have unwanted side effects, so only take the recommended amounts.

VITAMINS

Make sure your vitamin intake is right. A vitamin deficiency certainly affects your ability to perform well. An
excess of vitamins is not helpful (unless you have a vitamin deficiency). When the right ievel is reached the extra
vitamins have no extra positive effect - in fact, some vitamins can be harmful if taken in high doses, such as vita-
min A, D and Bo.

Competitive dancers should pay special attention to two of these vitamins. A vitamin B1 deficiency causes fatigue,
constipation and/or loss of appetite. A vitamin C deficiency makes your body more susceptible to infection and

decreases your body’s ability to recover. The next chart shows the amounts of vitamins a body uses daily:

Nutrition notes

CARBOHYDRATES

Carbohydrates are stored as a glycogen. This store is limited to a maximum of 11/2 hours. During intense prac-
tising and competitions your body will use carbohydrates as fuel. So carbohydrates are the most important source
of energy for dancers. The problem, however, is that your body can only store a limited amount of carbohydrates
as glycogen in the liver and muscles. When these glycogen stores are exhausted, your body starts to burn fat and
protein. You will then only be performing at 50% of your maximum ability. You start to suffer from fatigue, your
legs feel hollow and you can’t push yourself any more.

You can prevent these problems by following a high-carbohydrate diet. The glycogen store is usually enough for
a dancer to dance intensely for 1 to 11/2 hours. If the competition or practising takes longer than 11/2 hours,
then extra carbohydrates are needed during the practice or competition. It is important to maintain the glycogen

level for a competition or practice by consuming high-carbohydrate foods.

GENERAL DIETS FOR PRACTICE AND COMPETITIONS

Consume food which is easy to digest. Food that takes a long time to get through your digestive system will make

you feel tired.
High-carbohydrate diets are quite popular among sportspeople and competitive dancers. Diets like these have the

Part Two: The Season

advantages of providing increased stamina and more energy. But the side effects are that they may also cause fer-
mentation in the intestine, gas production and diarrhoea. These diets also tend to increase volume, making you
feel ’full’. You can avoid this feeling by selecting a carbohydrate with a low water content, like biscuits, choco-

late or fruit juices (although these can make you feel thirsty).

VEGETARIANS
A purely vegetarian diet has its drawbacks for competitive dancers. The amount of biological proteins is mostly

too low. This could cause problems with the body’s need for iron.

DANCER'S DIET

According to Michael Hamm the optimal diet for practice and competitions could be as follows:

If you take your competitive dance sport seriously, you will see a dietician at least once a year to have a tailor-

made diet designed for you.

NUTRITIONAL SUGGESTIONS FOR PRACTISING

According to Zenner competitive dancers are advised to consider the following intake when practising: eat car-
bohydrate-rich food to increase your intake of energy (carbohydrates supply 60% of your energy). Limit fats as
they only provide about 20-30% of your energy. Eat sufficient protein-rich food (1.2-1.5 gram per kilogram body
weight). Eat a varied diet to make sure you get the vitamins and minerals you need. Check your intake of flu-

ids for the right level of hydration. And time your meals well,

NUTRITIONAL SUGGESTIONS FOR THE COMPETITION DAY

According to Zenner competitive dancers ate advised to consider the following intake when competing: have
your last meal two to three hours before your competition (pasta, rice, bread, fruit). Make sure you drink suffi-
ciently when eating carbohydrates to prevent stomach and intestinal disorders. Drink plenty of fluid until the
competition starts (drinks without carbohydrates). Drink one to two cups of a thirst-quencher/ water during the
last 10 minutes before you dance, Drink 500 to 750 ml water per hour during the competition and eat carbo-

hydrates during the competition. Do this after one hour’s exertion (a banana, pasta, bread, sponge cake, etc.}.
NUTRITIONAL SUGGESTIONS FOR A QUICK RECOVERY
After your exertion (practice, competition, show} it is vital that the loss of fluid and carbohydrates is quickly

replenished. Make sure you take in enough liquids to stimulate the recovery of your body's hydration and to

Nutrition notes

assist the transport of waste products. Eat plenty of carbohydrate-rich foods to restore your carbohydrate supplies

(2 grams of carbohydrates per kilogram body weight within 2 hours after exertion),

i. NOTE

A Carbohydrate intake in your muscles is the most rapid during the first two hours after exertion.

NUTRITIONAL SUGGESTIONS FOR FOREIGN TRIPS

Competitions, lessons and practice abroad are in unfamiliar surroundings. Some situations may put you off bal-

ance physically, mentally and emotionally. Disturbances can be the new environment itself, the new climate, the
written or unwritten rules of the new society, time differences and even nutrition.

The following suggestions help you to prevent stomach and intestinal disorders caused by nutritional problems:

Do not use ice cubes nor drink ice cold drinks,

Do not eat unwashed fruit/ vegetables.

Avoid consuming food that has been displayed in shop counters or snack bars.

Avoid eating food that you are not familiar with.

Part Two: The Season

TEN EXERCISES DURING: THE SEASON

The exercises presented in this chapter help to increase the effect of all your efforts during a season (taking les-
sons, practising, planning, competing). Some of these exercises can also help. you during a competition day like
the suggested breathing and relaxation techniques. Other exercises can help you. when you work on your long-

term career planning (like building up a mental library and doing creative visualization).

SECTION 1: WARM-UP

1
Good warm-ups more than prepare your muscles for the physical work you are about to do. In this section you
read about how to prepare yourself as a total human being for dancing: physically, mentally, emotionally and even

spiritually. 1

SECTION 2: BREATHING TECHNIQUES

The largest muscle in our bédy is the diaphragm. This muscle regulates your breathing. Singers know that con-~

trol over this muscle is essential to produce quality singing. The same applies to dancing: the better the use of

the diaphragm the better the quality of your dancing. Good breathing techniques also release mental and emo-
1

tional stress. In section 2 you can read about how to increase your control over the diaphragm and how this con-

trol can help you to create a more personal interpretation of your dances.

SECTION 3: HANDICAP TRAINING

Many of you rely a lot on mirrors during your practice. This increases your visual feedback system but - at the
same time - prevents the development of other skills like awareness of touch, sensing the muscle use, sensing
the skin, creating inner balance and leading and following. The Handicap Model is an easy way to strengthen

such skills.

SECTION 4: BUILDING UP A MENTAL LIBRARY

In chapters 2-1 and 2-5 suggestions were made to plan your season and divide it into blocks - each handling one
subject matter. Your teachers can help you to find the right exercises to develop the required skills for a subject
matter like ‘posture’ or ’rhythm’ or ’dynamics’. You may develop the desired skills much quicker if you have the
correct images in your head’. In this section some suggestions are made on how to improve and increase your

mental library.

SECTION 5: RELAXATION TECHNIQUES,

In Part One of this book, the importance of relaxing before dancing has been stated more than once. Step 3 of
The Nine Step Connection Model® clearly shows how the Cleaning Breath not only helps you to relax your body
but also to relax your mind in just a few seconds. Sometimes you might need more intense relaxation. In this

section you will find some suggestions.

Part Two: The Season

SECTION 6: MARKING YOUR CHOREOGRAPHY

There are times when you may want to run through your choreography before you actually perform. For instance
just before you have a lesson, a practice, a first round of a competition or a show. Most dancers run through
their steps to refresh their memory before performing. This is a mental and/or physical approach. In section 6

you will find information and exercises for activating your emotional and spiritual levels as well.

SECTION 7: CREATIVE VISUALIZATION
Creative visualization means creating the desired picture/movie of the future in your mind before it happens.
Over the last few years scientific research has shown that such creative visualizations do work on the physical
plane. Just to mention one example of research in this area: three groups of students were tested on the strength
of the muscles in their index fingers. The first group was asked to strongly wiggle their index fingers every day
for a few minutes. The second group was asked not to exercise their index fingers at all but imagine every day
~ for a few minutes that they did. The third group - the control group - was not given an exercise. To the Surprise
of the researchers both the first and the second group showed increased muscle strength in their index fingers.
The students in the control group showed no change at all. Of course the increase in the first group - which real-
ly did the physical work - was larger than the increase in the second group. However a significant increase was
found in the second group.
So it is safe to state that creative visualization at least works on the physical plane. There are, however, more
and more people who also believe it improves their lives mentally, emotionally and spiritually. What do you have

to lose? For more information read section 7.

SECTION 8: ENERGETIC LINKAGE
If you ask me what the essence of ballroom dancing is, I would probably say that it is about two people sharing

moments of movement in dance to music. The origin of our dances stems from ballrooms and bars where men

were trying to communicate their interest and desire in women through dance. Those were sociatizing and con-

necting moments. And these moments ate still happening, although the dances may have changed. Just look at
behaviour in discos nowadays ... In these social circumstances we act as human beings: we not only use our
muscles to behave interestingly, we also try to personally connect with our partner. When the connection is
made we feel much safer and happier. In section 8 we have an in-depth look at the different ways of connect-

ing with people and how these ways can clearly influence our dancing. You have a choice there...

SECTION 9: VOICE DIALOGUE

Most of the exercises in this book handle issues related to our physical, mental and emotional plane. Sometimes
we even address spiritual practices. Being balanced on all four levels of human being helps us to find our essence
and find our power. Sometimes emotional processes are avoided because we do not know how to handle our emo-
tions. We try to cover them up in the hope that the emotional discomfort disappears by itself. Emotional healing
and self-control cannot be found in such ways. Deeper personal work is needed to balance yourself. In section 9

Voice Dialogue is introduced as a therapeutic way of dealing with this aspect.

Part Two: The Season

Section 2-8-1

Warm-up

PHYSICAL PREPARATION

Warming up is regarded as an essential preparation for the physical body to perform well when extra effort is
demanded. Without this warming up your body could feel threatened and therefore you would prompt your
Autonomous Nervous System to interfere. Stress is due to occur,

A good physical warm-up slowly increases the speed of your blood circulation through the veins. All crucial
muscles need to be warmed up gradually before the real practice/performance starts.

Physical warm-ups not only prepare the muscles but also the heart, the lungs and the tendons. Good warm-ups
make sure that all these four body parts are well prepared.

Stretching your muscles might be part of your physica! preparation programme. Do note that stretching always

follows after the warm-up and not before! Never stretch when your muscles are cold.

NOT ONLY FOR COMPETITIONS

Warm-ups should not only mark the start of the competition but of every practice. And there is an even more
important reason for warming up: competitions do come with stress. Under stress you fall back into old habits.
In fact, this is how the Autonomous Nervous System helps you. So, if you do not regularly warm up your body
during practices, you will probably not do it well on a competition day.

if you do a warm-up before a lesson starts you get more out of that lesson too. Without a warm-up it is quite nor-
mal that you dance better at the end of a lesson than in the beginning. This gives you the feeling that you have
learned something - while in fact it could be that your body has just warmed up and that makes you perform bet-

ter. You could get more out of your lessons if you do the warming up first. If you then improve, you really might

have learned something.

HOW MUCH TIME?
For top international dancers the first warming up of the day normally takes thirty to sixty minutes. Second and
third warm-ups (after breaks caused by the schedule of the event) are often done in ten to fifteen minutes.

A reasonable warm-up for other couples would take about twenty minutes. Second and third warm-ups could be

done in ten minutes.
WHAT IS A PROPER DRESS CODE FOR WARM-UPS?

It goes without saying that the physical warm-up should not be done in competition outfits. Ballroom dresses and

tails do not provide enough freedom to move in. Latin dresses are too bare to keep the body warm. Furthermore

Exercises: Worm-up

the body starts to perspire and you probably want to keep your competition outfits as impeccable as possible.
It is acommon misunderstanding among athletes that cotton is a good material for warm-up outfits. This mater-
ial absorbs perspiration. It makes the material a quick conductor of temperature.; Any draft in the room imme-
diately affects the temperature. of your body... Physical warm-up outfits should cbnsist of two parts: a tight fit-
ting under-layer made of a Bolyester fabric and a looser fitting upper-layer. For the upper-layer polyester or light
fleece are appropriate. These fabrics transport excess water into the air. | :

Flexible sport shoes give you more freedom in warm-up exercises. A ballroom style specific warming up - which
follows after the physical warming up - demands other outfits. Now it is appropriate to start wearing your regu-
lar competition shoes.

Standard dancing men may want to feel the high collar of a competition shirt as an extension of the body (instead
of a restriction). These men could wear a high collar when doing a Standard specific warming up (and during
the whole practice). Women may want to feel skirts around their legs to simulate the feeling they have in their

competition outfit.

BEST SPOTS FOR WARMING UPS

Fresh air helps to condition the body. Therefore you could start your warm-up outside the venue with some run-
ning, jogging and stretching exercises if this is possible. Most of the time you have to do your warming up entire-
ly inside the venue.

Fortunately you do not need a lot of space. Four square metres are sufficient. Most dressing rooms provide such

space. Do not forget to bring a towel and/or an exercise mattress with you.

INSECURE FEELINGS?

Lots of you feel the need to warm up but also feel insecure doing it because it is not a common thing to do. You
could help yourself by having a fixed routine. You could even wear a personal stereo to make you feel more at
ease. As a couple you can help each other by doing the same routine together at the same time. Together you

will feel stronger and it will have a positive effect on your team building.

MENTAL AND EMOTIONAL WARM-UP .
fin effective warm-up not only prepares you physically. As human beings we are more than just our bodies! Some
mental and emotional preparations are necessary too to perform well. You need some mental concentration to
take you out of the thoughts you have up to that moment. Concentrating on the warm-up gives your mind no
chance to think of any other stressful thoughts. It demands full concentration on the body itself, so the mind
focuses on something you can control. This decreases your stress level and brings. you back into your own body.
Emotionally you also need to prepare yourself to want to share the dance with your partner. There may be many
reasons why you do not want to do so at that time. Maybe you have had a tough day/week at work and need
some private time. Maybe you are worrying about your friends or family members. Maybe the two of you have
had a quarrel. Maybe your body is too tired. Maybe you are worrying about having a good practice or competi-

tion day. All such thoughts block your partnering ability. You first need to balance yourself before you are ready

Part Two: The Season

to share a dance with somebody else. If you feel you just have to pull yourself together and start dancing, your emo-
tions will still get in the way. You first need to get back into your body and clean your thoughts. A A physical warm-
up with a high mental concentration helps. Strong breathing during your physical warm-up helps to restore emo-
tional balance (in the short term).

Physical and mental preparation is individual work. You need time to find yourself before you start sharing your-
self with somebody else.

You need to connect with the dancer inside you. Does this dancer really want to dance our standardized dances
with a focus on technical skills? Or does your dancer want to express the emotions which comes with moving
to the music? Don’t we all have a wonderful dancer inside ourselves who wants to express itself freely? Part of
your warming up programme could/should include an activation of that dancer. Express yourself in a free style
solo dance. Image yourself being Fred Astaire or Ginger Rogers or any other great dancer. How would he or she
move to a Waltz or a Rumba? Just take a few minutes to find that expressive dancer inside you.

At some point you will feel you are ready to share the dance with your partner. An abrupt getting together ( ‘Let's
get in the dance hold’ does not respect your emotional needs. You need time to get used to having somebody
in your private space, to feel comfortable with that. Some kind of ‘getting to know each other in our private
Space’ emotionally helps you. Dance improvisations - starting without hold - will show how close you can be to
your partner. Respect the time you and your partner need to accept touch and finally dance with physical con-

nections. Most of the time it wiil take just a few minutes.

SUGGESTED WARMING UP PROGRAMME
The following warming up programme incorporates not only physical preparations but also handles mental and

emotional elements. The total programme brings you more in contact with yourself, makes you less stressed and

helps to create a good relationship with your partner.

At first glance, this holistic programme may seem elaborate (which it is). However - as soon as the programme
has become a routine - it will be easier to do.

Start with one of the four parts. As soon as you feel comfortable with this, add a new part and so on: until you
have contro! over all four parts. Taking up all four parts at once would probably increase (mental and emotion-

al) stress instead of reducing it.

€xercises: Warm-up

WARMING UP PART ONE: ACTIVATE ALL YOUR BODY PARTS :

As a dancer you need to make contact with all your body parts consciously before you start using them. Gabrielle
Roth has developed a fabulous way of connecting with your body, mind, emotions and soul. This is called Five
Rhythm dancing. She has written interesting books' about her work and aiso created helpful music CDs to put
her ideas into practice. For your warming up you could use four of the five rhythms Roth uses to activate your

body parts.

This part of the warm-up works as follows: Select music? which corresponds to the four rhythms and which

inspires you. Each piece takes about four minutes except ’Chaos’. This rhythm may last a little shorter.

FLOWING

Start walking around. Keep your focus to yourself. Breathe the rhythm of the music during the whole dance.

Every 30 seconds you activate one body part only, starting with your feet and ankles and ending with your head. You activate
your feet and ankles by improvising movements with them. What can you do with them? Point and flex your feet, wiggle your
toes, circulate a free foot, play with weight transfer (feel the weight on the ball of your feet and move, feel your weight at your
heels and move, etc.) The following thirty seconds you improvise with your knees. What can you do with them? Stretch them,
bend them, twist them, make rondees, etc., etc. Then you move on to improvise movements with your hips, then with your
torso, then with your shoulders, then with your elbows, then with your hands and wrists, then with your neck (be gentle!) and
finally you improvise movements with your, face.

During the whole exercise you keep on travelling over the floor. Make sure you have covered the whole floor: every corner,
every side of the room, the centre of the floor etc.

Let all your movements come from your breath. For instance: your exhalations may make you move more downwards and your
inhalations may make you move more upwards.

Move with the dynamics the music offers: ‘heavy’, ‘flexible’ and ‘free’. Your movements have no beginnings nor endings. Each

movement evoives into the next without sharp changes.

Part Two: The Season

STACCATO

Keep walking around. Keep your focus to yourself. Breathe the rhythm of the music during the whole ‘dance.

Every 30 seconds you activate another body part, starting again with your feet and ankles and ending with your head.

The music may inspire you to express movements that indicate strong opinions like "Yes’ or 'No’.

During the whole exercise you keep on travelling over the floor.

Let all your movements come from your breath.

Move with the dynamics the music offers: ’strong’, ‘direct’ and ’sudden’. Your movements have clear beginnings and end-

ings. Each movement sharply changes into the next.

cHAOS

Keep moving around. Keep your focus to yourself.

Breathe the rhythm of the music during the whole dance.

Forget about your body parts now. They are already activated. Just take in the fast rhythm and move to that.

The music is so fast that you can no longer think what to do. Give in to the speed and breathe and move freely. Mix all kinds

of dynamics spontaneously. Give up control!

LYRICAL

‘Chaos’ has taken a lot of energy from you. It is now time to keep moving at a much slower speed. Keep your focus to your:
self. Breathe the slow rhythm of the music.

Regulate your breath: make it slower and deeper.

The music is so slow that you now can express yourself in a poetic, gentle way. Just follow your breath gracefully and soothe

yourself.

You have now completed this part of the holistic warm-up. You will feel more grounded, you will have more
contact with all your body parts, you will notice a starker focus with your eyes, a clear fresh mind and - but not
least - a warmed-up body. You are ready to move on to the next step of the holistic warming up programme or

- if you feel you need to - start stretching and/or add some workout exercises.

WARMING UP PART TWO: BALLROOM DANCE EXPRESSION

Your body and mind are now getting ready to dance. Let’s make a gentle transition from what we did so freely
to the strict ballroom dances.

Change your clothes and shoes for ballroom dancing now.

Put on your favourite music for one of the ten ballroom dances.

Start dancing to this music on your own for three minutes (one track of a CD). Keep your focus to yourself. Breathe the rhythm
of the music during the whole dance. Express the selected dance as if you were Fred Astaire or Ginger Rogers. Forget about
your normal technique rules, posture and choreography. Move intuitively to the music and use all your body parts to express
yourself.

During the second track of music of the same dance you start to relate to your partner. First become aware where he or she

ercises: Warm-up

4s on the floor. Dance yourself towards your partner. React to each other's movements, still keeping your own rhythm. Make

eve contact. Investigate how close you can get to your partner and still feel comfortable. Investigate the right time fora touch.
Are you now willing to have physical contact? Is your partner willing? Take the time both of you need and then start creating

a free style dance together including touching and holding each other.

WARMING UP PART THREE: SOLO BALLROOM DANCING

You are now ready to emotionally share the dance. Let’s make sure this also works for strict ballroom dancing.

Dance the same dance you have selected for part two now for three minutes. Dance solo. Dance only basic figures. Create a
constant perfect dance hold. Imagine where your partner would be all the time. Each time you want to start this solo dance
you begin with a Cleaning Breath’, feel your body toning for that dance and then you move.

Dance another three minutes solo. Now dance your whole choreography to the fullest. Create a constant perfect dance hold.

Imagine where your partner would be all the time. Each time you want to start this solo dance you begin with a Cleaning

Breath, feel your body toning for that dance and then you move.

WARMING UP PART FOUR: BALLROOM DANCING TOGETHER

You are now ready to fully share the dance with your partner.

Dance the same dance you have selected for part two and three for three minutes. Dance together. Dance only basic figures
Greate a constant perfect dance hold. Be aware where your partner is all the time. Each time you want to start this dance you
begin with a Cleaning Breath, feel the body toning for that dance, make personal eye connection with your partner, feel con
sciously where your bodies connect and then move. (You can read more about these steps in chapter I-4).

Dance once more three minutes together. Now dance your whole choreography to the fullest. Create a constant perfect dance
hold. Be aware where your partner is all the time. Each time you want to start this dance you begin with a Cleaning Breath,

feel the body toning for that dance, make a personal eye connection with your partner, feel consciously where your bodies

connect and then move.

You are now ready to practise, to have a lesson, to compete or to perform a show.

Section 2-8-2

Breathing techniques

Control over breathing offers you many benefits. Controlled breathing techniques add more to your dance per-
formance than just giving you access to the available energy in your body. Deep breathing brings you, for
instance, a feeling of well being. Conscious breathing helps you to move with less muscle use. Furthermore,
breathing techniques help you to make personal contributions to the dances you perform: just a different accent
in your breath changes the outcome of the piece of choreography you are dancing!

Breathing techniques are so important that it pays off to spend some time becoming aware of the quality of your
breathing, You may find out that you need to take action to improve your breathing skills (first you could attend

to your general breathing skills and later incorporate such skills in your dancing).

SEVEN REASONS TO PAY ATTENTION TO YOUR BREATHING SKILLS

More available energy

Many dancers feel they often lack the stamina to perform five dances in a row well. Dance by dance it becomes
harder. And during each dance a decline in the quality of the performance can be noted. For some, lack of stam-
ina may really be the cause but for those who spend lots of time practising the problem probably lies elsewhere.
Insufficient breathing could be one of the major causes.

Short and fast breathing causes a short supply of oxygen. Such breathing puts an extra - unnecessary - load on the

heart. Deeper inhaling and exhaling prevents such problems and provides more access to your energy resources.

More freedom and flexibility in your body

Short and faster breathing may be caused by tension (mental and/or emotional stress) but the reverse also hap-
pens. Short and faster breathing may also cause tension in your body. The muscles start to resist because they
lack the necessary oxygen and extra energy is now needed to push them to continue.

Your chest and back start to freeze up. You feel you lose control over your body and this stresses you mentally and
emotionally. The Autonomous Nervous System warns you by producing chemicals that make you feel even less free.
Stronger control over your diaphragm - the largest muscle in your body that regulates your breath - improves the

freedom and the flexibility in your body to a great extent.

Better concentration
Breathing is not only of physical importance. Breathing is also the connection between your body and mind. The
better your breathing, the better the connection between the two. When this connection fails you might find

yourself in a position in which your body no longer listens to the stimuli of your mind. In your thoughts you may

Exercises: Breathing techniques

want to do something but somehow your body is no longer producing what your mind wants it to do.
And this problem may even intensify to a situation where you have no conscious mental activity at all. When.

H |

you lack concentration, this could be caused by inappropriate breathing.
ip .

Relaxation of a stressed mind |

Deep and slow conscious breathing helps to relax.a stressed mind. In chapter 1-4 Jou can Tead how this works

in step 3 of The Nine Step Connection Model®...

Improvement of posture and grounding

Deep breathing improves the posture of a Standard dancer. For deep breathing you need to use your diaphragm
strongly. This diaphragm contracts more during each deep exhalation, creating hore body contact with your
partner. Leading and following becomes easier when both pelvis areas are well connected. Less compensation in
the dance hold is then necessary.

Latin American dancers becomes more grounded when they learn to breathe more deeply.

Highlighting your choreography

A controlled use of breathing helps you to put accents into your choreography. Further on in this section an example
is given of how changes in your breath change the timing and outcome of the Throw Away Oversway in Waltz.
Where would you use breathing accents in that figure? You may have more options and choices than you
thought in the past. Just to mention a few: in the entry, in the middle or at the end of the figure. How would
you put the accent there? Constantly strong breathing? Impulse breathing? Impact breathing?

Breathing accents can support you to move forwards, backwards, downwards, upwards or sideways. Breathing
accents can make it easier to produce movements with impulse, impact, swing or a continuous movement and

even stillness.

Natural facial expression

Breathing techniques can help you to create a more natural facial expression. Many expressions seen on the
dance floor are falsely created and are not an expression of the moment and/or intention behind the movement.
Some people call such expressions emoting.

Conscious breathing can prevent the ugliness of pulling faces to make an impression. Conscious breathing can
help you to become more natural in your facial expression. Maybe because more chakras are activated when
your breathing is more optimal. Maybe because your breath helps you to balance as a human being: physically,
mentally, emotionally and spiritually. There is no scientific proof of this but you can easily check the effect of

conscious breathing by doing some of the exercises in this section.
BREATHING ALWAYS STARTS BY INHALING THROUGH THE NOSE
When we are in a natural, relaxed environment we normally inhale through the nose and exhale through the

mouth. There are several reasons why we do so:

Port Two: The Season

INHALATION THROUGH THE NOSE
When the air passes through the nostrils it is warmed, moistened and cleaned.

inhalation through the nose seems to contribute to the stimulation of the pituitary gland. This helps you to concentrate.

EXHALATION THROUGH THE MOUTH
Exhalation via your mouth makes the release of the used air easier (no nostrils in the way to block the exit...)
When the oxygen in the air has been used and transformed into nitrogen it needs to leave your lungs as fast as possible to

make space for fresh oxygen.

Under stress we seem to lose natural breathing: we inhale and exhale faster and shorter. Some only use their

nose while others only use their mouth.

SMOKING AND BREATHING
Proper breathing requires inhaling through the nose and exhaling through the mouth. The only correct excep-

tion to that rule is talking {we cannot talk and inhale through the nose at the same tim

Smoking is the other exception to the rule for proper breathing. During smoking you have to inhale through the
mouth (instead of the nose). Consequently smokers find it more difficult to achieve correct breathing. Smokers’
inhalations tend to be shallow, which is another danger. Therefore dancers who smoke tend to have shorter breath.
In short, smoking is not a healthy habit. For anybody. For dancers, smoking also tends to make them less flexi-

ble and free in their movements.

GENERAL BREATHING EXERCISES

The seven reasons (plus the smokers reason) might make you want to improve on your breathing skills to help
you to improve your dancing. The following programme would only take a few minutes per day. In a period of
jess than two months you can improve your general breathing considerably.

Read the following guidelines before starting any of the exercises:

The exercises might cause some dizziness in the beginning. Do not be alarmed: this is a signal that your body is not used to
the increase of oxygen. Be careful however when dizziness appears. Slow down the intensity of the exercises for a while to
allow your body to become accustomed to it gradually.

Do not exaggerate the exercises: this may cause unpleasant dizziness and it is not healthy.

It is better to do the exercises several times a day for a short while than to do them once a day intensely.

Abvays breathe in through the nose and breathe out via the mouth (exceptions to the rule are of course talking and - unfor
tunately - smoking...)

Ifyou have serious breathing problems you are advised to first see a doctor or a breathing therapist before doing these exer-

cises.

Weeks three and four: strengthen your diaphragm

During the first two weeks you may find that it is difficult in the beginning to feel the breathing in your sides
and your back. Now that you are aware of the potential of your lungs you need to strengthen your diaphragm.
Do the same exercises for Week Three and count your breath: inhale over four seconds and exhale over four

seconds. Make sure that you constantly inhale/exhale over all four seconds (many people probably finish with

the inhalation after two seconds: they need to learn to stretch it over another two seconds).

Do the same exercises for Week Four and count your breath: inhale over four seconds, hold for four seconds and

exhale over four seconds. Make sure that you constantly inhale/exhale over all four seconds.

Weeks five and six: rhyhmical changes in your breath

Dance movements are incredibly varied and are seldom constant. Sometimes you use impulse, impact, swing,
vibration, percussion, rebound and suspension'. You can make these movements happen by using the muscles
in your feet, legs, arms and torso. You can also jet your breath assist you and use your diaphragm. In the fol-

lowing weeks you will start to learn to control the diaphragm for three extra rhythms (next to the constant

rhythm you have already been practising).

Wear loose clothing and take off any restrictions like belts, wristwatches, bracelets and rings. Wear comfortable

sports shoes that allow you to feel the contact with the floor. Do the following three exercises at least once a

day. Twice would be ideal.

Exercises: Breathing techniques

Stand up straight with your knees slightly bent, your legs relaxed and almost parallel. Let your arms hang loose
and relaxed next to your body (or sit comfortably on a chair with your feet parallel on the ground and your arms
relaxed on the armrest}. Keep your focus to yourself to not be disturbed. '

seconds. |

out at the end. Repeat this exercise five times.

EXERCISE 2: IMPACT
Start with a constant inhalation (like you did before} and exhale first gently and finish with a strong accent at the end,

Repeat this exercise five times.

EXERCISE 3; SWING

Start with a constant inhalation [like you did before) and exhale first gently, make your breath stronger halfway through your

exhalation and finish by slowly fading out at the end. Repeat this exercise five times,

GENERAL BREATHING PRINCIPLES FOR DANCING
Now you have strengthened your breathing skills it is time to make use of them in your dancing. Now you can

start to apply the following general principles for dancing:

Regular breathing has seventeen breaths per minute. During sport activities the number of breaths tends to increase. Have a
look at the bars per minute per dance and see how many breaths you would really need on average (say, one breath per two
bars of Waltz or one breath per four bars of Jive}.

It is virtually impossible to put breathing accents constantly in a dance, Start working with general proper breathing. Then work on
breathing in a basic programme (for example, Basic Rumba or Basic Waltz). Finally select the highlights in each dance where a con-
sclous breath can be helpfuul. Do not select.too many of these highlights. You do not want to hyperventilate.

Inhale during steps prior to the action where you need energy the most (so do not inhale during the high-energy actions but
just before them).

A deep exhalation helps {Standard} dancers to make lowering actions fess physical and more easy.

Inhaling helps you to produce the dynamic quality of ‘Lightness’.

Inhaling can be helpful for rising actions. :

A short direct inhalation or exhalation with a sudden stop {impact} in it makes it easier for the body to stop any movement.

GENERAL BREATHING PRINCIPALS FOR WALTZ
The Waltz is normally played at 32-33 bars per minute. In Waltz you would use two bars for one inhalation and

one exhalation on average. Otherwise you would hyperventilate.
AWARENESS EXERCISE
Dance one minute Waltz by only using Natural turns, Reverse turns, right foot changes and left foot changes. Do this on

your own. Be aware of your breathing. Try to notice when and where you would like to inhale and when and where you tend
