GENERAL BREATHING PRINCIPLES FOR RUMBA
The Rumba is normally played at 27 bars per minute. Breathing for Rumba would have 16 inhales and 16 exhales
per minute. Consequently in Rumba you would use over two bars for one inhalation and one exhalation on

average.

AWARENESS EXERCISE
Dance one minute Rumba by only using forward and backward basics, fans and alemanas, Do this on your own,
Be aware of your breathing. Try to notice when and where you would like to inhale and when and where you tend to exhale.

Do you, for example, inhale on count 2 of the forward/backward step or do you want to exhale there? Fill in the following

form after you have danced the Rumba on your own.

Exercises: Breathing techniques

fas you may have noticed: there is a quite regular breathing pattern in Rumba. Also notice that you divide the
second bar into ’a-2’, On the ’a’ you do not have a breathing accent. On the ’2’ you exhale with impact. This
breathing helps you sustain the sidestep danced as ’4-a-1-a’ and to accelerate in the forward/backward step on
the ’2’.

You might have experienced that the more you breathe out on the ‘2’ the more grounded you feel and the more

control you have over your hip movement. You can use these experiences in your own choreography too.

Part Two: The Season

Section 2-8-3

Handicap training

Many of you like to practise in front of mirrors. These mirrors give you the opportunity to correct the visual
image of your efforts. It creates a habit of trusting your sight more than your other senses. It takes you away from
feeling your own body and relating to your partner (since you relate primarily to the mirror at such times), At
competitions you miss your feedback system offered by the mirrors. Now you have to trust your other senses but
you haven’t developed them.

In many other sports people are taught skills by introducing limitations. If you take out one sense - for instance
sight - then the other senses develop more strongly. The Handicap Model in this section limits you too. It offers

you a way out of your visual trap. And the model has more advantages...

ROWING

Years ago | used to row a lot. The trick for good rowing is to find the perfect stroke simultaneously with two or
four or even eight rowers at the same time. Synchronisation is essential to find balance in the boat. Only perfect
synchronized movements decrease the water resistance to a minimum and make the boat go fastest.

As a beginner you try to copy the movements of the rowers in front of you in the boat (they always put the best

rower in the front seat so others can follow his or her stroke...). This copying helps but is also only visually ori-
ented.

I was lucky to be trained by Julia Bronsgeest. She developed the Cybernetic teaching method - a system that develops
rowing skills very naturally, Her system was accepted in the sixties in the Netherlands and later on worldwide. Her
pupils became very successful and World and Olympic champions found Julia on their way to their success.

Play was always an important ingredient of Julia’s teaching methods. I stil! thank her for that. One of the exer-
cises she used was The Handicap Training. She made us row with closed eyes. She said that we should learn to
listen, We needed to learn to hear when a boat moves swiftly without hesitations or imbalances. She said that
we could feel whether the boat was in balance or not, that we could feel when to ride up with the moving seats
in the boat, when to start the stroke, when to finish it, etc. We could learn to feel whether we were seated in a
proper position or not.

We had a jot of fun learning, With our eyes closed we had to row without using the moving chair and without turn-
ing the blades. After some time we were allowed to turn the blades. When we could do that perfectly synchronized
we were allowed to drive the moving seat forward for just twenty-five per cent, then fifty per cent and finally hun-
dred per cent. Ail the time with our eyes closed. I feit the enormous concentration she gave us. At the end of half an

hour's rowing with our eyes closed the boat moved much faster while we did less muscle work. Those were great

rowing experiences. You can have experiences like these in your dancing too if you apply the same principles.

Exercises: Handicap trarming

THE HANDICAP MODEL. ;

The Handicap Model makes ‘use of'steps 3, 4, 5 (and 8) of the Nine Step Connection Model®. Before you start
to use the Handicap Model it is helpful to first:find the key word for the dance! you want to work on (read Part
One, chapters 1-4 and 1-5). | .

To prepare yourself further, you need to select a piece of choreography of about twenty seconds. Select a piece

that starts with some form of hold or physical connection. Make sure that the lwo of you can dance this piece
of choreography fully on your own before you start with the exercises.
Choose inspirational music for the dance. Take music without lyrics (lyrics tend to influence creative visualiza-

tions. Music without lyrics allows the mind to create visions more freely}.
€ach exercise uses the Cleaning Breath, which works as follows:

Close your eyes, stand with relaxed legs and arms hanging free. Make three slow inhalations and exhalations. Focus on how
the lungs fill and empty. Relax all muscles on the exhalation, each time a little more. Also relax your jaws! Inhale through the

nose and exhale through the mouth.

Stand opposite each other (at approximately 2 metres distance) to start the dance and do the following nine

exercises. Have lots of fun.

EXERCISE 1: CREATIVE VISURUZATION (SOLO)
Do your Cleaning Breath. Think of your key word. Tone your body slightly for dancing. Dance the twenty seconds of chore-
ography only in your mind: make no movernent with your body at all. Open your eyes when you are finished and shake your.

self loose.

EXEACISE 2: UPPER BODY MOVEMENTS (SOLO)
Do your Cleaning Breath. Close your eyes again. Think of your key word. Tone your body for dancing. Dance the twenty sec-
onds of choreography only with movement of the upper body and arms. Open your eyes when you are finished and shake

yourself loose.

EXERCISE 3: PELVIS MOVEMENTS (SOLO)
Do your Cleaning Breath. Close your eyes again. Think of your key word. Tone your body for dancing. Dance the ewenty sec-
onds of choreography only with movement of the upper body, arms and pelvis. Open your eyes when you are finished and

shake yourself loose.

EXERCISE 4: STATIONARY MOVEMENTS (SOLO)
Do your Cleaning Breath. Close your eyes again. Think of your key word. Tone your body for dancing. Dance the twenty sec-
onds of choreography with your whole body but stay stationary. Steps - on the spot - are allowed. Open your eyes when you

are finished and shake yourself loose.

Part Two: The Season

EXERCISE 5: FULL DANCING (SOLO)

Do the Cleaning Breath. Close your eyes again. Think of your key word. Tone your body for dancing. Dance the twenty sec-
onds of choreography completely with your eyes closed on your own three-five times, The first time. you dance with small steps.
Each of the following times you increase the size of the steps until they are at a normal size. Open your eyes when you are fin-

ished and shake yourself loose. After you have done this exercise a few times, your partner does the same.

PS
You need somebody to watch over you to prevent you from bumping into something/ somebody. Your partner could do that

job! The rule is as soon as you hear ‘stop’ or feel a touch you stop moving. This helps you to stay safe all the time.

EXERCISE 6: FULL DANCING (TOGETHER)
The two of you do the Cleaning Breath. Both of you close your eyes. Both of you think of your key word. Both of. ‘you tone your

body for dancing, Find each other now with your eyes closed. Dance together the 20 seconds of choreography completely with

your eyes closed three-five times. The first time you dance with small steps. Each of the following times you increase the size of

the steps until they are at a normal size. Open your eyes when you are finished and shake yourself loose.
bias PS
The two of you need somebody to watch over you to prevent you from bumping into something/somebody. The rule is as

Boos soon as you hear ‘stop’ or feel a touch you stop moving. This helps you to stay safe all the time.

EXEACISE 7: ONE IS BLIND (TOGETHER)

The two of you do the Cleaning Breath. Both of you close your eyes. Both of you think of your key word. Both of you tone your
body for dancing. Find each other now with your eyes closed. Dance together the twenty seconds of choreography. After the first
few seconds one of the two partners opens his/her eyes. Continue to dance and check afterwards if the feeling was as good as

with your eyes closed. Do the same routine with the other partner opening the eyes after a few seconds,

EXERCISE 8: BLIND PLUS SIGHT (TOGETHER)

The two of you do the Cleaning Breath. Both of you close your eyes. Both of you think of your key Word. Both of you tone your
body for dancing. Find each other now with your eyes closed. Dance together the twenty seconds of choreography. After the
first few seconds both partners open their eyes, Continue to dance and check afterwards if the feeling was as good as with

‘your eyes closed.

EXERCISE 9: REGULAR (TOGETHER)
The two of you do the Cleaning Breath. Do not close your eyes. Both of you think of your key word. Both of you tone your

body for dancing. Take the dance hold with your eyes open. Dance together the 20 seconds of choreography, Now check if

the feeling was as good as the exercises with the eyes closed or partly closed.

EFFECTS OF THE HANDICAP MODEL

Through a constant and conscious use of your breath you connect your thinking with what you are doing. Your
senses (except sight) are reinforced and this improves your body-mind connection as well. You probably increase
your freedom of movement thanks to a stronger body awareness and a less actively critical mind.

You probably increase awareness of your own tasks and awareness of what your partner needs. This strongly con-
tributes to embracing the thought ‘How can / improve myself’. Your inner critical mind is eased since sight has
been neutralized. This new-found peace of mind often opens the door to genuinely experiencing and expressing
: your dancer’s feelings from within. Finally the Handicap Model offers you a way to strongly experience the effect

of your selected key word. '

Part Two; The Season

ei

Section 2-8-4

Mental library

KNOWING MORE MAKES YOU MOVE BETTER

Al vast mental library helps you to develop a clearer picture in your mind of what you want to deliver as a dancer,
It not only helps you to have clear ideas about what others do but also gives you the opportunity to find out how
others do it. Comparing ideas from different - sometimes conflicting - sources gives you the opportunity to find
out what is right for you and what isn’t.

If you want to create dancing according to the standardized and generally accepted style you need to know what
that style looks like, where it came from and how it is produced. A clear and correct mental picture of such infor-
mation speeds up the process of your body dancing in such a way!

Some of you may want to stretch boundaries and innovate: the Reformers and Explorers’. These dancers are less
success oriented and use their dancing more for self-expression. If you are one of them then do realize that you
are still competing and are judged by people who mostly prefer the standardized and generally accepted style.
These people can accept innovations: there is enough proof of that in the recent past. The pre-condition is that
you first know the standardized and generally accepted style well before you innovate. This knowledge helps you
to find out why you want to make changes and if those changes comply with the standardized style according
to others. Without such knowledge innovations may serve just one goal: being different. This may feel good for
you and that is fine when you are not competing but doing shows. When, however, you compete you also want
others to accept you. Therefore innovations need to support the style. Some innovations may take a while to be

accepted. Some never are. The difference is whether the innovations contribute to the standardized and gener-

ally accepted style or not.

THINKING OUT OF THE BOX

Ballroom dancing has long been a closed and self-supporting discipline. All knowledge was developed by compet-
ing dancers through finding out ways to develop the style. Such dancers retired and started to teach younger
dancers. Books were written. Congresses were held to share the vast knowledge of these self-made dancers and
teachers. This is a very natural development and has served the ballroom world well for many decades.

Some dancers and teachers stepped outside the world of the standardised and generally accepted ballroom style.
Some went back to the roots and discovered a great wealth of knowledge in, for instance, Cuba (Rumba), Brazil
(Samba} and Argentina (Tango}. They analysed these sources and standardized the technique based on what they
experienced.

Others took bigger steps outside the ballroom world and its original dances. They studied supporting dances like

tap-dance for Quick Step, Flamenco for Paso Doble, Mambo and Salsa for Cha-cha and Samba.

Exercises: Mental library

‘A few really stepped out of the safe and familiar world of the dances we perform. They studied, for example,
Alexander Technique to learn about universal principles for posture and partnering. They studied Choreology to
learn about the principles of movement for dancing, a study which has already ‘served other dance styles for
many decades. Again others studied Theatre Arts or specialized in the effect of breathing techniques or studied
psychology and brought new information to our institution. :

Their new information is needed to keep our style alive. It is crucial, however, that such pioneers still keep their
knowledge of the standardized style up to high standards. So they know whether or not innovations contribute.
If you study great painters - like Pablo Picasso or Vincent van Gogh - you will notice that they all started paint-
ing according to the standardized style in their early careers. With that deep knowledge - and by trial and error
- they made innovations in their time. Innovations that are now accepted because they knew the roots so well
and knew why they needed to move away from what already existed.

For you it might be worthwhile to start learning from other sources outside what is available inside the ballroom

world. It can be a great and inspiring quest!

WAYS OF BUILDING UP YOUR MENTAL LIBRARY

Building up a stronger mental library can be realized in many different ways:

READ BOOKS WRITTEN ABOUT OUR BALLROOM DANCES

There are more books than the technique books teachers have to learn from to pass their exams.

READ MAGAZINES AND NEWSPAPERS

Read the existing sources in our ballroom world to, find out what is the general opinion about style issues.

WATCH VIDEOS OF BALLROOM DANCING

Just looking at a video at full speed makes it hard to internalize what you see. It is better to watch a video with one subject matter
in your mind (take the subject matter you are currently working on yourself. See how other dancers handle that one subject matter
luke shythm, posture, dynamics, partnering, choreography, etc). Watch interesting footage a few times, maybe even in slow motion
50 you can really download that image into your own mind.

WATCH OTHERS COMPETING

We not only attend competitions to dance but are also often present when others are dancing (especially if an event lasts
longer than one day). You could use the days you are not competing to be inspired by good dancing from others. You could
have that one subject matter as your focus and see how others manage it (you learn from good and bad examples).

You need to make it easier for your mind to register what you want to see {video rewind of footage and slow motions are now
not possible). Select a few couples you like and dislike and follow them through the competition with a focus on that one sub-
ject matter. You now not only get inspired but also feed your mental library.

FOUWOW WORKSHOPS IN OTHER DANCE DISCIPLINES

Dance workshops are popular even outside our dance world, Some of them are related to our ten ballroom dances like
Flamenco, Tapdance, Jazz, Argentine Tango, Spanish Folk dance, Mambo, Salsa and Meringue.

Following these workshops for taking seasonal classes or private lessons} not only develops your physical skills but also builds

up a stronger mental library. You know more since you have seen and experienced more. Great artists mostly make sure they

Part Two: The Season

take in many different impressions before they start creating, The more impressions in their minds, the more creative com-
binations they can make...

GO TO THE THEATRE

Watch ballet and other forms of theatrical art. Again you can focus on your selected subject matter and see how others handle
that... :

READ BOOKS WRITTEN ABOUT OTHER DANCE FORMS:

in the bibliography at the end of this book you may find some interesting tities. Around the world there are many fine bookstores with
specialized departments for dance. Follow your intuition and pick up a book which appeals to you.

ENJOY PICTURE BOOKS

Reading might not be what you like to do. There are many dance books and magazines which cater to your taste if you like
£0 look at pictures. Analyse these pictures. See what appeals to you and find out why. Internalize such pictures by taking the
time to really look at them.

GO TO THE CINEMA OR AENT VIDEOS

Watch ballet and other forms of theatrical art videos, Again you can focus on your selected Subject matter and see how others
handle that...

TAUT TO PROFESSIONALS

Have conversations inside and outside the ballroom institution. Not only ask professionals for their opinions, but also ask why
they have such opinions (the ‘why’ is often more important than the ‘what’. Challenge them if you do not understand them
or you do not agree. The goal of such discussions is not about right or wrong. You bath might be right. The goal is to develop
informed opinions.

LEARNING OUTSIDE THE BALLROOM INSTITUTION

Studies like Alexander technique, Choreology, choreography, theatre arts arid psychology can improve your insights for ball-

room dancing too.

INTERNAUZING NEW INFORMATION
There are many techniques that help you to internalize new information quickly, Looking at new information

with one subject matter as your focus is helpful. Looking at new information repeatedly also helps. Discussing

new information with others helps. Trying out new information physically speeds up acceptance (or rejection)

of new information. Teaching new information to others helps you to become aware of what you know and

when you need more information. Making collages of interesting pictures around one subject matter not only

helps you to internalize the pictures but also inspires you emotionally and spiritually.

There is a very helpful book on the market for those who like to read - and even those who don’t but sometimes

have to. This book offers techniques that help you to concentrate better, read faster and internalize information

quickly and with pleasure. This book is called ’Use your head’ and is written by Tony Buzan. It is available in many

languages. The technique calied "Mind mapping’ is easy to learn and you profit from it for the rest of your life.

Exercises: Mental lidrary

Section 2-8-5

Relaxation techniques

Relaxation helps to neutralize a stressed body and a stressed mind. Relaxation stimulates a good body-mind con-
nection. It also helps you to let go of what you have been doing before you start to dance, You might have had
a tough day at work, problems in the family, problems with your partner or dance partner, etc.

It might seem contradictory to first relax before you start practising, performing or taking lessons. However it
does help to make a good body-mind connection before you get active. The Cleaning Breath'(Step 3 in the Nine
Step Connection Model®) stimulates this body-mind connection. Of course there are other ways to relax too.

Some of them are presented in this section.

RELAXING? THAT'S A NONSENSE!

You might find that you do not want to relax before you get active. The fear behind that resistance is often the
concern that you might not want to be active after the relaxation... You might be afraid that - once you relax -
your body might send signals of tiredness and you will want to relax even more. Of course this is not a pleasant
message. You can ignore it and try to get the best out of your body. On the other hand it could be more effec-
tive to become aware of your body signals and take appropriate measures. You could decide to not practise (if
the signals are very strong) or you could decide to dance but to change the schedule for the following days to
get more rest (physically with more sleep or mentally by addressing less subjects at the same time).

Realize that your Autonomous Nervous System will interfere if you ignore the signals of your body. Your mind
wants you to dance while your body signals that there is no energy left for that. This conflict of body and mind
results in stress. Your breathing becomes faster and shorter and chemicals in your blood make you less free to
move. These are messages from the Autonomous Nervous System that something is not working and that you

need to change your course of action. Ignoring these signals will certainly not let you practise well.

RELAXING EXERCISES
Next to the Cleaning Breath and the Holistic warm-up’ other physical relaxation exercises can help you too. Here

are some options:
The short cut exercises

Here are some very short exercises that help you relax quickly. Pick out the ones that suit you. End each of the

following exercises with two strong and slow breaths.

Part Two: The Season

STAMP

Sit on a chair with your back straight. Stamp your feet for thirty seconds on the floor and double the speed. Do not forget to
breathe in through the nose and to exhale via the mouth.

JumP

Stand up straight. Close your eyes. Do Little jumps for thirty seconds. Do not forget to breathe in through the nose and to
exhale via the mouth. Notice that after this exercise you will probably have more energy and that your senses are stronger.
You wilt also be able to concentrate better.

BODY MASSAGE

Give yourself a body massage. Start with massaging your feet and work yourself up to finish with your head. You may give
yourself a soft massage but you can also pat yourself with flat hands or even with your fists. Breathe consciously during the
massage and be gentle with your face and neck.

SMILE

Smile or grin for ninety seconds. When you really need to relax you probably feel that there is nothing to smile or grin about.
That is understandable but you do not need to feel you want to smile or grin. You just do it. After forty-five seconds your brain
picks up this facial expression as a signal that you feel good. This interpretation starts the production of neurotransmitters
(endorphins) that give you a real feeling of well-being. So use the next forty five seconds of smiling or grinning to get enough
of these endorphins.

SNORT

Snorting like a horse has the same effect on your brain as the smiling or grinning exercise! Snort like a horse for twenty sec.
onds without using your voice. Repeat this with the use of your voice from a high pitch to a low pitch. Repeat it again and

start with a low pitch and end with a high pitch, Repeat it once more and play with low and high pitches in your voice. Have

a good stretch to finish this exercise.

OHMS:

Singing Ohms prepares people for meditation because it relaxes the body and mind and opens the chakras. You can use this
ancient technique to relax too. It works as follows.

In one deep exhalation you sing the following letters: AAA-OOO-MMM-NNN. Try to start to sing as low as possible. Fach
time you sing the Ohm you try to find a lower AAA. Make the sound of the AAA last for about two seconds and gradually
change it into OOO then MMM and finally NNN. Make the sound last as long as you can make your exhalation last. When
you do it well, you feel a vibration in your body from your toes to your head. Especially on the NNN!

The first time you make the Ohm just let it happen as it comes out of your mouth. Repeat the Ohm a few times and vary with
your voice: make it start lower or higher, make tt sound louder or softer. Feel the impact of the different versions and repeat
once or twice the version that gives you a maximum vibration. Shake yourself loose to finish this exercise.

The singing of the Ohm calms your body, relaxes your mind and helps to find an emotional balance. Find 4 place where you

can sing the Ohm privately. Use a dressing room. Even the restroom will do.

TIP

When you sing the Ohms together with other people you will experience a deeper vibration in your body.

€xercises: Relaxation techniques

The big draw exercise

The following exercise is helpful if you notice that the short exercises are not enough. You then need to relax
completely before you begin again. The exercise takes twenty to twenty-five minutes of focusing and working on
several body parts and relaxing. After this exercise you will feel reborn.

Start the exercise by putting on some soft music (preferably without lyrics} in quiet surroundings where you can-
not be disturbed. Take off your shoes and other restricting accessories like belts, wristwatches, tings, ties, etc.).
Put a mattress or big towel on the floor. Lie down on your back with a pillow under your head. Your legs are
relaxed and almost parallel. Let your hands rest slightly parallel to the body on the ground.

During the exercise you focus on one muscle group at a time. Try to keep the other muscle groups relaxed. After
twelve to fifteen minutes of work with all individual muscle groups, contract all muscles at once and strongly.
This is called "The Big Draw’. A deep relaxation follows in which you let your mind flow freely. You no longer
control your thoughts. Just let them pass without trying to hold on to them. Enjoy the deep relaxation and stay
in this state for about ten minutes. Slowly open your eyes. Get up when you feel ready. Shake your body loose

and do a body massage if you now decide to become active and want to dance.

BIG DRAW: PREPARATIONS

Lie down on the floor. Keep your eyes closed during the whole exercise. Stretch your legs and relax them in that position.
Put your arms on the floor parallel to your body. Stretch them in that position and then relax them. Notice where the body
touches the floor: your head, your shoulder blades, your back, your arms, your hands, your buttock’s, your thighs, your calves
and your feet.

BIG DRAW: MUSCLE GROUP MANIPULATION

Do each of the following exercises for thirty seconds (unless another time is stated). Start slowly, then increase the speed and
slow it down again at the end. Move your toes up and down. Move your feet up and down (your legs stay relaxed on the floor).
Also move your feet from left to right (and the reverse). Contract and release your calves. Contract and release the muscles in
‘your thighs. Lift your left leg up and lower slowly (keep your right leg on the floor). Repeat the previous exercise for your right
leg. Contract and release your abdomen. Contract and release the muscles in your upper chest. Rotate your shoulder blades
slowly backwards. Rotate your shoulder blades slowly forwards. Spread your fingers as wide as possible and keep them in that
position for five seconds. Then release the muscle tension while you exhale, Repeat this three times. Drum your fingers like
playing a plano or typing a letter). Lift your lower arms up and slowly let them move back to the ground (keep muscle tension
‘even when the arms move downwards). Lift your arms up completely and slowly let them move back to the ground (keep
muscle tension even when the arms move downwards). Rotate your shoulder blades slowly backwards. Rotate your shoulder
blades again slowly forwards.

BIG DRAW: FACE MANIPULATION

Turn your head from left to right and the reverse, Do this gently. Repeat for thirty seconds. Raise your eyebrows as far up as
possible five times. Try to move your ears as much as possible for ten seconds. The easiest way is to clench your teeth strong-
ly or to frown. Roll your closed eyes from left to right and the reverse in the eye sockets for ten seconds. Make circles with your
closed eyes in the eye sockets for ten seconds. Contract and release the muscles of the nose for ten seconds. Open your mouth

as wide as possible and close it siowly. Try to yawn. Repeat this ten times. Keep your mouth open and move the lower jaw. from

Part Two: The Season

left to right and the reverse for ten seconds, Stick out your tongue as far as possible five times.

BIG DRAW: THE BIG DRAW

Take a deep inhalation. Do not breathe out for five seconds. Contract all muscles (from top to_toe) during these five seconds
as strongly as you can (until it starts hurting]. Count the seconds. Pfffff. Relax all muscles and breathe out at the:same time.
BIG DRAW: DEEP RELAXATION

Feel the relaxation flow into your body when you have released your muscles. Do not move and keep your eyes closed. Let
your mind flow freely. Do not control your thoughts. Just let them pass without trying to hold on to them. Enjoy the deep
relaxation and stay in this state for about ten minutes. Slowly open your eyes. Get onto your feet when you feel ready to
become active again. Shake your body loose. Slowly rotate your head once more from left to right and the reverse. Take a deep

breath, Do a body massage if you now decide to become active and want to dance.

TIP

Section 2-8-6

Marking choreography

RUNNING THROUGH YOUR CHOREOGRAPHY DOES MAKE SENSE

You can increase the quality of your dancing by running through the choreography just before a lesson, a prac-
tice, a competition or a show. First of all because you give both mind and body incentives to activate the mem-
ory of the desired outcome in the very near future. Since we are creatures of habit, running through the chore-
ography gives easier access to already existing habits and skills. And even new choreography - that hasn’t set
completely in your body and mind yet - is helped by this running through: it also refreshes new skills.

Running through your choreography can work positively. Which of the following statements suits you best?

RUNNING THROUGH CHOREOGRAPHY WITH FULL ENERGY

One way of running through a choreography would be dancing the material to the full without taking note of

the quality of the performance. This is almost literally running through things. You use the available energy in
your body and do not mind the quality of the outcome. You just want to refresh your memory of the steps.

Unfortunately this is highly ineffective behaviour. Doing so regularly, you create a habit in your body and mind
in which it is acceptable to dance with full energy and low quality. Your Autonomous Nervous System (A.N.S.}
might pick that up as the desired habit. The A.N.S. might reproduce it for you when you are under stress. (The

A.N.S. always tries to help you under stress by bringing you back into a habit which it is sure you can do well.)'

RUNNING THROUGH CHOREOGRAPHY WITH RESTRICTED ENERGY

Another way of running through your programme is to do it with small steps and restricted use of energy. This
approach activates the memory in your mind and body. Fortunately you now prevent the creation of an unde-
sired habit: the outcome doesn’t compare to the real thing with full energy and full size steps and movements.

Now your A.N.S. cannot get mixed up about what the desired habit is.

Port Two: The Season

RUNNING THROUGH CHOREOGRAPHY WITH VISUALIZATION
Al third way of running through your choreography is similar to the previous approach with smail steps and a
low level of energy use. It is called ’The visualization running through’, which Ruud Vermeij* introduced to

dance sport dancers. It is a highly effective way of running through your programme because it activates all lev-

els of the human being (physically, mentally, emotionally and spiritually).

Running through with visualization

This is how the running through with visualization works:

EXERCISE 1 (SOLO)

Dance your choreography first by yourself (without your dance partner). Just do the steps without special thoughts. Keep the
steps small and the energy in the body low, This exercise helps you to get in the "here and now’. It takes your mind away
from distracting thoughts.

EXERCISE 2 (SOLO)

Remember the subject matter on which you have been working over the last few days, weeks, months?. This subject matter
could be one of the following: rhythm, partnering, use of dynamics, dance hold, creating shapes, time, space, balance, etc.
Become very conscious about what you have been working on recently.

EXERCISE 3 (SOLO)

Dance your choreography again with small steps and a low use of energy. Now visualize - while you are dancing - how it would
feel or look like if you would dance it like in the performance itself. Imagine yourself excel in your subject matter.

EXERCISE 4 (SOLO)

It could be helpful to repeat it ewo to three times to settle this visualization. This may help your body to function much better
{unless you get bored by this repetition...)

EXERCISE 5 (TOGETHER)

Dance the choreography together with your partner once you both have individually established an inspiring visualization. Do

this again with sinall steps and low energy. Even have some mote space in the frame when you so desire.
EXEACISE 6 (TOGETHER)
Finally you may want to dance the choreography to the fullest (if the occasion permits; for instance on a practice night). Feel

how much more freely you dance and how much higher the quality of the performance becomes.

Exercises: Marking choreography

When running through with visualization doesn’t work for you the first few times you try it, consider the
following:
Use the Cleaning Breath before you start each exercise. This Cleaning Breath’ purifies your body and cleans the mind of any
critical thoughts. : :
Remember that exercises 1-5 are not simulating the real performance. It is a private experience to activate the best skills locked
inside you on all human levels: physical, mental, emotional and spiritual.
You might want to do the following to help this sense of not performing: imagine you are on a stage and you are alone. The

curtains are still closed. You just have a few minutes to get ready for the performance. Nobody can watch you. You can run

through the choreography one more time on your own.

MARKING YOUR CHOREOGRAPHY

A final way of running through your choreography is similar to the previous two approaches. Again you dance
with small steps and a low use of energy. This is called "marking your choreography’. In this system you do not
dance the actual steps but improvise the movements. You walk along the dance floor and mark the spots of your
highlights by improvising the movements with a visualization of the subject matter. This systems works like

Running Through with visualization. The difference is that you do not need to make each step and movement.

You just go for the general feel of the choreography at that point. For some dahcers this way gives them better

access to their spiritual and emotional plane.

Section 2-8-7

Creative visualization

Our mind is a very powerful instrument. It is not only intelligent but it can also help to create a desired future
by creatively visualizing it,

Your mind can create a self-fulfilling prophecy. In daily life we often realize that: when you think negatively about
something, it probably turns out that way. And of course the opposite is also true.

Over the years, many of you have created feelings about the competitions you attend. Some you like and others
you don’t. Such opinions and feelings are almost a guarantee that you will perform better at the ones you like
and worse on the ones you dislike. This is not a prosperous situation since you cannot only select competitions
you like.

Years ago we started to use the self-fulfilling prophecy technique Creative Visualization to Prepare dancers for
their major competitions. This technique - developed by Shakti Gawain! in the USA - has helped dancers to pre-
pare themselves more positively. In her book, Shakti offers the readers many ideas. In this chapter you find an

adaptation of a guided meditation to prepare yourself mentally and emotionally for your next major competition.

PREPARATIONS

Make time for yourself. Free yourself for ten to fifteen minutes to be with yourself. Find somebody to instruct
you during this exercise or make a tape/CD with a voice giving you the instructions. Create breaks of a few sec-
onds between each statement/question. Select a place where you are sure you cannot be disturbed by people or
any other external stimulus (do not forget to switch off all telephones). Take off any restrictive accessories like
tings and watches. Take off your shoes and untie your belt. Put yourself in a comfortable position. This may be
lying down on the ground or on a mattress, Stretch your legs and relax them in that position. Put your arms on
the floor parallel to your body. Stretch them in that position and then relax them. Notice where your body touch-
es the floor: your head, your shoulder blades, your back, your arms, your hands, your buttocks, your thighs, your

calves and your feet.

You can also decide to sit in a chair. Sit relaxed with your spine vertically positioned and your feet on the floor.
Feel the gravity in your body and feel the connection with the earth. Stretch your legs and relax them. Put your
feet solidly on the floor again and keep your knees paraliel. Stretch your arms forwards and relax them on the
armrest. Straighten up, relax and settle the back solidly in the back of the chair. Notice where the body touches

the chair: your shoulder biades, your back, your arms, your hands, your buttocks and your thighs.

Exercises: Creative visualization

Now close your eyes for the next ten to fifteen minutes. Allow yourself to be guided through meditation about
your important competition day. Gently follow the given thoughts and questions. If you do not connect with
the thought or question then just let it go. If you notice you are focusing on something else, then concentrate
on your breathing for a while and pick up the meditation suggestions later on.,

there,

Section 2-8-8

Energetic linkage

i

While being with others we all have a preference for one of the following qualities of energetic linkage'. Some
people like a primary connection with others on a personal basis (warm, challenging, intruding or even attack-
ing). Others prefer to connect as a neutral observer (not indicating whether they like or dislike the connection).
Some primarily dislike to have a personal eye contact and withdraw their focus (like in a glaze) or even prefer to
have no visual connection with others.

Energetic linkage itself cannot be seen. This does not imply that it does not exist. When you look into your
microwave you do not see the energy but your dish becomes heated. You can’t see energy but you certainly can
feel the effect of it. Human energetic linkage works in the same way. You do not see the energies but you clear-
ly feel them. You immediately experience physical and emotional changes when the linkage between you and
your partner changes.

Invisible elastic bands are a great metaphor for energetic linkage. Is the elastic band giving you freedom or is

there some pulling that you try to resist? Are you pulling and is your partner resisting?

Control over the level of linkage with your partner increases the pleasure of dancing together, making it less phys-
ical work and highly contributing to how others see your dancing. .
In this section you find exercises designed to increase your awareness of your linkage system so that you can learn

how to control it effectively.

BASIC EXERCISE: BOTH PARTNERS USE THE SAME LEVEL OF LINKAGE
Take two chairs. Sit opposite each other with a distance of approximately one metre.
Put your feet on the ground in a parallel-relaxed position (distance approximately 30-40 centimetres).
Sit up straight. Relax your muscles without losing this position.
Take a Cleaning Breath (see chapter I-4, step 3} with a private focus.
Look at each other with a neutral focus. Just observe one another without indicating any feelings. Keep on breathing con-
sciously to stay neutral. (Do the Cleaning Breath again if you lose your neutrality. Lots of people feel a need to laugh when
they first try this exercise. The Cleaning Breath provides the control you need).
‘The two of you move into a warm and personal focus. Only use your eyes and do not pull faces. Enjoy the warm connection.
Stay that way for at least twenty seconds.
Move back into neutral. Stay there for at least ten seconds.
Now move into a cold, impersonal withdrawn focus. This is a focus where you still see one another but you actually would

prefer not to. Stay that way for at least 20 seconds.

Port Two: The Season

Finally move into a focus where both of you ignore one another. You avoid eye contact at all costs. Do that for at least 20
seconds. ,

Return to neutral and discuss your experience. Which experience do you personally prefer?

To make the exercise work it is important to take it seriously. A Cleaning Breath before each exercise helps you
to control your focus. You must have experienced that dancing with ‘Ignoring’ or with 'Withdrawn’ becomes

hard physical muscle work and less pleasant than the other options.

ENERGETICS: FOUR LEVELS OF LINKAGE

As humans we vary our level of linkage the whole day. Energetic shifts may happen in seconds depending on our

preference (primary linkage) and the level of safety and stress.

Basically all humans prefer personal linkage. It is, however, not always available to us. We have learned to survive
by adopting a safe primary linkage system. For some, it is Personal’, for others "Withdrawn’ or even ‘Ignore’. None
of these forms of linkage is better than the others. When the same linkage is not shared, both may suffer under it.
We need to access ‘neutral linkage’ to handle situations where linkage of the two is not synchronized or where the

two are both in Withdrawn’ or "Ignore’. There are ten basic forms of linkage between two people:

Part Two: The Season

Answer the following questions before you continue to read:

1. Which combination(s) of linkage is/are beneficial for dancing?

2. Which combination(s) of linkage is/are definitely not beneficial for dancing and need to be changed?

3. Which combination (s) of linkage is/are not beneficial for dancing but you have to accept it them?

BENEFICIAL COMBINATIONS OF ENERGETIC LINKAGE
Only the first three combinations of linkage produce quality dancing and create a fulfilling experience. If you do

not agree repeat the Advanced exercise: improvising dance with different levels of linkage’.

DYSFUNCTIONAL COMBINATIONS OF ENERGETIC LINKAGE

The combinations six to ten decrease the quality of your dancing and create a physical approach, which is not
very inspiring for you or for those watching. If you do not agree you repeat the ‘Advanced exercise: improvising
dance with different levels of linkage’. One solution is that one of the two of you must change your linkage to
’Neutral’. The effect is that the other no longer feels energetically pulled or pushed away. He or she now really

feels free and probably also adopts 'Neutral’ linkage.

Exercises: Energetic linkage

UNFAVOURABLE COMBINATIONS YOU HAVE TO ACCEPT

The fourth and fifth combinations are not really beneficial. One of you is Neutral’ while the other is ’Withdrawn’
or even ‘Ignoring’.

There is nothing else you can do but keep checking that you remain ’Neutral’. This means you observe your part-
ner and see his or her.lack of linkage. You hold no judgement in that situation. When you really are ’Neutral’
then your partner will shift - at some point - into ’Neutral’ as well. A Cleaning Breath might help you to find real

neutrality.

MASTERING ENERGETIC LINKAGE

The easiest way to have control over your energetic linkage is to start practising the first two exercises every day
for five to ten minutes. In about a month you will notice that energy really follows thought and that you have
obtained more control over the levels of linkage you need and use, After such a practice you will find it much
easier to control your linkage during dancing. A good execution of step 8 of the Nine Step Connection Model®

is largely based on a neutral or personal level of linkage!

You can do the exercises with your partner but it’s not necessary. You can do them with a friend, a family mem-
ber, etc. Anybody who is willing to do it seriously may help you to gain control,

You may also experiment with linkage in your private life. Imagine you are waiting in a bakery filled with people.
The saleswoman is under pressure (because it is busy) and has adopted a withdrawn linkage. How will you be
served best? Your "Warm and Personal’ linkage will not be rewarded. Nor will Personal and Attack’ help. Just
ty it out yourself! Withdrawn’ or ’Ignore’ are not rewarded either. The best way to get your loaf of bread is to
become ’Neutral’ in your linkage.

When you dominantly prefer Warm and Personal’ another exercise is available whenever you go to a super-
market and pay for your groceries. Some cashiers are really good in ignoring you and mechanically scan your
goods. This really feels unsatisfying for addicted "Warm and Personal’ linkage people. Try other levels of linkage
and you will notice that the best linkage is that of Neutral’!

Part Two: The Season

Section 2-8-9

Voice Dialogue

Most of the material in this book offers you tools to access your personal road to success. And most of this work
allows your personality to stay as it is. The models and structures presented are not intended to change you. They
only change the way you operate, In many cases, howeve, insight into your personality can result in personal
growth. In a balanced coaching practice some therapeutic assistance may help you. TeamAmsterdam uses Voice

Dialogue for this reason.

THE PSYCHOLOGY OF THE AWARE EGO

Voice Dialogue is one of the psychological methods used in transformational psychology - called Psychology of
Selves and Psychology of the Aware Ego. In the seventies this specialization was developed by psychologists Hal
and Sidra Stone Ph.D.' In opposition to fashionable transformation models of, for instance, Landmark, Essence
and NLP’, the Stones model does not accentuate the development of what is generally accepted as positive (most-
ly our power sides). Their model integrates what is perceived as positive and negative. This proves to be an enor-

mous step forward in the transformation of people.

THE MANY SELVES IN YOUR PERSONALITY

Voice Dialogue is basically a real dialogue of voices. A dialogue with your inner voices so.to speak. Every human
being has a number of different inner voices. And every voice has different opinions, rules and even behaviour.
For instance: I can be overactive but also can be very lazy at times. I can be very serious about my work but once
in a while I joke about everything. It feels like having ‘moods’. And everybody has lots of them. According to the
Transformation Psychology they are not just 'moods’. They are independent parts of your personality. They

represent the inner voices, also called sub-personalities or selves.

We all have an infinitive number of sub personalities. Not ail of them are active daily but they do exist. The sub per-
sonalities that rule our daily life are called Primary Selves. Consciously or unconsciously these Primary Selves con-
duct your life. They decide what needs to be done by you in any situation you are facing. For the other sub person-
alities there is little or no space in your operating system. These sub personalities are called Disowned Selves.

Early in life every individual decides whether a sub personality will be primary or disowned. It is a strategy for
survival, which already starts in the cradle. Babies learn instinctively what works better to get the attention they
need: for some smiling works, others find out that crying is more effective, or yelling, or being silent. These
Primary Selves continue to develop and increase the chance of survival. They get stronger each time you have

an impressive experience where the Primary Selves prove to be effective. Once in a while one or more Primary

Exercises: Voice Dialogue -

Selves can become disowned when they prove to be dysfunctional in a radical event. In such situations new

Primary Selves are created. They emerge from the Disowned Selves because they now prove to work.

VOICE DIALOGUE: CREATING FREEDOM OF CHOICE

Voice Dialogue is a method that uses the Jungian idea that every human being has different sub personalities.
You are not only a dancer, You are more than that. You may be a manager in daily life who enjoys hard work,
but you may also enjoy being lazy. Sometimes you like to take care of others while other times you want to be
taken care of. Sometimes you may seek safety and other times you like to be a daredevil. Sometimes you like to
be with friends, while there are also times you really need to be on your own.

This experiencing of opposites is a natural human inner conflict. Voice Dialogue is a method that helps you to
become more aware of the needs of all your sub-personalities: primary and disowned! The awareness and experi-
ence of these inner polarities create a stronger aware ego. Those who have more awareness and access to these
inner polarities develop a greater freedom of choice in life.

The goal of Voice Dialogue sessions is not to solve current problems in your life. That may happen but it is not the
ultimate goal. Each session basically contributes to the development of your Aware Ego. This helps you to identify
not only with your Primary Selves. You learn to listen to your inner polarities, feel them, detach from them and

make choices from a neutral place. This results in balanced choices that bring you more fulfilment in life.

EXAMPLE

Latin American dancer Xanne had intense stress during competitions. Especially in the rounds it mattered. Stress reduction
work (like the Nine Step Connection Mode and seasonal planning} relieved her somewhat, but not enough. Xanne decided
to have Voice Dialogue sessions, which were monitored by her partner lan. Xanne started talking about what happened dur-
ing these competitions. The facilitator helped her to identify with her Thinker. This primary sub personality always helped her
out whenever Xanne felt uncomfortable. The Thinker in Xanne told her not to dance because of all the unnecessary stress.
This Thinker had no access to her body. She didn't even want Xanne to feel her body. The Thinker cooperated with Xanne’s
Perfectionist and gave her a mental approach to her dancing. And what made it even worse: the Thinker needed no connec-
ton with Ian. Under stress Xanne was better off on her own!

‘The facilitator brought Xanne back to a place where she could reflect on what her Thinker had told her. Xanne was asked to
check if she felt that other parts of her personality were agreeing or disagreeing with the Thinker. She moved into her Inner
Dancer self. This Dancer told her that she could only be present when lan allowed her to play while dancing. Too much serious-
ness and work for results made her tired and stiff. In such situations she preferred to hide herself and gave space to the Thinker
to take control. Unfortunately this self had no control over her body. The Dancer self gave Xanne valuable clues about play.
Xanne became aware of how the Inner Dancer fett and acted. She started to access this self. Since lan had monitored the ses-

sions he was able to provide better conditions during competitions so Xanne's Dancer could emerge

OTHER SELVES TOO
During Xanne’s sessions her Primary and Disowned selves came more to life. Xanne could experience them and

become aware of them without identifying with them totally. Each dancer has different Primary and Disowned

selves. Subject matters during sessions with clients therefore vary: for example, handling anorexic tendencies,
working with partners/teachers, handling the Inner Critic, the Perfectionist, the Warrior and even the Inner

Patriarch (quite a dominant archetypical self in the ballroom world...)

S325!

In the 1990s, the American television host Oprah Winfrey started to advise her audience to keep a gratitude

diary. This diary work would help you to transform yourself by giving you a more satisfied feeling. The diary
would help you to become more aware of the positive experiences you have each day, however small those
experiences might be: unexpected friendly greetings from a neighbour in the street, having taken the break you
really needed, a compliment from your partner, teacher or anybody, the weather, a nice talk with a friend, a new
haircut, one moment in your practice that reaily felt good, the design for a new dress, the taste of your cereal at

breakfast and so on.

NOT ONLY FOR STRUGGLNG PEOPLE

Becoming more aware of the (any kind of) positive experience helps you to break with negative thinking pat-
terns. Not only the Resigned and Strugglers have such feelings. Most Westerners have a strong Inner Critic. This
Inner Critic can be so powerful that it does not aliow you to enjoy the positive experience. This Inner Critic may
indicate things that need to be improved. This Critic actually doesn’t allow you to experience moments con-
sciously. It prefers to activate your Inner Pusher and Inner Perfectionist.

On the other hand, it is true that awareness of positive experiences makes your life easier. It gives you ammuni-
tion to enjoy the present more often. It is like dancing. Dancing with awareness of all your mistakes doesn’t pro-
duce freedom but stress. Dancing with a greater awareness of what you do right improves your sense of accom-

plishment and decreases stress.

THE EFFECT OF KEEPING A DIARY: FOUR LEVELS OF HEALING
Writing the positive experiences down in a Gratitude Diary makes a great difference compared to only noticing
them sometimes during the day. The path of personal transformation requires work on all four levels of human

existence’: physical, mental, emotional and spiritual. By writing down the positive experiences you make growth

easier:

Keeping a Gratitude Diary can be beneficial for everybody. Dancers and non-dancers alike. Keeping the diary

doesn’t require a lot of time. A few minutes per day would be sufficient. The costs are extremely low {just a pen

and a diary) while the effects can be enormous. Lessons in writing the diary are also not necessary.

SUGGESTIONS
Write down a minimum of three positive experiences per day. If you feel you have had more positive experiences do write
these down too.
Think again if you feel you can't find three positive experiences. The positive experiences do not need to be impressive. Just
seeing a flower grow in your garden might be such an experience.
It is not necessary to write down new experiences every day. Sometimes you may feel good about the same experience for
many days. That is fine too. Write it down.
You do not have to name gratitude experiences only around dancing: Just promise to keep the diary. In fact it could be very
helpful to include all aspects of your life. Good feelings outside of your dancing could compensate the bad dancing days. As
a human being you could still feel OK or good, even on the days that the dancing doesn't contribute to it,
If you find it hard to find any positive experiences, some meditation? practice might be helpful.

‘ Just stick to the positive experiences. Do not add negative afterthoughts or expectations for the future. Just stick to the

moment of the positive experience.

EXAMPLE

John wants to lose weight. It really is a struggle for him since he likes food so much. Diets and exercises are difficult for him.

It would be good for John to acknowledge in his diaty that he had lost a pound when he weighed himself. it would not be

i
&

effective to add the afterthought that he still needs to lose another 18 pounds...

Do not share your diary with others. This diary is personal and it is very helpful if you feel absolutely free to write down what
‘you experienced as positive. Having others sharing your diary probably activates your Inner Critic to phrase things different-

ly or to delete an experience or to add experiences to impress others.
Make a habit of doing it at the same time each day, For instance before your breakfast or before you go to sleep. Starting your
day this way with greater awareness of the positive things in your life can be most helpful. Closing the day with it might have

a more positive effect on your sleep {and dreams...)

Ifyou decide to write in your diary during the day it might be helpful to do a relaxation or meditation exercise to help your

awareness.

Exercises: Gratitude diary

Ifyou consider keeping a Gratitude Diary, then promise yourself to do it for at least a month. In the beginning you might find

it hard to find any positive experiences at all, Do realize that the longer you keep your diary, the easier it becdmes and the
i

more pleasure you get out of doing it. You will also notice that you will start to feel more accomplished and that things are

going easier for you, because you are becoming a more aware human being.

1 Suggested reading: The Four Levels of Healing by Shokti Gawain.
2 Read Shakti Gowain'’s book Creative Visualisation for practical suggestions. This book is available in many languages

The Doncer's Career
CAREER PLANNING

THE IMPORTANCE OF CAREER PLANNING

Some people love structure while others don’t. The same applies to planning a career. It is understandable that
people often do not plan their career. Life cannot be completely planned anyway. Life is full of surprises and that’s
what makes it interesting. On the other hand, rejecting planning completely doesn’t make life easier either. We
need balance. Maybe life brings you what you really need when you are more aware of your possibilities and needs.

And that is another way of viewing career planning... Textbook advantages of career planning for dancers are:

Careér Planning Advantages

IS WINNING THE ULTIMATE GORL?

The ‘will to win’ is enormously stressed in most sports. Our dance world is no exception to such thinking. The
*will to win’ is highly rated: you need it to achieve success. Unfortunately this ‘will to win’ is often confused
with the will to become better than others.

it is expected of competitive sportspeople that they say they want to win. Also dance competitors often expect
that others will count on them to have that ‘will to win’. You may ask yourself: ’Do I really want to win? Or am
T trying to fulfil the expectations of my partner, coach, trainer and friends?’. Many dancers do function within

the narrow concept of winning, without thoroughly thinking this through. Is it about beating others?

Part Three: The Dancer’s Career

By

This is a bizarre situation. Research shows that only a quarter of Western society has a need to be perceived as
better than others. Only they have that dominant wil! to win (or will to keep on winning). This will to win
depends on personal characteristics. Only the so-called Aspirers and Succeeders' need success to really feel good.

But even people in these groups could define ‘success’ as more than beating others.

DANGERS AROUND THE WILL TO WIN

It could be quite dangerous to stress the will the win. Really, there are some serious drawbacks in this desire.
First of all, the will to win is not something you can easily acquire. In fact, the will to win is a state of mind
people possess or not. Mental shifts - for instance, if one doesn’t have the will to win dominantly - are only pos-
sible over a great length of time, if ever possible at all.

Secondly - and more importantly - the will to win only helps during preparations and not so much during com-
petitions. This will to win can really help you to find the motivation to practise hard, to work out your lessons
well and so on. However, on the competition day the will to win can be very dangerous for you.

Focusing on winning {i.e. being better than others) means that you focus on uncontrollable factors. Realize that
winning doesn’t only depend on your performance. It also depends on the performance of your partner, the other
couples on the floor and the perception and appreciation of the adjudicators.

Focusing on uncontrollable factors leads to activity of the Autonomous Nervous System (A.N.S). This system
notices when you desire something that is not guaranteed to happen. From that moment on the A.N.S, starts to
‘protect’ you. The A.N.S. now sends warning signals to your brain that you may perceive as stress. It may also
start the production of chemicals in the blood that prevent you from using your body freely. Oddly enough, you
may not even be aware of these stress signals. You may experience stress at competitions as normal.

As well as sending stress signals, the A.N.S. also tries to protect you in another way. It wants to give you some secur-
ity by making the body do something the A.N.S. knows it can do very well. This is something your body and mind
have contro! over. The A.N.S. will go for a habit you have obtained in the past. Habits the A.N.S. is sure that you
can control. Whatever the habits are. This is the time when old habits take over. The newest skills - which are not
regarded as habits - are not activated. Old habits (most of the time not the best dance qualities) reign now.

On many occasions dancers lose their desired success by focusing too much on winning during their champion-
ship. They no longer feel free to dance to the best of their abilities. They don’t lose because of others but because

of their personal focus during the competition. You can avoid this unnecessary trap.?

HOW DO YOU CREATE PERSONAL SUCCESS?

Personal success might be winning. But not for all dancers. Success is a larger concept, one element of which
is self-improvement. This improvement can be defined in many ways.

For some, improvement means escape from daily life. Competitive ballroom dancing can fulfil that need. For
others, dancing brings socializing opportunities. Personal success could also mean becoming or being better than
others (winning). And yes, there are also dancers who would describe success as being able to express them-
selves through dance.

Whatever the definition of your personal success, improving the quality of your dancing requires the following:

Coreer Planning

Career Planning Advantages

Most dancers start their improvement by increasing the amount of time they practise. This begins with
Medallists. Beginners get a good taste of dancing and want to improve their skills. So they start to dance more.
Take more lessons and dance more often at social nights.

At the next stage in their dancing career, most start to improve the quality. They enter competitors’ classes and
start taking private lessons. At some stage they might start to feel uncomfoftable. Their improvements are
decreasing while they still want to improve. This is the time when other quality decisions are made: taking les-
sons from more acclaimed teachers, getting better outfits, preparing the body better by doing warm-ups and cool
downs, and monitoring food and beverage intake.

One day they may have increased the quality level of the input so much that hardly any improvements can be
made. These dancers have lessons from the best teacher(s}, they use the best choreographer(s), they wear the
best outfits, but still they feel there is more to be done. Somehow it all doesn’t add up?!

Taking a clear direction in your dance is therefore crucial to maintaining quality improvement. You need to iden-
tify your personal long-term goals. This means: you need to know what personal style you want to use within
the chosen discipline (Standard or Latin American dancing). Answers to these questions help you to meet your
goals. Answers to these questions jead to clarifying your personal direction.

Clear personal direction positively influences both quantity and quality aspects. Therefore it is never too early to
start working on your personal direction. Quantity and quality aspects also introduce further possibilities in rela-

tion to your direction. Therefore the success triangle can be defined as follows:

Direction

The Success Traingle

Quantity Quality

Part Three: The Dancer's Career

You achieve personai success when all three poles of the triangle are activated. By activating all three poles you

also get more pleasure out of your dancing. And this pleasure is considered to be the best motivation of all.

WHAT DO YOU PERSONALLY ADD TO YOUR DANCES?

In the Career Planning process you define the direction. In other words: you define the personal style within
Standard or Latin American dancing which you want to develop,

Given the universally accepted principles of Latin American and Standard dances, there are a million ways to
perform these dances. You can accentuate what you feel is most important (or what you can do very well). To
just mention a few: a pure technique interpretation, extra rhythmical accent, a dynamic interpretation, a fash-
ionable or showy style, a classical approach, and an authentic influence. In chapter 3-7 you can read more about

these and other possible style choices (or concepts).

Once you have found your appropriate direction, it is easier to apply the content of quantity and quality during

the season and for a competition. An example illustrates the importance of this kind of career planning.

EXAMPLE

Let us look at the following couple: they are 25 years; both are former ballet dancers, They are the number 3 Latin couple of
their country. They are talented. At the moment thetr career is stagnant. What would you advise them to do in terms of their
dance style? Should they keep on conforming to the general Latin principles and work harder and have more acclaimed teach-
ers? Or should they try to incorporate more of their ballet skills into their Latin dances?

Now remember that many adjudicators do not stimulate new ideas and mark accordingly. The chances of becoming Latin
champions would be the highest if they conform as much as possible. Let’s say a 60% chance, Their chance of success fin
this case winning) would be only 25% if they incorporated their ballet strengths. What should the couple do? What should

their coach advise them to do? Go for the highest chance of winning?

We really do not know enough about the couple’s mindset to answer this question. Most people are inclined to
say that they want to become champions. Some really long for that success but many more say so because it is
the done thing to say so. Most people only reflect a longing for success, because their coaches long for success...
So we first need to know who they are before we know what they really want.

THE POST-COMPETITOR CAREER

Having a long-term career strategy can help you not only during your active dancing career but also after you have
stopped competing. Many active competitors become dance teachers after they have retired from competing. Some
aim to have a social dance school. Others might want to become a competitor's teacher themselves. If you are one
of the latter, then it is important to recognize this ambition as soon as possible. Do you want to become a good
teacher or would you need to be an excellent teacher working with the finest couples in the world?

Good teachers - and certainly excellent teachers - have, of course, a sound all-over knowledge of ballroom danc-

ing. Next to that they also have a speciality.

Coreer Planning

If you would audit the top teachers in the world, you would probably agree that they excel in one or more points
like: extremely knowledgeable on technique, very creative in choreography (in one or more dances), in charac-
terization, in musicality, in dynamics, in mental support, in developing performing skills and so on. Do you wish
to become such a good or excellent teacher for competitors? Then find out in what area you would like to spe-
cialize. This is probably an area in which you feel very comfortable and in which you already have developed
extra skills and knowledge. During your career you could strengthen this interest. So by the time you retire, you
have already built a reputation of being specially skilful in that area. This would then attract competitors to that
speciality.

This strategy not only helps in your post-competitive years. During your active dancing years such a specialism
also grants you competitive advantage. In chapter 3-7 this competitive advantage is discussed more, while stress-

ing the importance of a dance concept.

EXAMPLE

Roy has a strong desire to become a good dancer. He enjoys working with amateurs on choreography, experimenting and even

creating evening shows for the social dance school he works for. When he Is taking lessons, he really gets excited when his

teachers work on new choreography. Actually, Roy would like to do just that but he realizes that only choreography will not

offer him the success he needs. Sometimes Roy gets frustrated when his teachers do not offer him the chance to develop his

own choreography. Or when they disapprove of his ideas. He doesn’t really understand why his work is good or bad.

During his career planning sessions Roy becomes aware of his desire to become a great choreographer one day. He decides

to put more effort into getting the best choreographies (while not neglecting the other dance preconditions).

To develop his skill Roy starts taking lessons with the most renowned ballroom dance choreographers. Not just to get better

choreographies but also to understand what they are doing and why. Furthermore Roy is taking workshops on choreography

outside the ballroom arena and leams from the principles taught there.

Roy builds up a large expertise and learns to translate the knowledge of choreography from contemporary dancing into ballroom

dancing, He continues to read books on choreographers and making choreography. Roy visits the theatre to see as many chore-
: ographies as he can. By doing so, he is creating a vast knowledge and skills that help him both during his active dancing years

and afterwards. It is a lot of work for him but he doesn’t mind that. Roy is developing what he likes best.

Port Three: The Dancer's Career

THE IMPORTANCE OF GOAL SETTING

Goal setting is a helpful technique in all stages of a dancer’s life. You might have already set more goals than you
are aware of. You do set goals when you plan - or merely think about - your progress and your desires in your
career. Or when you plan the next season or even when you plan for your next major competition.

This part of the book is restricted to goal setting with regard to your total career. Of course these principles also
apply to seasonal planning or the planning of the next important championship.

In sports psychology {and also dance psychology) the importance of realistic goal setting is stressed as the sound

foundation of all other mental activities. The reasons for spending time on setting realistic goals are:

CRITERIA FOR USEFUL GOAL SETTING

Whenever you set goals, the following four criteria are important:

SET THE GOALS PERSONALLY
Setting goals works best when you start by setting goals that suit you (and not your social environment). It takes

some time to really identify your personal goals. The following chapters might help you start the process.
Your goals might differ from your partner. Don’t let this stop you from finding what you really need. It blocks
you and the end result will not be very satisfactory. Having different goals does not mean that the two of you

cannot work with your differences. Open communication often guarantees real solutions for all involved.

MAKE YOUR GOALS INSPIRATIONAL
Some goals are truly personal while they seem to feel cold. The passion is not there. The goal is just there. Such
goals do not inspire. They are made out of habit or out of the expectations others have of us. Goals that make

your eyes sparkle fuel you with energy to go for it. When that sparkle is missing, your goal may not really fit your

personal desires.

Part Three: The Dancer's Career

SET REALISTIC GOALS
There is no use in setting goals that you cannot accomplish. ‘Cannot’ in terms of the time to realize the goals.
Or ‘cannot’ in terms of lack of skills, money, etc. It is important to realize that effective goals not only match

your desires but also your skills, talent, available time (daily and lifetime) and available money.

EXAMPLE

Personal goat setting can change people's direction and fulfilment. Let's look at Pierre and Monique. They have devoted all
their life 0 competitive dancing only. From the early years on, they have always danced both Standard and Latin American
dancing. It felt quite natural to remain Ten Dance competitors. Their Latin American dancing was always their favourite dis
cipline and the cornerstone of their success. Their Standard dancing was of much less quality.

Mentally and emotionally they both had a strong desire to win and earn good money. Ten Dance seemed to be the easiest
way to achieve these goals. They decided to spend the next few years working on all ten dances to become World Ten Dance
Champions. This implied that they needed to keep up their Latin American dancing at their current level. Pierre and Monique
also needed to improve their Standard dancing from quarter final level to a top three position in the final.

Pierre and Monique put hard work, time and money into this strategy. Their Standard dancing began to improve. After a few
years they managed to make one Standard dance in the final. Their Latin American dances, however, were suffering for it.
The result in Ten Dance stayed the same while the result in Latin American competitions was dropping by then.

Finally they realized that they would not manage to get their Standard dances into a top three position and keep up their Latin
American dances at the same time. Their goal to become World Ten Dance champion was unrealistic.

They decided to stop Ten dance to only focus on Latin American dances, Within a year their results in this discipline changed
enormously. They felt free and were able to do what they really liked best. Better results then followed almost automatically.
Also their financial situation improved. They no longer needed to spend fortunes on their Standard dancing. Through their

success in Latin American dancing they attracted more dancers for private lessons and booked more shows.

MATCH YOUR GOALS WITH YOUR PARTNER'S GOALS.

Should the personal goals of the partners in a team be fully equal? Life would be easier if goals perfectly
matched. Life would also become a little bit boring,

It is also possible that your personal goals differ so much that it is hard to function together. These differences
could lead to constant conflicts. Not knowing the differences in a partnership is always dangerous. People with
different goals react differently to external signals. Knowing the differences doesn’t solve all the problems but at
least makes it possible to attempt to bridge the differences with respect. Two very different people can become

very functional - and even inspirational - together.

THREE QUESTIONS TO DETERMINE YOUR LONG-TERM GOALS

To find the right direction for a dance couple you have to have insight into your personal goals. It is not correct to
assume that all dancers want to become champions in order to achieve fulfilment. So you need to find out what
makes individual dancers tick. First you need to know who you are before you really know what you want.

You help yourself - and your goal setting - when you answer the following three basic questions:

out how you are going to realize your goals. You define what kind of dance style or concept suits you best. The

Part Three: The Dancer's Career

DETEAMINE WHO YOU ARE

How do you find out who you are without consulting a psychologist? How does a teacher find out what drives
the two individuals of a dance: couple?

Let’s take a look at advertising. In this field, professionals need to know what drives people. It would be impos-
sible to send all potential customers to a psychologist. Yet they need to know how people feel and think, what
they know and what their expectations of life are. The Young and Rubicam Advertising group has done world-
wide research on this subject, It is called "Four C’s” (Cross Cultural Consumer Characterizations). Y and R inter-
viewed on several occasions over 10,000 people throughout Europe and the USA and identified seven personal-
ities, Each personality gives insight into the state of mind, behaviour and expectations. Each individual within a
certain personality group mostly reacts in the same way to external stimuli.

This Four C’s research can help dancers and teachers to find out more about who they are and consequently what
they want. Of course there are many other models' that could be applied, but Team Amsterdam was fortunate

to have access to this model for almost a decade and has found the results motivating.

THE FOUR C'S MODEL
The research stratifies society into three main groups: The Constrained, The Middle Majority and The Innovators.

Constrained group

People in the Constrained Group would love to belong to the largest group, the Middle Majority. Somehow they
feel that they will never manage to fit in with them. The Resigned make no effort to improve. The Strugglers
work very hard because they feel they must. Unfortunately they think they will never improve and will never
belong to The Middle Majority.

Middle majority group

Almost two thirds of most Western societies belong to The Middle Majority. These people enjoy following the
rules and values of society. In their behaviour they like to conform. More than a third have the Mainstreamers
characterizations and are content with whatever they achieve. Some 11% want more from life, work for it and
feel that they are able to reach most of their goals. They are called the Aspirers. Finally, the Middle Majority
group has a group of people who have already succeeded in fulfilling their goals. They principally would like to
keep their position and power. These Succeeders make up 14% of the total population. A lot of coaches and

(world) champions could probably be found in this group...

Innovator group

A growing number of people belong to the last main group: The Innovators. The younger people in this group
are called Explorers. They make up only 5% of the population whilst the older Reformers make up some 13%.
Both personalities are looking for their own values in life instead of following the mainstream values. They do
not always conform to the rules of society. They are not necessarily looking for confrontation but they feel they

need to follow their own ideas more than those of others.
