DETERMINE WHAT YOU REALLY WANT

As stated before, just a few people really dance for success and titles. In fact, it is the primary goal only for
Aspirers and Succeedets. For Explorers and Reformers a title can be very tempting as long as it does not inter-
fere with their personal goals. Mainstreamers like the social side of dancing much more than success. If they win,
it will be great but if they lose and enjoyed the competition it is fine too. The Strugglers and Resigned don’t
believe in their success, and if they do get it, they probably fear that they will lose it in no time.

Part Three: The Dancer's Career

MORE THAN ONE PERSONALITY GROUP

Most people do not Tecognize themselves in one group only. In fact, we have all groups in our personality. Some

dominaie, others are average and some are below or even far below average. The majority of people have two

to three groups above average. This means that these few groups indicate our behaviour the most.

€XAMPLE: MAN

Sergio’s will to become and be better than average (Aspirer and Succeeder} are clearly dominant. His Succeeder is not so
strong that it overpowers his Aspirer. We can assume that Sergio has a positive attitude to keep on improving. He also
dominantly likes to innovate (Reformer). His Reformer is stronger than his Explorer: his need for innovation is more balanced
and he doesn’t need to shock the world so much. His combination of Aspirer and Reformer gives Sergio the mental oppor-
tunity to become one of those dancers that make a difference in the dance world. He likes to innovate (for personal fulfilment
reasons} and balances that need so that others can appreciate it.

At the time of the test Sergio does not have any isolation group feelings (Resigned and Struggler} nor does he need to devote

much of his attention to his social life.

Ata first glance this man has quite an ideal chart for a top competitor. But he is not on his own. How does he
compare to his partner?

Determine what you really want

EXAMPLE: WOMAN

Lalla dominantly feels part of the isolation group Struggler. She always works hard and doesn’t need to be pushed to make
her work harder. However Laila feels that whatever she does will not work out positively for her. She works hard because she
(feels she has to. This kind of mental attitude towards life can easily become a self-fulfilling prophecy. Fortunately her second
and third dominant groups are the Aspirer and Succeeder.

Lalla sometimes feels left out of society. At other times Lalla feels she is doing well and can even improve. Making her aware

of these two parts within her personality helps Lalla to make conscious choices. Some regular counselling could be helpful to

learn to separate them. Unlike Sergio, Lalla does not appreciate innovative behaviour very much. She also has very little desire

to be a Mainstreamer.

MANAGING THE DIFFERENCES WITHIN ONE COUPLE
In these examples you can identify major differences between the two. This is quite a natural occurrence, since

opposites do attract. Being aware of these differences greatly helps them to cope with their differences. Let’s turn

back to these examples and put their two graphs together:

EXAMPLE: COUPLE

What first looked great in Sergio'’s graph could now easily become a threat. His will to innovate could make him a dancer that
could enrich the dance world with new views and progress. However, Lalla does not share this will to be different. In fact,
she already often feels left out of society (Struggler) and needs to develop selfacceptance to become part of society. Lalla’s

Aspirer and Succeeder could help her with that.

The more Sergio innovates, the more Lalla feels left out. She cannot recognize the value of it. She doesn’t want to be differ-

ent from the other dancers. She really wants to be the same (or sometimes better).

This situation has a few consequences, Lalla could work on her selfesteem through counselling. Sergio could learn not to
impose innovations too strongly and give Lalla more time to adopt them. She needs that time. Sergio's innovative behaviour
could also be fulfilled outside his dancing. It would probably be very wise not to improvise and innovate on the spot during
(or just before} a competition.

Sergio should also be aware that he perceives messages completely differently than Lalla does. if their coach would say, for

CHANGE IS POSSIBLE OVER TIME

Nobody stays the same throughout their life. Change doesn’t come overnight either but change does happen and
can be stimulated overtime. Being aware of your current state of mind is a good starting point. It helps to under-
stand the differences in your behaviour compared to that of your partner, your coach, your teacher(s) and other

important people in your life. Change could also be stimulated by, for instance, counselling and meditation. Let's

take a took at a case where major shifts took place over a period of two years:

EXAMPLE: CHANGE OVER TIME
In the first test Hiro has above average Struggler characteristics in his personality. This dropped considerably over a two-year
period to clearly below average. Knowing more about his personality, Hiro started to understand that his Struggler behaviour
was caused by internal conflicts between his innovative desires and his Aspirer. Knowing the origin of his conflicts, Hiro could
make more conscious choices about what to do when.

Moving away from his dominant isolation feelings, both his success groups {Aspirer and Succeeder) and innovator groups
(Explorer and Reformer} blossomed and reached higher (levels. Hiro started to feel much more successful and realized that

there was even more to achieve. He accepted his innovator groups much more and started to feel more accomplished.

Determine whot you really wont

CREATIVE VISUALIZATION ALSO HELPS TO FIND WHAT YOU WANT

Creative visualization is another way to find out what you really want. This is a meditation technique that
enables people to tune into their hidden desires. In a meditative state you could ask yourself what it is you real-
ly want out of dancing and visualize yourself doing it. You can easily check your creative visualization on its feasi-
bility: you will find it hard to visualize yourself doing something that does not suit you.

In the beginning you might find it hard to meditate at all but a daily practice of some five to ten minutes soon
proves effective. Once you have found the meditative state you might want to visualize something that doesn’t
fit you. After some time you will give up directing your mind and let it flow naturally. That will be the time when.
your inner guide tells you what you really desire.

In section 2-8-7 you can find some meditation and visualization techniques used for other purposes. You could

use the same techniques to tune in with your inner self to find out what it is you really want out of dancing.

SUGGESTION
Shakti Gawain has written a very practical guide to meditation and creative visualization. It is called ‘Creative Visualisation’

and is published in over twenty different languages.

VOICE DIALOGUE: AN ALTERNATIVE TO FIND WHAT YOU WANT
*’A third method of finding out what you really want is called Voice Dialogue. This method has been developed
by Dr Hal and Sidra Stone in the USA to increase awareness of all parts of your personality. Voice Dialogue is now

widely used by psychologists and therapists around the world. You can read more about this method in section
2-8-9,

DETERMINE WHAT YOU CAN DO
Knowing who you are makes it easier to know what you really want. Setting dance goals by only looking at what

you want is a dangerous thing. Wishing and reality are not always compatible. To define your dance career goals

we also need to have an objective look at our resources in terms of:

To determine what you really can do asks for honesty and objectivity. It is very hard for most people to be object-
ive about themselves or their partner. Therefore it is advisable to discuss your resources with knowledgeable,
objective and honest people. A lot of dance teachers find it hard to be honest. It is not easy to inform somebody
on his or her limitations. So if a dancer really wants to learn about his or her resources, real openness is crucial.
In other words: make it very clear to your teacher you are ready to accept the truth.

Realize that it is far better to know your limitations than to hide from them. Knowing them makes it possible to

set feasible goals. Knowing them makes it possible to compensate for weaknesses...

PHYSICAL ASSETS
Have a good look at your body. Which discipline suits your body type/build best? Standard, Latin American or

both? Or do you think other dance forms are more appropriate for you?

ABILITY TO ACQUIRE SKILLS
It is necessary to determine your ability to acquire skills. What could be the highest level you could reach? It might
seem very unusual to talk about your potential. People tend to be shy on this subject. Shy to ask but also shy to lis-

ten. Your coach or teachers could help you out. Especially when you show you want an honest answer!

EXAMPLE

Your ability to acquire skills ‘might be too limited to match the needs of your Achiever personality. This knowledge could help
you to find your own path to happiness in ballroom dancing. And that doesn’t always need to be as a top competitor. Such
dancers might find formation dancing very rewarding. Formation dancing might match both ability and mental structure. But
there are many other alternatives too. You might even find that ballroom dancing isn't for you. That you would be a good jazz

ballet dancer or show dancer or flamenco dancer...

PERSONAL EXAMPLE

It took me some years following my own path to becoming the author of this book. In my younger years | was probably an
Aspirer (although I wasn’t aware of it). 1 also had very limited dance talents and | only reached the Dutch sub-top in Standard
dancing as an amateur. In the early years my ASPIRER was quite happy but in the end - when no higher results could be real
ized - my Aspirer felt stuck. At that time I also had access to my Explorer and / liked to transform ballroom dancing into total
concepts. | created shows for cruise liners with total concepts (the music, the choreography and the looks all telling one story).

I didn’t sell five dancers but short stories. The audiences appreciated this so my Aspiter felt quite successful.

Aas I grew older my dance skills decreased. However, my love for ballroom dancing remained. I saw no way of
fulfilling the needs of my Aspirer and Explorer through dancing. I started to develop my strategy skills in com-
munications and one day | was asked to become co-founder of the Dutch National DanceSport Academy. | start-
ed to give lectures on strategy planning for competitive dancers (dance is a non-verbal form of communication)
and couples started to book me for personal guidance. This work grew and grew. I studied psychology to improve
my facilitating skills. In the end my practice became so successful that I had to stop my regular job in commu-
nications.

After my first experimental years, the time was right to put my thoughts down in this book for competitive

dancers. My Aspirer was pleased. I had found my personal road to being part of ballroom dancing.

TIME: AVAILABLE FOR DANCING
Time is also an important matter for consideration. You might be talented in both the disciplines Standard and
Latin American Dancing. But do you have enough time to devote to ten dances? Ten dances demand double

practice and lesson time compared to one discipline.

For Strugglers available time is very crucial. They already possess a negative view on the possible outcome. So if
they do not have enough time to develop the required skills they will see it as another reason for not ever manag-
ing. For Mainstreamers this question of time is less important since they enjoy dancing and socializing. If you are
an Achiever or Succeeder you want to have success. So if the time needed for the improvement of ten dances is
not available, it is better to select five dances and do them well. For Explorers and Reformers time is also a rele-

vant question. How much time would you need to develop the skills resulting in your personal fulfilment?

TIME: YEARS LEFT FOR COMPETITIVE DANCING

There is another dimension of time to consider: age! How much time do you still have to develop your potential?
Many people do not want to talk about this. They want to ignore it because getting older makes them feel uncom-
fortable in this world. Yet everybody gets older and there will come a time when you can no longer physically pro-
duce what is needed. Being aware of your time horizon makes it easier to make the right decisions. Combine what

you need to do to satisfy your personality, what is physically possible and how much time it would take.

Determine what you can do

EXAMPLE
In the example of the former Ten Dance couple in chapter 3-2 the dancers decided to stop Ten Dance and only focus on Latin
American dancing. Their choice was based on both the available time in terms of lessons and practice as well as the years left

to dance as competitors. ~

MONEY
There will always be the matter of money. What can you afford? Competitive dancing is quite costly. Especially
if you do not make the top. Limited money should be allocated as effectively as possible. This might mean that

dancing two disciplines could simply bé too expensive. Be wise!

MENTAL FLEXIBILITY

Next to the ability to acquire skills, the physical assets, and available time and money, there is more to consider
with regard to what you can do, Some people have the mental flexibility to do many things at the same time and
feel good about that. Others find it hard to focus on a few things. Ballroom dancing demands a lot from your
mental capacity. Doing five or ten dances makes a great difference, not only in mental capacity but also in terms
of the amount of stress you can handle. Ten dances means more teachers, more travel, more competitions, more
wardrobes, more practice, etc.

Be honest and ask yourself how much you really can handle mentally. Do realize that if you like both disciplines,
you do not necessarily have to dance both of them competitively. You could consider dancing, for instance,

Standard competitions and South American Show dances as a professional...

Part Three: The Dancer's Career

QF A CONCEPT

MPOR TANCE

THE IMPORTANCE OF A DANCE CONCEPT

Knowing your long-term career goals is one thing. How to realize them is another long-term question. The long-
term strategy planning should now culminate in a clear dance concept.

A concept is an overall consistent image of how you would like to be perceived by others. The concept clearly
defines the special assets of the two of you in a way that suits you best and makes them unique.

The most vague way to define a concept is to say that you are dancing ballroom dances. There is no uniqueness

in that concept. All other competitors on the floor are doing exactly the same.

An example. No washing powder sells on the market if it is advertised as something that washes. Marketing spe-
cialists know that their brands need something extra. So the first discriminative step is communicating what kind
of washing powder it is: for white or colour washes. Dancers could discriminate Standard and Latin American
dances, ;

Marketers know that a brand needs an extra reason to be preferred above others. It needs to be specifically bet-
ter in one aspect. That is the reason why some washing powders stress that they are the cheapest, others claim
that they eliminate dirt the best, certain brands claim that they are best for the environment or are very safe for

delicate fabrics. You could make use of the same marketing technique to reach your long-term goals.

In the dance world you are trained to become the best possible dancers. This focus leads easily to putting tech:
nique on a pedestal. Trying to be the best technical couple is therefore the most common concept. Since it is
used so widely there is hardly any distinction in it. Basically it is not a concept but a precondition! Every couple
needs to produce the best possible technique anyway. It is almost like a bank telling you that your money is safe
with them. Such a concept wouldn’t make that bank unique. All banks should be safe.

Technique is very important overall, however it is not the only thing that counts. Likeability for a couple is per-
haps as important for success as pure technical skill. Likeability is related to our personal interpretations. Do we
like their dancing style? Do we like the image they create? Do we like their choreography? Do we like their look?
Do we like their behaviour? Judging competitions is not only to do with skills. There is also a matter of credit-

ing the total aspects of a couple.

Let’s make this clearer, What would you do as an adjudicator with two equally skilled couples on the competi-
tion floor? Would you look for the best possible heel turn? What happens if another couple then has a better rise
at the end of three? In the end both couples would prove to have equal skills. What would happen now? One
part of the judges/audience might like couple A more because they have a sophisticated interpretation while oth-
ers might like the dynamic approach of couple B more.

Following the rules of marketing it is safe to state that the couple which produces the strongest total concept will

win (despite their equality in dancing skills!)

In this list of possible overall dance concepts you do not find the Man-Woman concept, which is often referred

The importonce of a concept

PERCEPTION OF SOME GREAT CHAMPIONS
Current and previous world champions are highly technically skilled dancers. They fulfil not only the precondi-

tion of technique but also tend to add special assets to their technical skills, Some of these great champions might
not even have been aware of their personal concept at atl. They naturally portrayed it. Others may try to build
on their concept with greater awareness. You could help your career by making concept choices with greater
awareness so the effect can be perceived. Here are a few concept interpretations of some past and current cham-

pions as inspiration.

Some Standard champions could be perceived as having an accent on: Technical excellence or Sophistication or
Athletic impact or Dynamical and experimental or Unexpected and sensual or Easy going or The beauty of simplicity.
Some Latin American champions could be perceived as having an accent on: Dominating man and willing lady or
Rhythmical and trendsetting choreography or Artistic or Nice and perfect or Fashionable and showy or Showy or

Clever and showy or Fashionable and artistic or Sensitivity and contemporary choreography or Equality in partnership.

Part Three: The Dancer's Career

we

THE CHOSEN CONCEPT SHOULD SUIT YOUR PERSONALITY
The list of possible concepts is infinite. The choice is yours. Be creative with it. But there is also a warning. The

chosen concept should not only suit your skills (What can you do) but also your personality.

EXAMPLE

Let’s get back to the question about the couple with the ballet training from page 256. The question was: 'What would you
advise them to do in terms of dance style? Should they keep on conforming to the general Latin principles and work harder?
Or should they try to incorporate more of their ballet skill into their Latin dances?’

The advice depends much on who they are and what they want. If their personalities match the Aspirers or the Succeeders,
they should look into working harder and conforming to the reigning style as much as possible. For the Mainstreamers the
ultimate goal would not be to win the title. They would use the reigning style and be happy with their result. The Struggters
and Resigned also conform but need mental assistance in breaking away from the negative thoughts of failure. However, if
the couple consist of Explorers or Reformers they would probably feel much better if they could integrate thetr ballet skills

more into their dancing.

THE USE OF ROLE MODELS

Role models can be very helpful for developing personal concept ideas. Realize that you are not aiming to
become the role model. The role model leads you in the direction of your own path. If you use, for instance, 4
current top dancer as a role model than it is wise not imitate him/her. Look for the qualities that appeal to you
and develop these qualities in your own personal way. Do not copy the total concept of your role model. Also
check if you can develop these skills yourself.

Role models can also be found outside the ballroom arena. Role models can be your favourite singer, actor,
teacher or TV host. Whoever you like. Again look for the qualities this role model represents and see if these
qualities suit you now (or in the future}. Often people select role models to compensate for aspects they do not
possess themselves. This is ill advised. It is very hard to bring qualities that are in your shadow personality into

your primary personality.

IMPLICATIONS OF THE CHOSEN CONCEPT
At ail times you should be clear about your concept. There is no point in having a sophisticated dancing style
and wearing a very contemporary dress. Nor is it advisable to wear a classical Latin dress while dancing in what

would be characterized as ‘postmodern’, ‘contemporary artistic’ or for example ‘showy’.
The chosen concept influences all aspects you have to deal with during a season. You could choose a sophisticat-

ed concept to match your sophisticated personality and skills. You could add a sophisticated dance style, chore-

ography, dress, hairstyle, make up and publicity. All these aspects would make the concept a lot clearer.

The importance of a concept

LONG-TERM GOALS AND PERSONAL CONCEPTS

In the previous chapters suggestions were made to find answers to the questions: know who you are, what you

want and what you can do, Your long-term goals can be effectively planned.

There is no mathematical way of bringing the three questions together to establish your long-term goals. It is an
individual process for each dancer that can be facilitated by a mental trainer, a coach, a dance teacher or even a

psychologist.
The process is not always easy or clear. But starting with the right data helps you to discover what you really

want to get out of your dancing and how to combine that with your resources.
It isn’t that easy to create a clear-cut concept by yourself. You really have to get into your personality, your (hid-
den) desires and your assets and weaknesses objectively. Find a skilled and trustworthy facilitator to work with

you on your concept. Use the questionnaire on the next two pages as a guideline.

Phases of concepting

Port Three: The Dancer's Career

Phase 1: Fill in the form individually

{t is important to complete the questionnaire individually and get through the following phases together. Filling
in the form yourself increases your awareness of what you really want and how you can get there. Of course
your perception of yourself is not always compatible with reality. But it is better to start yourself, (instead of let-
ting others tell you what they think of you). You can find aut later what is feasible. This process really increas-

es your awareness and thus control over your own life.

Phase 2: Look for the common ground

Very often the two individuals in a couple are not alike. The man may be a technical and classical dancer while
the woman may be dynamical and expressive. Weaknesses and strengths become clearer. But it helps to find the
common ground between them. Also being aware of mental differences is helpful. The same applies, of course,
to the goal setting (since this is greatly influenced by who you are).

Start making concepts based on the profile of the two individuals. Then compare the two concepts you have
found. The first check is which of the concepts lies in the common ground best. In other words, which of the

concepts is appropriate for both male and female dancer.

Common

ground —

Phase 3: Look for the competitive strengths
In the next phase, check whether the concept will stand out on the competition floor. is the concept new? Has

it been used before? Would it be acceptable for the dance scene (adjudicators, coaches, audience, etc.}?

Phase 4: Check the compatibility
In this phase you do an audit on the compatibility of the concept in all aspects of the seasonal planning: chore-

ography, technique, look, promotion, competition selection, practice, study, etc.

Phase 5: Make the long-term concept decision

After the audit of Phase 4 the first concept decision could be made. It’s an important decision you cannot take
lightly. It will rule your dance life for at least a few seasons. Both of you should feel completely at ease with the
concept. If not, one of you will become counterproductive in the execution phases. This would certainly lead to

an unclear concept.

Phase 6: Increase awareness of the chosen concept
Reading books, searching for role models (inside or outside our ballroom dance world}, watching movies, visiting
the theatre, listening to music, discussions, meditation: they all lead to a greater knowledge of the concept you

desire. You need to build up the greatest library in your head of all possibilities related to the chosen concept.

Long term gools and personal concepts

NOTE
For some couples there may be a direct click when they start to work on their ‘personal concept. Others need time to think it
over and discuss it many times, Take that time. It is important. And even for those who feel the click immediately, take some

time to feel and see how it might affect your whole career... The more you work on it, the clearer the chosen concept becomes.

Phase 7: Use mood boards

You could start the execution phase of the concept by using mood boards. Mood boards are a collage of clippings
from books and magazines. You can use all kind of pictures. They may come from fashion magazines, current
affairs magazines, movie magazines and so on. Include all kind of pictures: fashion, the desired atmosphere, an
interior, a picture of art, pictures of colours, etc.

The mood board makes talking about the concept much easier for all involved. Primary team members? could take
out the pictures they feel are not appropriate together, building up the right combination or combinations,

Such a mood board session makes the concept clear. The process of creating the desired look for the couple can

now commence. The mood board proves to be helpful for the dress designer, hairdresser and make-up artist.

Phase 8: Execute the concept
The long-term strategy sessions make you more aware of what you want and what the concept is you are look-

ing for. In the execution phase you have to put the concept into action.

EXAMPLE

A Japanese couple. They are Standard dancers. They are small and they are very aware of their height. They try to copy the
reigning British style for Standard dancing as much as they can. Results are good but they feel unsatisfied. Dancing competi.
tions causes a lot of stress. During their career planning process they realized that they might be small compared to others but
that they shouldn't compare themselves with others on this weakness. They increased the awareness of their personal strengths
and learned to focus on that during competitions. This already started to give them much more freedom and to produce the qual-
ities they possessed. When discussing their personal concept they started to realize that copying the British style as best as
they could didn’t ft their innovative mindset. They also understood that an imitation would always lose from an original (being
really British). They needed to find out what their cultural inheritance meant for thetr dancing, For them it meant that they
liked to be more abstract. The woman also liked to show subdued emotions and put more accents on her Partner. In their

appearance they selected a more abstract and Japanese style of dress.

The long-term strategy sessions bring a road full of surprises where you will find the hidden treasures inside your-
self. Like Martha Graham said so well before: there is only one you. And if you block it, it will never exist
through any other medium... You do not get it for free but your search and your efforts will be rewarded!

The world will not have it.

The value of showdance

MORE VALUE THAN JUST ANOTHER CHANCE OF A TITLE

Working on show dance offers dancers more than a chance of winning titles and bookings for demonstrations.

‘Via this medium you have the opportunity and freedom to discover and create your personal contribution to the
dance world. There is much more control and freedom than in ’regular’ competitions: music, choreography,

interpretations, look, etc.

Show dance criteria

This medium also offers you a possibility to dance in the spotlight. There is nobody else on the floor. Instead of
having judges watch you for just a few seconds they now watch you for your entire performance. This can be

inspirational and offers you a chance to show your (unique) skills.

CRITERIA FOR CREATING SHOW DANCES
When you select show dance as an opportunity to accelerate your ‘regular’ dance career you could address the

following criteria. Your show dance could...

ADD TO YOUR PERSONAL DANCE STYLE

Show dance offers you minutes of undivided attention from judges. You offer them a chance to reconsider their
opinion of you based on their own perception of you in competition. Such new perceptions can be helpful.
Some dancers opt for a show dance concept that accentuates one dance only (instead of connecting all five
dances). They choose their strongest dance. These coupies invest time becoming more knowledgeable about that

particular dance and increase their skills and versatility. Prior to creating the show dance they go back to the

Part Three: The Dancer's Coreer

Bex

roots of the selected dance. They study, for example, Flamenco, Argentine Tango, Melonga or tap dance. It takes
them a few years to gain real insight into the new material. They increase their ‘regular’ dance skills, increase
their personal contribution to the specific dance and create a well-founded show dance.

Once in a while dancers try to create a show dance that compensates for their weaknesses. These dancers hope-

fully try to learn from that but it is questionable whether it is helpful for them to do so in show dance.

EXAMPLE

Todd and Paula’s strengths as Standard dancers are showing ‘Lightness’, ’Flexibility’, ‘Freedom’, ‘Sensitivity’ and ‘Energetic
linkage’ | They have had many complaints that they are not visible enough on the competition floor because they lack ‘Direct’
and 'Strong’ dynamics. They look too sweet and they attract less attention. They do not get the results they feel they should
get during ‘regular’ competitions. Todd and Paula decide to work on their weaknesses via their show dance.

They created choreography mainly consisting of ‘Direct’ and ’Strong’ movements combined with an impersonal, abstract
approach towards each other. Their dance lacked humanness and looked quite mechanical.

Their choice shocked the audience. First of all because it was too one-dimensional. Secondly because they were not skilled
enough for such a concept and the believability of the show dance was clearly lacking, And finally - worst of all - it didn’t

match their personalities and image in any way. It felt like Barbara Streisand singing heavy metal music...

DEEPEN YOUR KNOWLEDGE AND SKILLS

When you use material from other dances/dance styles - for instance Argentine Tango, Mambo, Salsa, Flamenco,
tap dance or lift work - it takes time to develop the skill to control the execution of that new material (longer
than your usual time span to adopt new material in ballroom dancing). Particularly when you do not want to
make it look under-rehearsed or a cliché interpretation of the original. You might even need a few years to real-

jy master dance skills specific to other dance styles.

HAVE A CLEAR CHOREOGRAPHICAL CONCEPT
Look at your strengths as a dancer, What is it that you uniquely offer the dance world? Make use of such
strengths. Become aware of them and use them as a guide for creating choreography that heips you to accentu-

ate your strengths. Using strengths does not imply that you only show them and nothing else.

EXAMPLE

Gerry and Hannah are able to use the dynamics ‘Direct’ and ‘Strong’ well. It becomes boring when their show dance only
offers material with these dynamics, It would help to also include the opposites like ‘Flexible’ and ‘Free’. Their dance would
be less predictable (and less boring) and - what is even more interesting - their primary dynamics would look even more

’Strong’ and ‘Direct’.
Show dances often have titles. This can be helpful in maintaining a tighter control on the concept for the cre-
ators, the performers, the judges and even the audience. If the name is too vague, the concept and choreography

is probably the same.

The value of show dance

Section 3-8-2

Mass communications

MASS COMMUNICATIONS FOR COMPETITIVE DANCERS

Both amateur and professional dancers as well as teachers and coaches experience moments when they are in
the news (free publicity), create publicity themselves (advertising) or are sponsored to create exposure for some
product or service. In this chapter some general information on advertising, free publicity and sponsoring is

given to help you understand what works and what doesn’t.

ADVERTISING BY PROFESSIONALS

It is common for professionals in the ballroom world to advertise in programmes, dance newspapers, magazines
and even on the internet. They may have several reasons for doing this: sponsoring the event by advertising in
the programme, informing the audience and judges about your competing in the event, informing the dance
world that you are still active (for retired dancers who have taken up teaching/adjudicating/lecturing activities),

informing the audience about your shows and demonstrations, and so on.

ADVERTISING PREREQUISITE: CREATE ADEQUATE IMPACT

Good advertising has one basic rule: you want adequate impact. Most professionals run a picture of themselves
and put their names on it. As this is common, it needs to be done very well to get attention. The most beauti-
ful picture in your advertisement would bring you some positive attention. But is that enough? If you do want
to advertise effectively it is wise to differentiate from the rest. You could have a more interesting message: tell
your audience what you are about. Inform them about the personal dance concept you are building up or inform
them about what you have to offer as a teacher. Advertisements could give the audience some insights into your
uniqueness. Your long-term career goals and concept could attract effective attention.

On the following page you will find a questionnaire that may help you to discover the answer to the question

"What do you really want to tell your audience?’

THREE RULES MAY HELP YOU IMPROVE YOUR ADVERTISEMENTS

Some basic principles for an effective ‘Look’ can also be applied to advertising:

RECOGNITION. REQUIRES DIFFERENTIATION...\

Moss communications

ome

~

Recognition requires differentiation
Being different helps you to get noticed. You may want to show yourself if it says something significant about
you, but is it necessary to show yourself as a dance couple in a competition outfit? There are many other ways

to be creative and make an impact.

EXAMPLES

Ifyou want your audience to know you have the best footwork in the world then maybe a close-up of your feet is more intrigu-
ing than showing all of you.

If your concept is about sensitivity you may want to show two touching hands.

If everybody is showing pictures you may get more attention by just putting some copy on a full for double) page.

If everybody is using normal copy you may consider using poetry to get your message across.

if you have practised like never before and feel you are ready to take the title then a headline stating ‘Blood, sweat and tears’
in combination with your best ever picture might do the trick.

Advertisements with no copy at all may be very intriguing too. Especially if you are very well known. A good picture can say

more than a thousand words...

Recognition requires repetition

for your audience to take in your message, you need to repeat the same message over and over again. We get
over six thousand commercial stimuli a day. That is one reason why we need to repeat ourselves and to differ-
entiate from others. Another reason is that the-human mind is not quick in adopting commercial messages as
truthful. Consistency heips.

You could repeat the same good advertisement in several different programmes, papers, and magazines or even
repeat them over several years. Many premium-brands do advertise in this way so why wouldn't it work for you?
You could also consider repeating the same design in_all your materials. Your advertisements, business cards,

mailings, contracts and website: they could all add up and convey the same message.

KISS: Keep It Stupidly Simple

in between all the six thousand commercial stimuli, the human eye gets easily tired. Information that is not pre-
sented well is unlikely to be processed. If too many ingredients demand attention in your advertisements, hard-
ly anybody will really look at them. Your audience does not know where to start. They stop taking in any infor-

mation.
Ask yourself the following: are you trying to be the grocery shop attempting to sell many different products or

do you want to be a specialized shop? An premium-brand? The fatter applies: less is more!

EXAMPLES
Stating only one message helps. The most important one of course.
Deleting a lot of copy like ‘available for demonstrations, teaching and judging’ helps. If you are a professional, people expect

you to do these jobs anyway.

Mass communications

SPONSORING

Dancing has been recognized as a sport in many countries. Ballroom dancing might even become an Olympic
sport one day. That may still be a long way away but ballroom dancing as a sport seems to be attracting grow-
ing attention.

Top sportspeople are largely financed by sponsors of international premium-brands in many different areas: not
only the sports industry sponsors them but also many other sectors like personal care, the automobile industry,
the electronics industry, airlines, luxury products like perfumes, watches and fashion and so on.

For dancers such offers of sponsorship are still a dream. Our dance sport still lacks media exposure which would
encourage more industries to sponsor dances. Yet it is possible for you. to get sponsorship. Your choice is still

somewhat limited to suppliers to our sport like the dressmakers, the dance shoe industry and the music indus-

uy.

NO TRIAL AND ERROR

In our dance world dance-related sponsors may find you - or - through your network you may help them find
you. Sponsors from outside our dance world may need a more proactive attitude.

It is useless to try to find such a sponsor by trial and error. Look for relevancy. Maybe the relevance is that you
work for a large company who might be willing to sponsor you. Maybe friends of yours work at such companies
and can arrange the right introduction. You could look for companies with relevant products for dancing, like
make-up, tights but also dance-related products/services like travel organizations, music labels, dress makers,
shoe producers, etc. You may even look for sponsors who need your dancing as part of their roadshow, exhib-
itions or showroom activities. The car industry has sponsored dancers to liven up their sales shows. If you feel

you would like to do work like this you may want to get in contact with such sponsors.

Find help from people around you whenever you are looking for a personal sponsor. Don’t do it yourself. This
does not give a professional impression and it makes it harder for you to negotiate the best deal. Ask somebody
else to do it for you. Supply them with relevant information. Make personal contact..Just sending a letter or a

fax does not work.

START WITH WHAT YOU HAVE TO OFFER AND NOT WITH WHAT YOU NEED’

Companies are overloaded with requests for sponsoring. Their selection highly depends on corporate relevance
(what is in it for our company), top management interest (personal interest and relationships), costs and returns
(in terms of exposure or services).

Ballroom dancing still offers relatively little exposure for most companies. Maybe you put the name on your suit
or dress but the value would be negligible for them. But if you are really good you might get press interviews in
which you could mention your sponsor. You might offer them shows, demonstrations and/or master classes in
return. Master classes for their personnel or for their business contacts...

On the following page you will find a sponsor application form from a major corporation. So you have an idea

of what companies need to know from you...

THE LECTURE CONTENT SEEMS TO HAVE LITTLE IMPACT
In chapter 1-1 research in communications showed that what people say counts for a mere 7% of how the pre-
sentation is received. The larger contributors are your voice (accounting for 38%) and especially your body lan-

guage (accounting for more than half: 55%).

The effect distribution of your communication Factors

The conclusion is that however good the content of your lecture might be, success will be determined by your
voice and body language! This does not imply that you just disregard the content and only work on your voice
and body language. At the moment of presentation the content is less relevant for the receiver. For you as a
sender the content has a much higher impact and that is reflected in your voice and body language. For most
people a weil-structured, content-rich and rehearsed lecture brings peace of mind. It brings them the self-confi-
dence that receivers pick up in their body language and voice.

The research is still vital. It tells you to prepare the content well in advance. At the moment of the presentation you

can forget about the importance of the content. Your focus then changes to handling your voice and body language.

In this chapter all three factors shall be dealt with in the following five sections:

CHAOS AND STRUCTURE
With the Pre-Lecture-Questionnaire you may find your first raw idea for the content of your lecture. It is then
time to start collecting ideas you can use in the lecture. These ideas may consist of information, demonstrations,

exercises, discussions, etc. Later you structure the ideas into a lecture. This. is how it works:

Have a brainstorm of ideas

Don’t be critical: you first want to have as many ideas you can find.

Describe every idea in short

Put it on single cue cards. Collect as many ideas as you can find.

Put the cards in iogical groups
These groups are the chapters in your lecture. Keep the cards that do not fit in any group aside. Maybe you can
use them later.

Put the groups in a logical order.

Creating and presenting lectures

Teaching methods are for instance:
Live demonstration by yourself, by others or by video.
Asking questions (maybe even using forms to support that). :
Discussing with the audience or lettering the audience discuss with each other.
Letting the audience summarize the content to their neighbours.
Having the audience experience exercises. ‘ an
Giving a part of the audience the role of observer while the other part is doing the exercise. Giving the observers a clear
instruction what to observe and how to give feedback to those who are actually doing the exercise.
Let the dancers change partners.
Let the audience find out the truth themselves by seeing or by experiencing instead of by telling them.

Check with the audience if you have been clear and can go on or if you need to continue to explain

Check what the highlights per group are
Do you have enough time to make each point? Or do you need to skip information and exercises. Less mostly

creates more impact.
Try out your lecture a few times without an audience.
Does the structure still feel logical? Or do you need to restructure it? Do you have enough time? Or do you still

need to cut down on information, demonstration, discussions and/or exercises?

Reread the answers to your Pre-Lecture Questionnaire

Check once more if the lecture fits all requirements.

Creating and presenting lectures

MORE BREATH IS NEEDED FOR MORE SOUND

This statement is untrue. the real facts are:

For more sound little extra breath is needed.

‘Strong breathing causes tension in your chest and throat.

A strong inhalation also needs to leave your lungs immediately and strongly. this causes a cramped sound,

More sound needs peace and space!

GOOD DICTION DEMANDS MORE US€ OF MUSCLES IN MOUTH AND JAWS

This statement is untrue. The real facts are:

Overactive articulation with lots of mouth gymnastics leads to cramped jaws and prevents your tongue from subtle use.
The sound will be sharp, tense and have less volume.

Good articulation is created by ‘your tongue, the corners of your mouth and a loose jaw.

A BIG ROOM NEEDS TO BE FILLED WITH MY VOICE
This statement is untrue. The real facts are:
Your vocal chords cannot have continuous pressure. Such pressure makes your voice hoarse.

Relaxation helps you to let the voice resonate. Concentrate on relaxing the pelvis and abdomen-area.

VOICE IS CREATED BY THE THROAT AND LUNGS

This statement is untrue, The real facts are:

This leads to too much force in the throat and high and short breathing. The sounds-you produce in this way become throaty,
colourless and tack conviction.

The power in your voice comes from your abdomen while the articulation comes from lips and tongue.

MISUSE OF THE VOICE
Ten of the most common misuses of the voice and the effect of that on the audience are:
SPEAKING TOO SOFTLY
Your audience does not pay attention.
SPEAKING TOO LOUDLY
This causes irritation from your audience {but is still better than Speaking too softy). It creates distance from your audience.
Your audience doesn’t feel it is taken seriously,
BAD DICTION AND BAD.AATICULATION
Your audience does not pay attention.
THE COLD START
Your voice cracks and hesitates. You now start with a bad first impression, which takes a lot of effort to be compensated for.
When you start by slightly clearing your throat you create a bad first impression.
THE TOO FAST START

This mostly comes with a flat intonation. Your audience becomes restless and less concentrated.

Creating and presenting lectures

Start with a low pitch and end with a high pitch. Repeat it once more play with low and high pitches in your voice. Stretch
yourself out to finish this exercise.

SINGING SONGS -

After the previous two exercises you start to feel more relaxed and happy. Help your voice now by Singing your favourite song.
It might be a song from your childhood, a pop song, a religious song. Any song will do. It won't be hard to find such a song
if you have done the previous two exercises well. A song will pop up freely. :

NONSENSE SENTENCE

Close your voice warm-up by expressing @ nonsense sentence with various emotions. Such a sentence could be: Behind the
open church a pink cow was riding a bicycle’. Express this sentence in different ways without changing the words. Think of-
gentle, convincing, shy, proud, sensual, sad, happy, joking, caring, etc. Put it as a question, as an attack, an objective state-

ment, etc.

SUGGESTIONS FOR THE USE OF YOUR VOICE DURING A LECTURE
The following points of attention help you to improve your speech during your lecture. It might be worth lis-

tening to a tape of your most recent lecture and checking how you handled the following issues.

CHECK THE RIGHT POSTURE

Walk slowly to the spot where you will start to speak. First check your posture before you start speaking. Divide the weight
over both your legs, stand up straight yet relaxed.

BREATHE LOW AND SLOW

Do not gasp for breath. Make the rhythm slow.

SPEAK SO LOUD THAT EVEN THE BACK ROWS CAN HEAR YOU CLEARLY

Do not force yourself, Just fill yourself with sound and speak more slowly than in a normal conversation.

MAKE VARIATIONS IN THE SENTENCE MELODY

Use different sentence structures. Use different emotions in the voice (personal, detached, objective, caring, joking, defend-
ing, proud, etc). Use different sentences lengths. Use a break in between new blocks of information. Accentuate what is real-
ly important using your voice. Use different voice levels: soft, medium, and loud.

AVOID FILLERS

For instance... you know.

For instance... er.

For instance... of course.

For instance... yes?!

For instance... for instance.

Creating ond presenting lectures

OVERACTIVE USE OF THE BODY

Some lecturers seem to need to check their body to see if all is still there. They touch their nose, ears, chin, hair, pockets,
belt, rings, bracelets, etc.

Some lecturers seem to be itchy in some ‘unhandy’ places. They need to scratch such places regularly (not to take away the
itching but to release them from stress...)

TOO STATIC USE OF SPACE

The lecturer stays stationary far too long while he or she speaks.

OVERACTIVE USE OF SPACE

Some lecturers are restless and walk up and down the available space.

IMPORTANT BODY LANGUAGE ATTENTION POINTS
As stated before: follow your intuition and try to present yourself and your material in a natural way. What is
missing can be learned but you need to make use of the following points in a natural way. The classic philoso-

phers already knew that: ars artem celare! which means ’The art to conceal subtle tricks!’. So attend to the fol-

lowing five points of attention with extra care:

€ye contact
€ye contact shows your interest in your material and the audience to whom you are presenting it. When you

fail to link with your audience constantly the audience feels neglected and does not pay attention.

The energetic linkage exercises in chapter 2-8-8 help you to master different levels of linkage with individuals and audiences.

The exercises make you aware of your primary linkage (personal, neutral, withdrawn or ignoring) and offer you techniques to

master different levels of linkage.
Try to connect with different people during the lecture. Spread your connection over the audience. Connect with different

people in a friendly way, making the connection not too shy, too tong or too intense.

Posture.
Many lecturers in the dance world were originally dancers. Their posture is usually excellent. One observation might

be interesting. When women lecture on their own (without a partner) they often demonstrate the man’s steps using

a guest woman as their partner. There is nothing at all wrong with that choice. What could become disturbing is

MENTAL AND EMOTIONAL PREPARATION FOR A STRESS-FREE BODY

You may have to lecture in locations that are unknown to you. Yet it is important to feel free in each location.
Studies have shown that people perform better if they known the room well. There are several ways to get to know
new places quickly. Chapter 1-2' offers exercises to physically, mentally and emotionally help you to acknowledge

new locations.
For very important lectures it might be worth checking out the place before you lecture there or view a video

of others lecturing in that location in the past.

SUGGESTIONS FOR BODY PREPARATION BEFORE A LECTURE

The following exercises help you to connect with your body (the crucial instrument during your lecture).

The Warming up as explained in chapter 2-8-1 physically, mentally and emotionally helps you to get ready for your lecture.
Only Part One of the warming-up programme is needed when you lecture on your own.

Make contact with your body and Stretch yourself out.

Smile or grin for 90 seconds if you feel stressed before your lecture. You really need to relax but you probably feel that there
is nothing to smile or grin about. That is understandable but just do it. After 45 seconds your brain picks up this facial expres-
sion as a signal that you feel good. This interpretation of the brain starts the production of neurotransmitter {endorphins}
that give you a real feeling of welt being. So use the next 45 seconds of smiling or grinning to get enough of these endor-
phins.

Relaxing exercises (see chapter 2-8-5}.

EFFECTIVE OPENINGS

Start with an opening that is relevant for the audience and then give an overview of the structure and content
of your lecture. This informs the audience of what is to be expected and relaxes them to listen to you.
Lectures that start with an impressive opening statement are generally better understood, accepted and remem-

bered than neutral or defensive openings. Suggestions for openings that create impact are:

Start with a question
Questions tend to activate the audience since they are immediately a partner in the lecture. ’Are you bored sit-
ting still all day?’ might be an interesting opening when your audience have been listening passively to a few

lecturers before you. Especially when you intend to make them do exercises.

Start with a tantalizing statement
An unexpected opening statement draws attention. It makes the audience actively wonder where the statement
will lead to. For example:’Since we have all been trained on Cuba we all know enough about the origin of the

Rumba.’ or 'For dancing you do not need to work your muscles. Relaxation is all it takes.’

Use a metaphor
Metaphors are not commonly used as opening statements but they do activate the creative minds of your audi-
ence. 'l intend to dust off an old picture which has faded away a little over time. This picture shows two dancers

enjoying each other's company.’ might be an interesting metaphor when you lecture on partnering.

Unk your subject matter to ao personal experience

Using personal experiences - when well done and not to boast or make excuses - may make you vulnerable and
at the same time more accessible. For example "I know from a very reliable source how hard it is to express
yourself naturally in dance’. Such a statement only works if the audience knows you are referring to yourself of

course.

Please your audience
Often the pleasing opening statement feels like you are stating the obvious. But if you can voice it with believ-
ability - or humour - it can work. For instance: Bad Harzburg is the most beautiful town in the world. Thank

God so few people have heard of it.’
Start with an anecdote
Some people have a great sense of humour and attract the attention well by starting to telt a funny anecdote:

As you all know I was a lousy dancer but fifteen years ago I danced shows on cruise ships. For that short peri-

The art of finishing

YOUR FINAL CHANCE

Beginnings and endings are moments audiences easily remember. Most of the time you remember the beginning
and the end of a movie or a book. Not everything that happens in between. Endings are memorable moments
in life and also in dance lectures. It is your final moment where you can impress.

A four-year-old child finishes his story by just stopping. One year later the child says: That's all’ or "The End’.
Six and seven year old children do make an ending combined with an exclamation like 'Finished’. From the age
of eight, children realize that they need to bring a story to a favourable conclusion.

How well do you bring your lecture to a close? Is it an anticlimax or do you have the finishing touch? In this

section two sets of information are given to help you finish with even more impact:

INEFFECTIVE ENDINGS

Just stopping
Some lecturers just stop talking. Like a four-year-old child. This is the perfect anticlimax. Your content will not

be appreciated as coming from a real professional. "That was it folks.’ is a clear example of just stopping.

Hurrying
Some lecturers have too much to say and run out of time. They panic and speed up their speech to such an

extent that you can even notice a shortage of breath. Their audience can’t follow them any longer and are flab-

bergasted.

The never-ending story
The speaker can’t find an end to the story. He or she seems to stop but doesn’t. The speaker keeps on bringing in
new information, sidetracks and so on. The audience gets restless since they are aware of the inability to bring it

to a close. These lecturers often exceed the time they are given for their lecture and this makes things even worse.

Ending with a cliché
Closing with a cliché hardly ever impresses people. Examples are: ’My conclusion is that ballroom dancing has
